schedule_event.title: ONLINE - Entanglements of Environmental (In)Justices in the Sea of Islands
schedule_event.description:
The Sea of Islands (Hau'ofa 1994) refers to the Pacific region where people live on islands but are connected through their common ocean. Western imaginaries of this region often focus on large linear distances on topographic maps, conceptualizing the islands as remote and isolated. They further deny and devalue the people living there by exoticizing and naturalizing them, and by focusing on their small populations rather than their rich cultural histories of resilience. The notion of “Large Ocean States” instead of “Small Islands Developing States,” promoted by political actors in the region, presents a counterpoint to these imaginaries. In order to relate to pacific epistemologies, geographic research on and about the Sea of Islands must emphasize connectivity—between diverse peoples, between human and more-than-human entities, land, sea, and sky, across generations—and thus employ relational approaches and a rather holistic way of thinking (Smith 2021). For mapping, a more relational, topological approach has been proposed (Kuper et al. 2024), which aligns with these epistemological perspectives by emphasizing connections and continuities over linear distances.

Entangled with the misreading of the region—as part of colonial oppression—is the region’s exposure to a web of intertwined apocalyptic crises (Maurer 2024). Colonization and Christianization decimated populations through violence and imported diseases, while colonial exploitation transformed not just the socio-economic system but the very land —most notably through phosphate mining. Today, the deep-sea environments are threatened by intense resource extraction. The region's cultural neglect is contrasted by strategic militarization. Over 300 nuclear explosions were conducted from the 1940s to the 1990s in Māo’hi Nui/French Polynesia, the Marshall Islands, Kiribati, and Johnston Atoll/Kalama and have caused widespread contamination, environmental destruction, fisheries collapse, and forced resettlements. Meanwhile, climate change poses severe threats via rising sea levels, extreme weather events, and the loss of sustainable livelihoods.

These crises are rooted in colonial histories, with powerful states denying the rights of indigenous populations. Pacific peoples advocate for environmental justice through diverse means, including art, poetry, international rallies, and panels. Their demands encompass recognition, compensation, truthtelling, nuclear disarmament, and genuine independence.

Our session includes contributions  that integrate a focus on methodological approaches aligning with local epistemologies and realities with addressing environmental injustices, resistance, and resilience.

paper.title: Islands of Hope: Fiji’s Climate Justice Movements Between Local Perceptions and Global Discourses
How Rimajel (Marshallese) Acquire Jelalokjen (Place Based Knowledge)→A Qualitative Research Project on Marshallese Ways of Knowing and Learning

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Map Room Conversations (2): Foresting Landscapes
schedule_event.description: Maps establish conversations between spaces, bringing into dialogue locations, symbols, meaning, and people. The relation between mapping as practice and maps as objects has long been the subject of theoretical and methodological debate within and beyond the discipline of geography. In the sessions below we use the RGS-IBG map collection to continue this debate about maps and mapping through two types of conversation (which build on the first sessions of this type at the 2024 conference, see https://rgs-ibg.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/toc/10.1111/(ISSN)1475-4762.map-room-conv). The first is between two researchers who use maps from the collection to explore a shared interest from different perspectives. The second conversation will be between the researchers, who will present some of the maps they work with, and the participants in these interactive sessions. We invite you to take this opportunity for close encounters and conversations with the Society’s map collection.

paper.title: Organising the forest space in Colonial Bombay, India
Mapping “unoccupied areas” in colonial Assam

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Gender and Feminist Geographies Research Group (GFGRG) Walking Tour- Designed to be forgotten: women in South Kensington
schedule_event.description:
Designed to be forgotten: women in South Kensington

Join Iya London for a thought-provoking walking tour exploring the hidden contributions of Victorian women to the architecture and design of South Kensington.

Discover how they helped shape this renowned arts and science district—and why their stories remain largely untold.

You'll also explore the differing representations of men and women in the area’s public spaces. This walk will change the way you see South Kensington!

What You’ll See

We'll focus on the Victoria and Albert Museum, the Royal School of Mines, the Memorial to the 1851 Great Exhibition, the Royal Albert Hall and the Albert Memorial.

About Your Guide

Joanna Oyediran is a qualified Open City guide. She regularly leads walks round White City for Open City as part of their year-round tours programme. Her other clients have included the Royal College of Art, the LSE, the Great Exhibition Road Festival, and Planning Aid for London.

Joanna is the founder of Iya London, which unearths and shares London’s overlooked stories, past and present.

Meeting Point

Outside the main entrance of the RGS

Booking a place

This will be organised nearer the time



Timetabling note: Joanna is available for the slot on Thursday 3rd September- 11.10am-12.50pm

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Writing successfully for the Journal of Geography in Higher Education
schedule_event.description: This session is designed to encourage and enable new academics/postgraduates that have undertaken pedagogic research to get their work published and disseminated to the rest of the geography community. After discussing the mission of the Journal of Geography in Higher Education (JGHE), panelists will explain the submission, review, and publication processes of the JGHE. Topics will include the nature of material appropriate for submission, the types and level of evidence necessary to support findings, the recommended length of manuscripts, advice about writing for an international readership, and JGHE's citation index. In this practical workshop, prospective authors will interact with panelists through an interactive paper review session and to discuss issues specific to manuscripts they are planning or writing. Practical guidance and advice will be provided to potential authors. The session will end with a plenary around the demands of getting published. It should be noted that the proposed running order of the session needs to be flexible to accommodate the needs and level of response from prospective.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Working with remainders: traces and spectrality in justice-making processes (1): analytics
schedule_event.description:
Session co-convenors: Annaclaudia Martini (University of Bologna) and Martina Tazzioli (University of Bologna)

The contemporary cultural moment is characterized by a renewed interest towards evidence, and evidence-based epistemologies and methodologies. This concern has also permeated geographical debates, partly as a result of impact-driven analyses that declare the aim of recognising inequalities and fostering justice-making processes. This session challenges such a quest for evidence as justice seeking instrument and seeks to shift attention towards traces and spectrality as analytical lenses for re-thinking geographies and politics of justice.

This session interrogates how the analytical lens of traces, haunting and spectres can be effectively used in geography for articulating critical knowledge in the current cultural and geopolitical conjuncture. We aim to explore how traces and spectrality might be productively mobilised as analytics or as methods on different phenomena such as wars, migrations, natural disasters and ecological loss, heritage and memory, collective struggles and mobilizations, taking traces and spectres seriously in their heterogeneity and incompleteness. In focusing on hauntings (Derrida, 1994) and on traces (Ginzburg, 2012), we complicate historical narratives implicitly dependent on the linear temporalities of progress, and justice-seeking processes exclusively grounded on the production of evidence. Such perspective shift exposes individuals' and collectives' fragmented experiences of memory, justice, the impact of colonial legacies on our present, and intergenerational trauma.

We invite contributions that engage with traces and/or spectrality from a theoretical, methodological or empirical point of view, and that interrogate how these can be used for bringing into focus concealed and naturalised inequalities, and for envisioning justice-seeking processes in different fields. Such approach can expand and move beyond current epistemologies on traces (Didi-Huberman, 2017; McGeachan, 2018; Ogborn, 2005) and hauntology and spectrality (Derrida, 1994; Maddern & Adey, McCormack, 2010), interrogating how these not only allow us to enable novel understandings of past events and violations, but also unfold and activate research pathways for envisioning justice-making processes.


References

Derrida, J. (1994). Specters of Marx: The State of the Debt, the Work of Mourning and the New International. London: Routledge.

Didi-Huberman, G. (2017). Bark. MIT Press.

Ginzburg, C. (2012). Threads and traces: True false fictive. University of California Press.

Maddern, J. F., & Adey, P. (2008). Spectro-geographies. cultural geographies, 15(3), 291-295.

McCormack, D.P. (2010). Remotely sensing affective afterlives: The spectral geographies of material remains. Annals of the Association of American Geographers, 100(3), 640-654.

McGeachan, C. (2018). Historical geography II: Traces remain. Progress in Human Geography, 42(1), 134-147.

Ogborn, M. (2005). Atlantic geographies. Social & Cultural Geography, 6(3), 379-385

paper.title: Beyond evidentiary epistemologies: tracing the afterlives of violence

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Just Transitions in and Beyond China (3)
schedule_event.description:
The global climate and biodiversity crises cannot be addressed without ‘just transitions’ – profound and far-reaching social reconfigurations that place well-being and social justice at their core. While just transition narratives resonate with activists, scholars, and policy makers across geographies, the concept has gained significantly less traction in China. This represents a critical oversight. First, China is the largest emitter of greenhouse gases (IEA, 2023) and leading nation in renewable electricity capacity additions at the global level (IEA, 2025). China’s increasing influence – economically, politically and ecologically – places the nation in a position to significantly shape global transition trajectories. Second, ongoing and planned environmental reform within China is already producing social impacts (e.g., through the phase-out of fossil fuel industries (Gong and Lewis, 2024) and local, uneven effects of environmental conservation (Wang and Lo, 2022), only likely to deepen as transitions unfold. Third, Chinese flows of investment shape patterns of economic development and socio-spatial configurations in locations around the world (Apostolopoulou et al., 2024), with highly uneven and uncertain outcomes. Finally, theoretical frameworks on ‘justice’ (social, environmental, political, and beyond) may require re-conceptualisation in relation to China’s socio-cultural and political context. Mainstream understandings remain shaped by liberal-democratic traditions, which may constrain examinations of transitions in China and China-connected spaces (Wang and Lo, 2021).

Mapping and exploring how just transitions unfold within and beyond China is therefore an urgent research agenda. This panel engages with multiple dimensions of these dynamics, including but not limited to:

  • Social justice and socio-economic impacts of transition-related policies and reforms in China, including uneven outcomes of industrial restructuring, labour displacement, infrastructure investment, and urban-rural inequality. This includes studies on environmental justice dimensions of transitions, such as uneven impacts of environmental policies and programmes of ecological conservation.
  • Just transition outcomes of Chinese overseas investment, such as renewable energy projects, forestry/afforestation, and mining of critical minerals. We especially invite contributions that unpack the complex dynamics at play in global supply chains and the decoupling of impacts across locations of resource extraction and product deployment.
  • Chinese discourse of justice and just transition, including studies that explore contemporary policy narratives and public debates and their approaches to justice, as well as critiques of Anglophone conceptions of these debates. We also welcome work that engages wider and diverse forms of justice and just transition issues across China and China-connected spaces.

paper.title: Exploring the role of temporary urbanism in urban regeneration within the context of China’s urban transformation: the shift from a focus on quantity to a focus on quality
Rethinking neighborhood participation through everyday practice and participation infrastructure: evidence from Chengdu
Remaking Verticality: Repair, Temporality, and Urban-Rural Inequality in Kangding, Southwestern China

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - The Gendered Labours of Everyday Ecological Transformation (1): Governance and responsibility
schedule_event.description:
Across households, neighbourhoods, community projects and eco-homes, women and people identifying with feminised or non-cis-masculine roles disproportionately drive everyday ecological transformation. Cooking from scratch, zero-waste practices, community food projects, care work, and sustaining eco-homes or eco-communities all form part of the daily labour of ecological change. Yet these practices often reproduce entrenched gendered divisions of labour, assigning women responsibility for food, waste, childcare, emotional wellbeing, and household sustainability. A central question animates this session: Is ecological transformation dependent on gendered inequalities?

Feminist environmental thinkers have long shown that care is structurally devalued in capitalist and patriarchal systems (Tronto, 1993; Federici, 2019). Ecofeminists argue that ecological transformation demands confronting the patriarchal hierarchies that render feminised care invisible (Gaard, 1997; Shiva, 2000; Salleh et al., 2017). Gender and sustainability scholarship likewise highlights gendered labour divisions as central to socio-ecological change (Cohen, 2017). At the same time, development and political-economy research shows how women’s labour is often instrumentalised within neoliberal narratives of market expansion and ecological solutionism (Moeller, 2018). Everyday socio-ecological change is therefore deeply entangled with the politics of social reproduction.

In many grassroots sustainability initiatives, ecological aspirations materialise through the everyday reorganisation of domestic practices—community kitchens, shared gardens, composting, and collective food provisioning. These are sites where ‘matters of care’ (Puig de la Bellacasa, 2017) become ecological commitments enacted through often-shared daily labour. Research on co-housing and intentional communities shows how shared spaces are imagined to redistribute labour and collectivise sustainability efforts (Williams, 2005; Lietaert, 2010). Yet gendered responsibilities frequently re-emerge, even within settings explicitly committed to equality and ecological ethics (Eräranta et al., 2009; Jarvis, 2013; Vestbro & Horelli, 2012; Pickerill, 2015). Care is often collectivised without being politicised, reproducing hidden hierarchies of gender, labour and power (Bhatia, 2022; Gerner, 2025).

These tensions—between empowerment and burden, ecological commitment and gendered expectation—sit at the centre of this session. We seek to foreground the geographies of ambivalence that shape who performs ecological labour, how this labour becomes visible or invisible, and how spaces of sustainability can both challenge and reproduce inequality. We invite empirical, theoretical, and methodological contributions engaging with these tensions and the gendered labours of ecological transformation.

paper.title: Exploring Water Supply Dynamics and Women’s Strategic role in Water Management within Rural and Peri-Urban Kano, Nigeria
Between Responsibility and Empowerment: Gendered Labour and Ecological Discourses in Rural China
Citizen women and migrant domestic workers: Gender and class in Singapore’s household plastic waste management
Care as commitment to sustainable city life: A scalar, decolonial feminist reading of waste in Mexico City
Inclusive training and skills pathways for meaningful retrofit employment

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Interactions between residential location, housing, and mobility: the challenges of a cross-cutting theme (1)
schedule_event.description:
A wealth of literature has developed in Anglo-Saxon and European countries over the past 20 years on the interactions between residential location, housing, and mobility. It has highlighted the importance of a systemic approach that combines housing and transportation costs at the household level (Haas and Osland, 2013; Coulombel, 2018). Some studies have adopted a macroscopic approach to show the central role of the state of socio-technical systems, including a political economy of car dependency that enables space-consuming and energy-intensive land use patterns (Le Goff et al., 2025). Other more sociological approaches have focused on households’ overall residential strategies (Desvallées and Haine, 2025, Zanolini and Morency, 2025). Finally, most of this research has focused on the social issues raised by this double expense. They highlight the interconnection between vulnerabilities in housing and mobility, which overlap in households facing a combination of socioeconomic difficulties and spatial marginalisation (Mattioli et al., 2018; Simcock et al., 2021).

In addition to this social issue, which can be further explored through changes in lifestyles (e.g., teleworking, electric cars), the topic of household spending on housing and mobility is now giving rise to new avenues of research, broadening and enriching the questions being asked.

1) Firstly, from a methodological point of view, statistical data on housing and mobility are very often constructed in thematic silos, and the development of statistical tools to observe these expenditures is currently very limited. Some recent work has attempted to overcome this limitation, either by using databases that had not been exploited from this perspective until now (Makarewics et al., 2020), or by developing original collection methods (Nicolas et al., 2025).

2) Still on the issue of the cross-cutting nature of the topic, the treatment of mobility-housing interactions raises key questions about spatial planning and public policy choices in terms of anticipating and controlling urban sprawl. It therefore seems important to examine public policy measures in order to diagnose the persistence of the siloed treatment of these two issues, but also to report on the concerns of local authorities regarding the role of this double expenditure on regional planning.

3) Exploring the environmental dimension of these interactions broadens the discussion beyond social issues to include impacts in terms of emissions and shifts the focus to other audiences, potentially moving from low-income households to the residential footprint of wealthy households. It also questions the tools used to represent the interactions between transportation and urban planning in situations of strong urban dynamics.

4) Finally, there is a wide variety of fields that have been explored from this angle of interactions between residential location, housing, and mobility, with opportunities to cross-reference different spatial contexts. The idea is to offer regional perspectives on North and South America, Europe, Asia, Africa, and the Pacific region/

The session we are proposing will therefore be an opportunity to take stock of recent academic work on the theme, with the intention of launching a collective reflection in the social sciences, engineering sciences, environmental sciences, and health sciences.

paper.title: How does population ageing reshape suburban inequalities?

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - What does landscape mean in my research? Can it be captured in a photograph?
schedule_event.description:
In this session, we will convene a roundtable and open discussion about the use of photography in landscape geography research. As ideas of landscape are often considered or interpreted as visual elements, we ask how we as researchers use photography as a means of communicating the research itself, the subject matter, or as some other form of representation or methodological approach. We will also explore if the visual nature of representing our research – and how this changes – is something that we as landscape geographers need to strengthen in our toolkit?

Members of the Landscape Geography Working Group will convene this roundtable. discussion Dr Nicola Dempsey (University of Sheffield), Dr Meredith Whitten (University College London), Dr Daniel Bos (University of Chester) will be in conversation with Jamie Owen from RGS. 

Calling on examples from specific projects and practices, we will explore how researchers can consider aesthetics, ethics and the methodological underpinnings of photography in our research. We will also gain insight from Jamie who organises the Society’s international photography competition, Earth Photo.

The discussion will consider aspects and use of photography in research such as involving (or not) research participants or co-creators. This might be photo-elicitation based on our own photographs taken as a researcher or based on those of participants. It might be photography as a means of documentation, record-making or evaluation combined with activities such as walking, watching or monitoring e.g. seasonal change. How researchers consider the representation and symbolic dimensions of photography will be explored to consider how we acknowledge our practices and processes of image creation and production.

The session will take place in person. The session is linked to the Landscape Geography Working research group photography exhibition when the entries for the competition will be displayed, discussed as part of a wider roundtable discussion. The session will end with the announcement of the competition winners.

Some reading

Oldrup, H.H. and Carstensen, T.A. (2012) Producing geographical knowledge through visual methods, Geografiska Annaler: Series B, Human Geography, 94:3, 223-237, DOI: 10.1111/j.1468-0467.2012.00411.x

Rose, M., Heddon, D., Qualmann, C., Wilson, H., & Maggie, O. (2022) The Walkbook: Recipes for Walking and WellBeing. https://walkcreate.gla.ac.uk/the-walkbook/

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Burning Inequalities: Critical Geographies of Fire (1)
schedule_event.description:
Where and why fire impacts people and places is deeply intertwined with many facets of inequality and injustice. This session aims to contribute to discussions around the role of geographical perspectives in conceptualising and researching fire inequality.

The devastating fires of Grenfell Tower in 2017 and more recently at Wang Fuk Court in Hong Kong in 2025 serve as stark reminders that we must understand and redress inequalities that have shaped exposure to fire. Fires like Grenfell do not exist in a vacuum, instead they can expose and uncover inequalities and injustice embedded within our societies (Marmot, 2020). Scholars who have explored the contexts surrounding Grenfell in particular, have asserted the importance of foregrounding the role of racial logics of neoliberal urban governance (Danewid, 2020), geographies of injustice and inequality (MacLeod, 2018) and, institutional negligence and evasion of accountability (Özbilgin, Erbil and Valsecchi, 2026) in fomenting fire inequalities.

Furthermore, as global warming intensifies wildfires, the devastating consequences of such disasters continue to be disproportionately felt by underserved, minority and poor communities (Thomas, 2024). Research has highlighted that these impacts follow the contours of existing economic, social and racial inequalities as a result of insufficient resources, exclusion from risk preparedness (Baker et al., 2024) but also the lack of resources to rebuild after a wildfire (Davies et al., 2018). This is not by any means an exhaustive list, but speaks to the various factors that shape vulnerability to fire, and the injustices that follow a fire incident.

Geographical perspectives and geographical imaginaries can be harnessed to explore the spatiality of these overlapping and intersecting inequalities, and the mechanisms through which they emerge and are sustained. For example, how inequalities are produced and shaped at different scales and sites, from government decision making to geographies of the home. Critical perspectives on disasters have argued that they are not natural phenomena, therefore how can geographers contribute knowledge around the transformation of fire into a human disaster?

paper.title: Gendering fire justice

paper_authors.identity_string: Greater Manchester Fire and Rescue Service

Where and why fire impacts people and places is deeply intertwined with many facets of inequality and injustice. This session aims to contribute to discussions around the role of geographical perspectives in conceptualising and researching fire inequality.

The devastating fires of Grenfell Tower in 2017 and more recently at Wang Fuk Court in Hong Kong in 2025 serve as stark reminders that we must understand and redress inequalities that have shaped exposure to fire. Fires like Grenfell do not exist in a vacuum, instead they can expose and uncover inequalities and injustice embedded within our societies (Marmot, 2020). Scholars who have explored the contexts surrounding Grenfell in particular, have asserted the importance of foregrounding the role of racial logics of neoliberal urban governance (Danewid, 2020), geographies of injustice and inequality (MacLeod, 2018) and, institutional negligence and evasion of accountability (Özbilgin, Erbil and Valsecchi, 2026) in fomenting fire inequalities.

Furthermore, as global warming intensifies wildfires, the devastating consequences of such disasters continue to be disproportionately felt by underserved, minority and poor communities (Thomas, 2024). Research has highlighted that these impacts follow the contours of existing economic, social and racial inequalities as a result of insufficient resources, exclusion from risk preparedness (Baker et al., 2024) but also the lack of resources to rebuild after a wildfire (Davies et al., 2018). This is not by any means an exhaustive list, but speaks to the various factors that shape vulnerability to fire, and the injustices that follow a fire incident.

Geographical perspectives and geographical imaginaries can be harnessed to explore the spatiality of these overlapping and intersecting inequalities, and the mechanisms through which they emerge and are sustained. For example, how inequalities are produced and shaped at different scales and sites, from government decision making to geographies of the home. Critical perspectives on disasters have argued that they are not natural phenomena, therefore how can geographers contribute knowledge around the transformation of fire into a human disaster?

paper.title: Smoke and Mirrors: Precarity, risk and vulnerability to wildfires in Europe
Igniting Resilience: Co-Created Strategies for Wildfire-Adaptive Tourism Destinations
Displaced in Place: Temporal and Spatial Injustices after the 2021 Fire in North Evia
The emerging controversies of wildfire in Scotland and Yorkshire
Reconfigured Metabolism and the Politics of Wildfire Governance: Producing Risk and Vulnerability in Post-Soviet Armenia

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Climate Apartheid: meanings, contexts, politics, legitimacy (1)
schedule_event.description:
The lexicon of ‘justice’ is increasingly central to climate policy debates and interventions, framed through discourses such as climate, environmental or energy justice, and more broadly the ‘just transition’. These frameworks recognise that climate change disproportionately affects marginalised groups who bear little responsibility for emissions and lack influence in political negotiations (e.g. Schlosberg and Collins, 2014; Sultana, 2022). The scope of climate injustice has since expanded, encompassing not only the inequalities of climate change impacts, but also the ways in which climate mitigation and adaptation strategies also contribute to unequal processes and outcomes (e.g. Rice et al., 2022). This is increasingly referred to as climate apartheid. Initially deployed by activists as a provocative term to secure attention in global policy circuits (e.g. Bond 2016), the concepts of eco/climate apartheid are currently being developed by critical scholars as a framework to explain and critique how climate action by privileged individuals and groups (re)produces injustice (e.g. Tuana, 2019; Long et al., 2020; Rice et al., 2022; Long, 2024a, 2024b; Heron, 2024).

The roots of the term are largely traced to South African Archbishop Desmond Tutu, who in 2008 introduced the idea of “adaptation apartheid” to highlight his concerns that “adaptation is becoming a euphemism for social injustice on a global scale” in which wealthy countries protect themselves from climate change impacts via infrastructure technology investments unavailable to poorer counterparts (UN, 2008:166). A decade later, Philip Alston warned the UN Human Rights Council that “we risk a ‘climate apartheid’ scenario where the wealthy pay to escape overheating, hunger and conflict while the rest of the world is left to suffer” (UN, 2019). While initially the concept lacked coherence, recent scholarship has produced a comprehensive definition of climate apartheid as “an emerging system of discrimination, segregation, and violence based on various axes of oppression and privilege (race, class, gender, sexuality) that is produced by the material effects of climate change, but also many responses to the crisis” (Rice et al, 2001:627). This definition also introduces the analytical binaries of climate privileged and climate precarious, to capture how climate action affords protection and benefits to some individuals and groups, whilst generating heightened vulnerability and exclusion for others. Nonetheless, there remain a wide range of uses and interpretations in circulation, applied across disciplines and case study contexts.

Given the focus of the RGS/IBG2026 conference on exploring geographies of inequalities and just places, alongside the current wave of scholarship on climate apartheid, it seems an appropriate place and time to encourage further debate. As the concept and research field of climate apartheid is relatively new, we envisage this session as a platform for scholars whose research resonates with the concept of eco/climate apartheid and who are interested in engaging in debates related to the concepts’ meaning and development, politics and legitimacy, policy implications, as well as critical reflections on how climate apartheid functions ‘in practice’. Primarily we conceive this as a space to interrogate how the concept of climate apartheid is emerging conceptually and empirically.

paper.title: Climate Apartheid: The politics and legitimacy of reappropriating apartheid in climate discourse
Climate apartheid beyond the usual suspects: real estate development, water scarcity, and environmental privilege in Mexico
The Well Kept Garden: Climate Apartheid and the Production of Environmental Privilege, Los Angeles – Cape Town
A street view of Climate Apartheid: Insights from street-based work in Indian cities

The mind of climate apartheid

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Touching Visions: Touch, Embodiment and the Sensory for More Just and Caring Cities - Discussion and workshop
schedule_event.description:
Cities are encountered through bodies that sense, feel, and are touched by their environments, yet much of this everyday felt sensing is excluded from urban knowledge systems. In this session we seek to explore how the body “becomes a site of possibility” (Merleau-Ponty, 2002) in the caring city (Power & Williams, 2020). Puig de la Bellacasa (2017) invites us to think of the “webs of care through sensorial materiality, as chains of touch that link and remake worlds” (p.120). Drawing in the sensory encountering of the city we explore the often neglected senses of urban everyday life through the lens of care. In particular we draw on Puig de la Bellacasa’s ‘touching visions’, which refer to ways of knowing and imagining the world that are shaped by care, proximity, and response-ability, rather than by detached, distanced, or objective observations. They are ethico-onto-epistemic orientations that emphasise how knowledge is always situated, relational, and affectively “touched” by what and whom it engages with.

Yet bodies are not touched equally by the city. Sensory exposures, vulnerabilities, and capacities to care are unevenly distributed along lines of gender, race, class, disability, age, and caregiving relations. Approaching urban health through “touching visions” therefore opens up a way of thinking about inequalities as embodied, affective, and sensorial; enacted through everyday encounters with urban spaces.

Situated at the intersection of urban geography, feminist geographies of care, sensory geographies, and critical geographies of health, this session explores how touching visions might reframe understandings of urban health and care. We are particularly interested in how gendered, racialised, disabled, and caregiving bodies experience and navigate urban environments; how infrastructures of care shape access to health and wellbeing; and how more-than-human relations; with air, heat, water, plants, and urban ecologies; participate in chains of touch that sustain or undermine health and wellbeing.

Part 1 – Touching provocations (10 minutes each)

We invite scholars to prepare short, conceptually focused provocations that critically engage with how urban life is sensed, touched, and cared for (or not) through bodies, environments, and infrastructures. Presentations may be empirical, theoretical, or methodological, but should foreground how in/equalities are experienced through embodied and sensory relations of touch, proximity, and exposure. In keeping with the session’s emphasis on sensorial materiality, we welcome multimodal or sensory elements in presentations, including sound, images, maps, audio recordings, movement, video, or other media that can communicate how the urban is felt and lived.

Part 2 – Arts-based, sensory mapping workshop (touching visions in practice)

The second half of the session will be an interactive, arts-based workshop in which participants collaboratively map the speakers’ provocations through drawing, collage, or diagramming. Rather than treating each paper as separate, the workshop will explore how different in/equalities touch one another across bodies, spaces, and infrastructures, tracing resonances, tensions, and unexpected connections across speakers’ and session participants’ lived experiences. This collective mapping will enact touching visions as a method: a way of knowing that is situated, relational, and responsive.

paper.title: Haptic recall: Sensing familiarity in fieldwork
Cycling from Frame to Frame: Communicating Sensory Urban Experiences through Graphic Storytelling
Fostering Waste Care: Touch and Sensory Encounters in Community Composting Initiatives
Holding hands at the crossroads: child-parent touching interdependencies on the move
Situated Sensing: Crip Celebration and Caring Co-Creation for Urban Mobility

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Cities of hope: Urban citizenship and social solidarity in troubled times
schedule_event.description:
As cities around the world find themselves under threat from attacks on civil liberties, authoritarian violence and even complete destruction in the case of Rafah and Jabalya, while the impacts of global pandemics such as Covid-19 continue to be felt especially among the poorest and most vulnerable urban communities, how do everyday practices of humanity, dignity and solidarity help to forge a collective sense of hope for a better future?

From the streets of Minneapolis to Mumbai hope signals the motivation for ethical activity in moments of crisis and breakdown (Zigon, 2018). It has, thus, been understood as not only about looking forward to a better future but as Les Back (2021:5) writes, hope “is an attention to the present and the anticipation that something unexpected will happen and emerge from its ruins”. Hope is a set of dispositions, including the ensemble of knowledge, embodied inclinations, and affective investment that condition practices and are conditioned by them (Jansen, 2016). In operationalising hope as the collective transformation of the urban through new solidarities and imagined communities (Roy et al 2022), hope can be seen as both relational and institutional - drawing on individual emotions and aspirations as well as a collective (re)envisioning of the future alongside state-centred demands on the right to the city.

A geographical re-imagining of hope could focus attention on “those moments when, regardless of status or substance, subjects constitute themselves as citizens” (Isin & Nielsen, 2008: 18). These reflections on hope and citizenship assume poignant intellectual and practical significance in the context of the troubled times in which cities and their residents find themselves.

paper.title: Exiting authoritarianism for London's surplus of hope: Trajectories of out-migrants from Turkish metropoles to London
City without Civility:  Being Outcastes in Ahmedabad
Migration, Hope, and Resilience: Migrant Workers in Kochi, Kerala
Making Time: Enchronic Practices and Urban Belonging in Athens

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Reparative Geographies of Education: Children’s politics, visions and future-making
schedule_event.description:
Reparative approaches call on us to surface the interconnections between the past, present and futures in both the formation of educational injustice and its repair. They recognise how the ‘afterlives’ of historical injustice shape interests and action within the spatial infrastructures of education systems today. Structures of deprivation and advantage can be inherited as history traces across generations as well as sediments in place. However, injustice does not have to be predetermined, totalising or inevitable: redress and repair can offer new possibilities towards the building of more hopeful futures.

This session makes a case for reparative geographies of education and explores how we might harness its future-focused and action-orientated commitments. We do so through a collection of papers based on our work on the Reparative Futures of Education (Repair-Ed) project, a five-year research project based at the University of Oxford. The project involves place-based research with 10 schools and their surrounding communities in the city of Bristol (UK), examining past and present racial and classed injustices in experiences of primary schooling. It explores what reparative action for these injustices might look like, aiming to foster cross-city dialogue towards the collective recognition and responsibility for redress.

The session draws together the theoretical and methodological dimensions of our work on Repair-Ed, and introduces our development of a spatial-reparative praxis that is experimental and responsive to the lived histories and materialities of schooling in Bristol. Our praxis calls for responsiveness to how structural injustice is sedimented in place and carried in the body, and how repair necessitates opening creative spaces of participation, dialogue and exchange. Through a multi-scalar approach, our session brings together critical reflections on the possibilities and challenges of building a reparative praxis across different threads of our work on the project.

In recognition that children’s voices are central to reimagining reparative futures of education, we explore what a spatial-reparative praxis might look like in the context of our participatory research with primary school children. This area of our research addresses the question: how do children make sense of past and present injustices in schooling and how do they conceptualise educational justice for future children? The session will introduce reparative approaches to working with children as researchers and co-creators of knowledge through a range of approaches, from Youth Participatory Action Research to photo-walk methods. It will invite discussion on how children make sense of the spatiality of injustice in their schools, local communities and the wider city. It will also open reflection on their ideas and actions towards reimaging school spaces, educational infrastructures and their relational connections to their locals and the wider city.

paper.title: Towards just educational futures: Experimenting with a spatial-reparative praxis
How do children imagine just futures of education? Creating reparative futures of education through Youth Participatory Action Research
Reciprocal Encounters: Photo-Walking and Reparative Visual Pedagogies
The Futures We Inherit: Children, Space, and the Reparative Politics of Education

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Urban inequalities and transformative interventions (2)
schedule_event.description: Cities are increasingly paradoxical spaces, concurrently representing vast wealth concentration and deeply embedded socio‑economic and spatial disparities. Set within a context of intensifying global crises – from climate change to financial precarity – traditional geographical perspectives are being encouraged to extend diagnosis to critically examine transformative interventions that can reshape urban policy and practice. This session brings together papers that not only identify urban injustices but also critically examine effective solutions to those injustices, thereby challenging the status quo. The core challenge addressed across the papers is how interventions translate from academic critique into tangible political or planning initiatives, thereby ‘shaping agendas’. Many of the papers draw innovative methodologies (for example, comparative longitudinal analysis, participatory action research, and advanced spatial modelling) to critically assess the effectiveness and scalability of these interventions. Ultimately, these papers collectively address the question: How can geographical research on inequality, through rigorous and forward‑thinking analysis, serve as a force for radical urban change, shaping legislative agendas and governance toward a more just urban future?

paper.title: Grounding the flow: transformative place making with nature-based solutions along the River Dodder, Dublin, Ireland

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - ‘On mission?’: Understanding the geographies of clean energy growth and development (1)
schedule_event.description:
Building on a long period of cross-party political consensus in the UK, which included establishing its world leading legally binding net zero 2050 target, in 2024 Prime Minister Starmer announced that ‘Making Britain a Clean Energy Superpower’ was one of the six milestones for his new mission-led government (UK Govt 2024). Alongside delivering energy security, reducing consumer bills and reaching net zero, the Clean Energy Superpower Mission promised to drive “investment, good jobs and growth across the country” (UK Govt 2024a). 

Like the preceding Conservative Government’s Clean Growth (2017) and Powering Up Britain (2023) strategies, clean energy is seen as offering not only significant national economic returns but also the promise of more regionally balanced growth (Curran et al 2022). Such rebalancing, ‘levelling-up’, or ‘realising potential’ will, by its very nature, require “leveraging the clean energy transition to turbocharge growth” (UK Govt 2025b) across poorer and more peripheral areas of the UK. In particular, industrial heartlands and coastal communities have been identified as areas with “enormous opportunity to build the energy industries of the future and create the good jobs at good wages that people have long demanded” (UK Govt 2025b p.4). Consequently, clean energy has become central to the visions and economic development policy lexicon of many peripheral and old industrial regions.

This session uses the bold ambitions of the UK’s mission-led approach, as a point of departure, to reflect on what progress we have made, and what challenges still exist, in understanding the geographies of clean energy growth and their contributions to regional development. Considerable conceptual and empirical contributions have been made to this agenda in recent years from work within and across inter alia energy geographies (e.g. Bridge and Gailing 2019); EEG and Green Path Development (e.g. Dawley et al 2025); Green Innovation and Technological Diversification (e.g. Moreno and Ocampo-Corrales 2022); Geographies of Sustainability Transitions (e.g. Binz et al 2025); Regional Innovation Systems (e.g. Jakobsen et al 2022) and Global Production Networks (e.g. Coe and Gibson 2023). However, how do these promising contributions help us understand, or even inform, the geographical opportunities and challenges facing supra-national (e.g. EU); national; and local and regional clean energy growth visions, policies and strategies (e.g. Coenen et al 2021; Trippl 2025). Equally, as the political consensus surrounding Clean Power and Net Zero comes under greater challenge internationally, including within the UK itself, we also look for insights into how we can understand the implications for localities and regions facing increased uncertainty and risk around clean energy investment from both state and industry.

Themes could include, but are not limited to, international perspectives and policy insights related to:

  • What forms of local and regional development are emerging around clean power ? How are these being manifested in local and regional visions and futures?
  • To what extent does the growth of clean power disrupt or reinforce existing forms of uneven development?
  • How effective are existing and emerging forms of governance in delivering the development of clean power across, and within, regions and localities?

paper.title: Powering regional paths of development? 25 Years of Offshore Wind in the UK
The regional promise of UK solar: Imaginaries, fixes, and contradictions of green accumulation regimes
Reframing UK energy policy towards wellbeing within planetary boundaries

paper_authors.identity_string: Science Policy Research Unit, University of Sussex

paper.title: Regional implications of private network ownership and power the UK’s clean energy transition

paper_authors.identity_string: UCL Institute for Innovation and Public Purpose

paper.title: Policy Mixes and State Capacities in Regional Path Development: Low Carbon and Renewable Energy in Northern England

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Blue anxiety? Rethinking the promise of oceanic blue spaces
schedule_event.description:
The ocean has long been cast as cure. Its rhythms, immersions, and vertiginous horizons populate an expanding literature on therapeutic blue spaces with salutogenic promises – restorative gifts for the stressed, the depleted, the land-bound. But what happens when the cure is also the catastrophe? When the same water that heals also takes livelihoods, erodes sacred sites, deposits plastic on shores that cannot refuse it, and renders futures grammatically subjunctive? This session begins precisely where the therapeutic turn falters, insisting that “blue anxiety” – that particular dread born not merely of environmental hazard, but of the sea’s structural contradictions – is not the exception in the story of coastal wellbeing. It is, perhaps, its most honest chapter.

The session maps this contradictory human condition across empirical, creative, and conceptual contributions spanning multiple geographies – from tropical small-island communities navigating ecological collapse to urban waterfronts caught between gentrification and climate risk; from island coastlines bearing the disproportionate moral weight of marine pollution to low-lying communities where climate denial and climate anxiety inhabit the same anxious body. Contributions engage with who gets to access the blue in the first place – asking how disability, race, class, and geography structure not just proximity to water but the very possibility of its restorative encounter. Together, they show that blue anxiety is not simply hazard-induced. It is produced: by collapsing ecosystems, by normative governance assumptions, by the slow violence of environmental injustice, and by the stubborn mismatch between the promise of coastal space and the unequal conditions of its inhabitation.

Together, these contributions – empirical, creative, poetic, and methodologically restless – provincialise the therapeutic: they return a universal promise to its specific geographies of production, and in doing so, reveal it to have always been unevenly distributed. They think with disability as epistemic expertise, with ecological grief as political diagnostic, with creative and poetic forms of knowing as scholarly method in their own right. They ask not merely “what does the ocean do to us?” but “for whom, under what conditions, and at whose expense?” The session neither retreats to lament nor dissolves into critique without care. It insists on the dialectic: that wellbeing and anxiety, belonging and alienation, restoration and rupture are co-produced within dynamic water/land-scapes – and that holding this co-production in tension is the necessary precondition for any honest coastal justice. Our land-bound bodies breathe forever precarious on this blue planet. The theory, this session argues, must finally match the tide.

paper.title: The Sea That Heals and Kills: Wellbeing, Precariousness, and the Paradox of Tropical Blue Spaces
Living with the sea: urban blue spaces, wellbeing and inequality
Citizens of a Changing Coast: Wellbeing at the Frontlines of Marine Plastic Pollution
Unsettling the Therapeutic Coast: Disability, Risk and the Making of Blue Anxiety

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Geographies of Justice: mapping legal, carceral and civil societal notions of an elusive concept
schedule_event.description:
Justice is inextricably linked to all our lives. It is an undercurrent that affects our experiences in myriad of ways from personal interactions to our understanding of legal systems as well as global politics. At the same time today's interconnected world is increasingly affected by multiple crises: an interplay of intertwined and mutually reinforcing challenges – including climate change, economic uncertainty, war, displacement and the erosion of democratic institutions. This is accompanied by a changing understanding of justice and a close interconnection and centralisation of these crises in the field of justice. At the heart of these matters lies a philosophical question of how justice is interpreted, including whether justice is a universal human condition or instead a more localised form of solidarity (Jeffrey 2013). In relation to a moral geography on how we ought to or want to live, what constraints we experience and how these are negotiated (Smith 1998, 2000; Proctor 1998), justice sits alongside other conceptual ideas like trust and confidence.

One such area in which questions of justice converge is the legal domain (Blomley 1994; Delaney 2010): in court many people are confronted with justice in particularly personal but also wider institutional ways in the sense that they are seeking justice, delivering justice or find themselves at the receiving end (Schliehe & Jeffrey 2023). For the ones who are convicted and find themselves in the justice system, be it in prison or on probation often see themselves confronted not only with their own emotional geography of justice but with a changing sense of trust in the justice system, questioning the legitimacy of institutions and one’s own sense of trust in the world at large. It is at this intersection of legal and carceral geographies, that justice often takes a sharper form (Schliehe & Jeffrey 2023). These questions in turn also affect society at large and our sense of (mis-)trust in the state or level of confidence in its institutions.

This session is addressing questions and concerns in fields ranging from geographies of moral philosophy and its bearing on our understandings of justice to emotional-geographical and legal-geographical lenses and mechanisms used to address injustice and resolve conflict.

paper.title: On spatial justice: loitering under Public Space Protection Orders
Enduring collectively: Spatialities of transformative justice in German (post-)autonomous movement contexts
Crafting Justice: How Girls Persist to Resist Violence through Community Zine-making
Right to be included in the community: intersections of disability rights and geographies of justice

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Contesting Housing Injustice: Mobilising Materials of Concern (1)
schedule_event.description:
Mould has recently become a potent symbol of domestic inequality in postwelfare states (Kane, 2023; Shurety, 2025; Storer et al., 2025). Similar to other uncharismatic intrusions, mould reveals a material dimension to longstanding geographical concerns for the domestic as a site of struggle, permeated by, and very much a part of, discriminatory ideologies and sociopolitical forces (Blunt and Dowling, 2006; Brickell, 2012; Massey, 1994; Sibley, 1995; Speer 2017). But mould is not just a site of despair, it is also political – this substance has simultaneously been mobilised by activists to articulate reform to discriminatory and neglectful modes of housing provisioning (NEF, 2024). Within the still unfolding mould epidemic, materiality bleeds into metaphor –mould renders visible structural and historical processes of marginalisation lost in technocratic and austere planning regimes (Simpson et al., 2025).

Mouldy struggles not only reveal any bounded notion of home to be a bourgeois fantasy (Kaika, 2004), but politicise the unequal risks and labours sprung from disrupted socio-natural boundaries over which tenants have little control. Whilst Kaika writes of water, we might add concerns relating to a multiplicity of transgressive organic and chemical matter – mould, pests, pollutants – all which enter, grow with, leak from the lining of, and cohabitate our homes at markedly uneven rates. Indeed, precursors to the politicisation of mould can be found in struggles for housing justice which centre cladding (Smith, 2023), lead (Fennel, 2015), dust (Noterman, 2023), and pollution (Schneller et al., 2022). These non-human and uncharismatic intrusions reshape inhabitation, carrying the potential to destabilise security and even to unmake the home under regimes of precarity (Baxter and Brickell, 2014).

A growing chorus of empirical studies draws our attention to racialised-classed logics which shape the distribution of matter out of place (Douglas, 1966). This panel is interested in how people live with, and contest uncharismatic, toxic, or ‘feral’ non-humans and materials. It explores the interface between lived experience and the work of contesting potentially harmful materials – in the practical and evidentiary practices through which matter becomes concerning within and beyond domestic space.

We are interested in the processes through which people, communities and activists ‘scale’ with uncanny domestic intrusions to confront grinding forces of in-situ dispossession. We likewise welcome coalitional scholarship which traces political contestations which form around materials – both in early struggles for recognition where harm is often contested and contentious (Murphy, 2006), and where later, following the “designation of the unseeable” (ibid: 9), matter comes to be used to make claims against hostile and dismissive states, landowners, and institutions for fairer and juster habitation. We hope to draw together scholars engaged in activist work, or studying the quieter work of persevering in conditions of housing distress amidst biomedical, legal and regulatory structures which limit claims and compensation.

paper.title: Mobilising Mould in the UK Hostile Environment
Resisting mould in the face of dispossession: Experiences of poor conditions on regenerating housing estates in London
Contesting Mould and the Politics of Responsibility
Lifestyle Takes Place: Dwelling, Mould and the Moralisation of Everyday Life in Social Housing
Fifty Years of Campaigning Against Dampness

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Digital participation: a route to reducing inequalities?
schedule_event.description:
This panel critically explores the extent to which digital participation can serve as a route to reducing inequalities in place-making and knowledge production.

The UK’s recently published Industrial Strategy Plans (DTI, 2026) emphasise that digital technology and ‘createch’ approaches will be at the forefront of research and investment in coming decades. At the same time, UKRI reflect the ‘participatory turn’ across their funding schemes, emphasising the importance of public involvement through co-production and citizen science. Digital tools are increasingly used to inform policy, planning, and research, yet the challenge remains of how to do digital participation without recreating existing inequalities (British Academy, 2022; Fung, 2025; Maming et al, 2025; Robinson et al, 2015).

It is well established that participatory methods allow people to represent their own realities rather than being spoken for (for example Aczel et al 2023; Halstead, 2025; Peimani and Kalamipour, 2025) and the hope as been that digital iterations of these approaches can capture lived, experiential, and place-based knowledge  (Maming et al, 2025; Stempeck, 2025). Yet our experiences as researchers using digital participatory methods suggests that the affordances of digital participation are finely balanced against its limitations.

We welcome short papers, creative/interactive presentations exploring these issues. We offer this panel as an opportunity for researchers, practitioners and research participants to reflect on (amongst other things):

  • Ways that digitised participatory approaches are shaped by and can counteract intersecting inequalities
  • Emerging, unexpected or hard-to-handle insights from digital participatory projects
  • The potential for digital participation to foreground “other” kinds of data about places and how this can be mobilised to create just places

Contact k.smith7@hull.ac.uk or sarah.gambell@glasgow.ac.uk for submissions.

References

Aczel, M., Makuch, K.E.(2023) Climate change, young people, and the IPCC: The role of citizen science. Elementa: Science of the Anthropocene; 11 (1): 00029. doi: https://doi.org/10.1525/elementa.2022.00029

British Academy (2022), Understanding digital poverty and inequality in the UK, http://doi.org/10.5871/digital-society/understanding-digital-poverty

Fung, A. (2015), Putting the Public Back into Governance: The Challenges of Citizen Participation and Its Future. Public Administration Review, 75: 513-522. https://doi.org/10.1111/puar.12361

Halstead, F., Parsons, K. J., Vo, T., Lloyd Williams, A., Jones, L., Le, H., Nguyen, A., Hackney, C., Parsons, D. (2025). Place-based and intergenerational storytelling for youth climate action: moving beyond participation and towards momentum. Children’s Geographies, 1–8. https://doi.org/10.1080/14733285.2025.2519027

Maming, J. B., Escarilla, C., Taño-an, E. L., & Fare, N. (2025). Technology-enabled public participation: opportunities and challenges of participatory research. Psychology and Education: A Multidisciplinary Journal, 39(6), 723–731. https://doi.org/10.70838/pemj.390602

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Knowing the City: A Dialogues in Urban Research Panel on the Epistemological Turn in Urban Geography
schedule_event.description:
Over the past two decades, urban geography has been confronting a range of epistemological questions. Scholars have routinely questioned core concepts, critiqued where and by whom knowledge is produced, debated the kinds of knowledge that should be generated, and argued over how best to reconcile diverse approaches within the sub-discipline. In response, several recent efforts have sought to reconstruct urban geography’s approach to knowledge production (e.g. conjuncturalism, fragmentation, singularity). These debates have unfolded alongside broader societal shifts that have undermined trust in academic knowledge and devalued the idea of truth itself. The stakes of current epistemological work in urban geography are therefore high. This panel brings together scholars to reflect on the epistemological orientations shaping urban geography today.

Questions to be discussed include:

  • Do our epistemological concerns make the present moment exceptional?
  • How should urban geographers respond to the so-called “post-truth” moment?
  • Are there ways to bring pluralistic epistemological projects into dialogue—and is this necessary or desirable?
  • What have urban geographers turned away from, and how should we think about intellectual inheritance and legacy at this moment?
  • How should urban geographers navigate a world in which evidence and affect are both central to claim-making?
  • Is some degree of epistemological alignment necessary for a sub-discipline to function?

The session invites panelists to reflect on their own navigation of the epistemological turn and to consider how their work can contribute to the collective project of producing geographical knowledge about “the urban.”

paper.title: Knowing in fragments
How to know the digital city?
The epistemic turn in urban studies
The New Urban Aesthetic: the experiential turn and power in the digitally-mediated city
Who knows the city?

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Elemental thinking: Shaping worlds, shaping lives
schedule_event.description:
Elemental thinking: shaping worlds, shaping lives responds to a growing appetite among scholars, students, professionals, and practitioners fascinated by the interplay between the elements and our lives. That appetite is underpinned by people’s desire to care for Earth, for others, and for themselves in ways that account for the planet’s dynamism, power, and agency. This, then, will be a wide-ranging panel discussion for those who want to explore how we live, think, feel, act and are shaped by elemental forces. Such forces include but are not limited to earth, air, fire, water, metal, wood, ice, or void. Among other topics, panellists will consider the idea that elements actively co-constitute bodies, subjectivities, relations, settings, and environments. The session should appeal to those interested in examining this idea in diverse and generative ways that extend our understanding of being in the world.

The core argument to be explored is that elements are not passive entities that support human life from the outside; they are dynamic participants in shaping what lives are possible and how those lives unfold in worlding processes. By attending to how elements form, constrain, and enable bodily, emotional, spiritual, cultural, political and other experiences, the discussion will offer a framework for understanding diverse and divergent worlds and selves. In doing so, it will invite participants to rethink the elements as central to questions of identity, power, knowledge, survival, and our capacities to flourish. 

Importantly, the panel will also be an opportunity to launch the Palgrave Macmillan Series in Elemental Thinking. Members of the core editorial team will be on hand to listen to and learn from prospective authors and editors of collections about your ideas for possible book proposals. We warmly encourage seasoned and nascent elemental thinkers to join us for this event.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Democratising the Economy (1)
schedule_event.description:
People across the world are rethinking and reorganising their local economies towards more democratic, equitable, and sustainable forms in response to long-term (and uneven) economic and socio-spatial inequalities and multiple crises: from the global financial crisis and austerity policies decimating cultural provision and public capacity, the COVID-19 pandemic’s exposure of complex geographies of inequality and structural issues of health and social care under capitalism, and the climate emergency which too regularly affirms a crisis of essential infrastructures created by decades of underinvestment.

Theoretically, a democratisation of economic relations is, of course, closely related to the concept of economic democracy (Cumbers et al 2020), but the vision of a democratic economy also has many ‘siblings’: diverse economies (Gibson-Graham 2008), approaches including the foundational, social and solidarity, and wellbeing economies, and tools such as circular and doughnut economics (Raworth 2019). In practice, we see efforts to democratise economic activity particularly at the local and regional level such as a return of public, collective, and diverse ownership forms through de-privatisation of (public) services, infrastructures, and assets across a wide range of sectors (Kishimoto et al 2020, Cumbers and Paul 2022, Bianchi and Russell 2026). Equally, the global cooperative movement’s long history of mutual economic development and alternative practice, and more recently, experimentation with post-growth and community wealth building is taking hold in cities and communities across the world (Guinan and O’Neill 2020, Savini 2023, Crisp et al 2024).

This session seeks to gather theories, approaches, models, and case studies of alternative economic development and organisation that ‘democratise the economy’ to examine them as more than the sum of their parts. Deliberately inclusive, the session invites theoretical and/ or empirical contributions, including but not limited to public, collective, and community ownership, democratic governance, municipalism, (re)municipalisation and de-privatisation, cooperativism, questions of growth, and alternative imaginaries.

paper.title: Democratising the economy through cooperatives? Promises and realities in the cooperative agri-food sector
Building solidarity economies: tracing the historical geographies of UK credit unions
Inclusion and Economic Development: Towards a Place-Based Approach

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Making Sense of Unstable Ground: Living On with Disaster (1)
schedule_event.description:
Environmental disasters are often approached as moments of rupture requiring technical intervention, governance, or recovery. Yet for those who live with ongoing environmental instability, disaster is also a condition of everyday life, one that must be interpreted, endured, and rendered meaningful in order for life to continue. This panel invites papers that examine how people make sense of unstable ground and persist in living with it, attending to the ethical, temporal, spiritual, pragmatic, political, and moral forms of reasoning through which environmental volatility come to be lived with, remains tolerable, or becomes inhabitable.

We are particularly interested in empirically grounded work that explores how expectations of loss, recurrence, return, or repair shape everyday conduct in landscapes affected by flooding, landslides, erosion, drought, or seismic uncertainty. How are futures imagined, and obligations sustained amid prolonged instability? How do religious or sacred understandings, moral responsibilities, temporal orientations, and situated knowledges inform decisions about staying, moving, rebuilding, or enduring? What forms of reasoning emerge in contexts where disaster is neither exceptional nor resolved?

The panel especially welcomes contributions from settings that sit at the physical, political, or epistemic margins of dominant disaster narratives, including postcolonial and Global South contexts. We invite papers that foreground lived reasoning and situated knowledge, and the practices through which people continue inhabiting environments marked by ongoing instability, including the thresholds, tensions, and decisions through which staying, adaptation, or departure are weighed and enacted, and that invite reflection on how Geography has approached disaster. Contributions may engage critically with but are not limited to themes such as:

  • Moral and ethical reasoning in disaster-affected lives
  • Religious, sacred, or cosmological interpretations of environmental instability
  • Temporalities of waiting, anticipation, recurrence, and endurance
  • Living with infrastructure, protection, or neglect
  • Practices of staying, return, repair, and inhabitation under conditions of ongoing risk, including the thresholds and tensions through which leaving or relocation may be considered
  • Disaster as a lived condition rather than a discrete event
  • Social contracts and imagined obligations in at-risk contexts, including how expectations, responsibilities, and claims are negotiated between residents, states, infrastructures, and other forms of authority

paper.title: Inhabiting Unstable Ground: Reasoning with Disaster in Peru and the Philippines
All That Begins in Water Ends in Water: Mourning on the Brahmaputra Chars
Living with Volcanic Risk: Religious Responses to Environmental Uncertainty on Mount Etna

paper_authors.identity_string: University of Siegen/ University of Bern

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Clearing roadblocks: a workshop for interdisciplinary collaboration in transport geography
schedule_event.description:
This workshop is primarily designed for early careers researchers and postgraduate students.

At the TGRG, we recognise that transport geography is one of the most interdisciplinary strands of research. Our committee is composed of engineers, qualitative researchers, social theorists, planners and data scientists, matching the breadth of the sector itself. However, we recognise a degree of stigma exists across this ‘broad church’, caricatured as qualitative researchers scorning what they see as pure positivism, or quantitative researchers deriding what they see as work with insufficiently big data. The truth, as always, is a shade of grey, a missed opportunity, an inequality that stands to be corrected.

Please join us for this TGRG sponsored workshop, where we will aim to bring together researchers across the discipline, encouraging us to see the value in our different methodological and theoretical approaches, and hopefully working to make transportation research more diverse and more accessible.

After all, if one sub-discipline is going to be talking about research accessibility - surely it should be the transport geographers.

Feel free to bring your lunch along to this session.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Geographies of Care and Violence (1)
schedule_event.description:
Feminist, political, and social geographies have richly examined care as an ethical relation, a form of labour, an affective practice, and a political project (Bartos 2018; Bondi 2008; Gutiérrez Sánchez 2022; McDowell 2004; Power and Hall 2018; Raghuram et al. 2009; Raghuram 2016), fundamental to the ways lives, bodies, and worlds are sustained (Fisher and Tronto 1990). At the same time, geographers have extensively documented violence in its many forms—intimate, structural, slow, and spectacular (Christian and Dowler 2019; Pain and Cahill 2022). The session seeks to examine the relations between care and violence.

This session examines care and violence not as opposites, but as entangled and co-constitutive processes. The session is particularly interested in work that explores how violence is sustained, extended, or transformed through the absence, withdrawal, misrecognition, or uneven distribution of care, and how care may be complicit in or resistant to violence.

Feminist geographies of care have argued that the relationship between caregiver and care receiver is not a simple or linear practice, unsettling assumptions that care is inherently benign, highlighting its ambivalences, exclusions, and conflicts, and calling for greater attention to the ways care and conflict are not mutually exclusive (Bartos 2018; Power 2019; Raghuram et al. 2009).

Building on these debates, this session foregrounds questions of carelessness, neglect, abandonment, and misaligned care, asking how harm persists not only through acts of violence but also through everyday relations, institutional practices, and political–economic arrangements that deny or foreclose care. By bringing care and violence into conversation, this session seeks to open space to explore how care might be reimagined as a political and geographical practice capable of interrupting violence.

paper.title: Geographies of care and violence: reflecting on research with men who are survivors of sexual violence

paper_authors.identity_string: Department of human geography and spatial planning, Utrecht University

Trauma-informed and trauma-responsive practices in research on violence and care: Reflections on collective learnings and embodied practices from a collaborative workshop
Violent Homes and the Uneven Geographies of Care Infrastructures
Housing (“Problem”) Families: An History of Care and Coercion

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - From Camps to Island: The Politics of Protection for Rohingya Genocide Survivors
schedule_event.description:
Bhasan Char has become one of the most consequential and contested spaces in contemporary refugee governance. It is also inseparable from the fact that the Rohingyas are genocide survivors, subjected to systematic violence, persecution, serious crimes against humanity and forced displacement from Myanmar. Many first sought refuge in Bangladesh and were concentrated in the Cox’s Bazar camps. More recently, a substantial number have been relocated to Bhasan Char, a newly developed island settlement in the Meghna estuary. This trajectory, from genocidal expulsion to camp settlements and then to island relocation, provides a critical lens for the 2026 RGS conference theme, Geographies of inequalities: towards just places, because it foregrounds how extreme violence and displacement are translated into spatial arrangements that can reproduce or reconfigure injustice.

This session examines Bhasan Char as a managed geography of inequality in which material improvements in shelter and basic services can coexist with serious human rights concerns, including restricted freedom of movement, limited livelihood opportunities, uneven access to education and healthcare, protection risks, and the social harms associated with separation from kinship networks and familiar community infrastructures. The panel offers an empirically grounded, conceptually rigorous discussion of how inequality is produced through governance practices, infrastructural design, and the politics of evidence in a context shaped by genocide and its aftermath.

The contributions develop three integrated lines of inquiry. First, the session foregrounds lived experiences and the gap between official assurances and everyday realities, drawing on field-based interviews and narratives that document how refugees assess safety, housing, rations, healthcare, education, cultural and religious life, and the dignity of everyday practice. These accounts illuminate how inequalities become embedded within apparently administrative decisions about services, permissions, and everyday regulation, often with uneven effects across age, gender, and household position.

Second, the panel examines livelihood, capability, and agency under constraint. Using the capability approach and related frameworks, contributions explore informal economic activities and coping strategies that create modest autonomy, skill development, and dignity, while remaining shaped by market isolation, regulatory uncertainty, and mobility restriction. This strand directly addresses a central concern for “just places”: the conditions under which resilience becomes a genuine expansion of freedom and opportunity rather than a rhetorical substitute for enforceable rights.

Third, contributions analyse communicative ecologies, showing how mobile phones and social media can function as coping infrastructures while also reflecting inequalities in safety, affordability, and access. Environmental and geomorphological perspectives assess settlement sustainability and risk transfer within a dynamic delta, including cyclone and storm-surge exposure, erosion and accretion processes, and climate variability. A further contribution draws on micro-narratives of gendered violence and vulnerability, conceptualising harm as a continuum from genocide through displacement to camp and relocation settings and highlighting how structural and cultural violence may persist even where physical security appears improved.

The session concludes by proposing a practical agenda towards just places for genocide-displaced populations: rights-centred mobility arrangements, accountable risk governance, ethically designed livelihood pathways, and evidence practices that amplify refugee voices while minimising harm.
