schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - The overlooked spatial politics of the energy transition (1)
schedule_event.description:
In the grand task of overhauling energy infrastructures to shift towards fossil fuel-free sources and reduce energy consumption, proponents such as municipalities and other public actors have generally foregrounded technology, policy, and cooperation. In this process, other – and perhaps less solutionist – dynamics have largely been overlooked, deemed minor, or have operated under the radar. This session invites contributions on dynamics in the energy transition that complement dominant dialogues.
The session aims to (1) provide multi-scalar readings of the transition, (2) contribute to dialogues on the multiplicity of the transition process in various contexts, and (3) collect and share empirical contributions outlining how energy transitions are being materialized.
We will discuss contributions that bring insights from diverse geographical contexts, alongside conceptual  discussions. The sub-topics include, but are not limited to:
• the role of everyday maintenance and repair
• geographies of waste and redundancy
• social reproduction and the energy transition at the micro-scale (gender, households, unpaid labour, and more)
• the role of the arts, creative expression, and storytelling
• social infrastructure of "left behind" regions

paper.title: Energy left behind? The role of energy in making places “left behind”

Coping with darkness: Energy injustice at margins of Gauteng's economy

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Geographies of Food Insecurity: Spatialities, Precarity and the Pursuit of Just Food Places (1)
schedule_event.description:
Food insecurity is rarely a simple matter of food supply or financial poverty; it is a deeply spatialised phenomenon. As the 2026 conference theme highlights, entrenched inequalities are often reinforced by specific city, rural, and embodied landscapes. This session seeks to explore how a geographical lens can re-theorise food insecurity by moving beyond "food deserts" or income-centric models toward a more nuanced understanding of how space and place produce—and potentially resolve—food security and ensure that these resolutions are also socially just.

Papers presented in this session interrogate the "more-than-financial" aspects of food insecurity and responses that engage directly with social justice. We focus on how geographical contexts create unique vulnerabilities for specific groups, how multi-scalar crises (such as the climate emergency or geopolitical shifts) translate into everyday precarity, and how place-based interventions can foster greater capability for socially just food security. By bringing together diverse theoretical and empirical approaches, this session aims to chart a path toward more "just places" in our global, national and local food systems.

paper.title: Precarity and Everyday Practice: The Value-Action Interface and Prosthetic Infrastructures

Everyday negotiations of food security: The lived experience of multi-dimensional poverty in urban South Africa.
Social connectivity and diet: A quantitative study in Whitley (Reading) of the how social connections, including community group connections, correlate to self-reported diets and wellbeing

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Authors meet critics: Eda Beyazit and Karen Lucas's edited volume Gendered Mobilities in the Global South
schedule_event.description:
This book explores how gender shapes everyday mobilities across the Global South, bringing together insights from gender studies, mobilities research, and critical perspectives on infrastructure development. The book approaches mobility as a socio-spatial and political process shaped by power relations, cultural norms, and economic inequalities. It examines how gender intersects with class, race, age, and other identities to structure who moves, how, and under what conditions.

Through a combination of theoretical reflections and empirical case studies from diverse contexts, including South Asia, Sub-Saharan Africa, and Latin America, the book highlights how mobility systems often reinforce gender inequalities, from unsafe public transport environments to unequal access to employment and education. At the same time, it records the everyday strategies women employ to navigate these constraints.

In this Meet the Authors session, the editors, Dr Eda Beyazit (UWE Bristol) and Prof Karen Lucas (University of Manchester), will discuss the motivations behind the project, the challenges of researching gendered mobilities in the Global South, and the implications of the book for more equitable and inclusive transport policy and infrastructure planning. Professor Tim Schwanen (University of Oxford) will reflect on the book’s themes by situating them within broader debates on gender, mobility justice, and decolonising transport research, and will open the discussion to questions from the audience. Gendered Mobilities in the Global South, Edward Elgar Publishing,

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Feminist political ecologies of the agrarian-forest interface
schedule_event.description:
Feminist political ecology has long foregrounded how inequalities in resource access and use, politics of representation, and ways of knowing shape human-environment relations (Elmhirst 2015, Rocheleau et al. 2013). More recent political ecology research from feminists and beyond has sought to explicitly link justice frameworks to understand how multiple forms of inequalities in terms of distributional, procedural, and recognitional justice are reproduced, contested, or subverted through social (in)actions (Svarstad & Benjaminsen 2020, San Martin & Wood 2022, Sultana 2021). These concerns are especially acute at the agrarian-forest interface where communities reliant on natural resources for their livelihoods struggle to survive amidst threats from land grabs, technocratic conservation, capitalist-patriarchal systems, and adverse incorporation to capitalist markets (Elias et al. 2021, Martin et al. 2016). Yet agrarian–forest interfaces are also sites of possibility, where struggles for justice take shape through Indigenous and women’s land rights movements, food sovereignty initiatives, and climate justice organizing (Deere 2017, Guerrero 2013, Radcliffe 2014).

In this session we invite contributions that use feminist political ecology to critically analyze politics of inequality and justice across diverse agrarian-forest interfaces. We are particularly interested in research that attends to intersectional difference—including but not limited to class, caste, gender, sexuality, and able-bodied-ness—and traces how injustices operate across political-economic, epistemic, embodied, and intimate scales. We welcome empirically grounded and theoretically engaged papers, as well as contributions that feature novel or innovative methodological approaches to studying these issues or elevate movements for justice and healing in agrarian-forest landscapes.

Works Cited

Deere, C. D. (2017). Women's land rights, rural social movements, and the state in the 21st‐century Latin American agrarian reforms. Journal of Agrarian Change 17(2), 258-278.

Elias, M., Joshi, D., & Meinzen-Dick, R. (2021). Restoration for whom, by whom? A feminist political ecology of restoration. Ecological Restoration, 39(1-2), 3-15.

Elmhirst, R. (2015). Feminist political ecology. In The Routledge handbook of political ecology (pp. 519-530). Routledge.

Guerrero, M. A. J. (2013). Civil rights versus sovereignty: Native American women in life and land struggles. In Feminist genealogies, colonial legacies, democratic futures (pp. 101-122). Routledge.

Martin, A., Coolsaet, B., Corbera, E., Dawson, N. M., Fraser, J. A., Lehmann, I., & Rodriguez, I. (2016). Justice and conservation: The need to incorporate recognition. Biological conservation, 197, 254-261.

Radcliffe, S. A. (2014). Gendered frontiers of land control: indigenous territory, women and contests over land in Ecuador. Gender, Place & Culture, 21(7), 854-871.

Rocheleau, D., Thomas-Slayter, B., & Wangari, E. (2013). Feminist political ecology: Global issues and local experience. Routledge.

San Martin, W., & Wood, N. (2022). Pluralising planetary justice beyond the North-South divide: Recentring procedural, epistemic, and recognition-based justice in earth-systems governance. Environmental Science & Policy, 128, 256-263.

Sultana, F. (2021). Political ecology 1: From margins to center. Progress in human geography, 45(1), 156-165.

Svarstad, H., & Benjaminsen, T. A. (2020). Reading radical environmental justice through a political ecology lens. Geoforum, 108, 1-11.

paper.title: Contested Human-Environments: A feminist political ecology of nature-society relations at the agrarian-forest interface in northern Thailand

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - The family as a site of environmental politics
schedule_event.description:
In recent years, families and family relations have been increasingly drawn into political discourse surrounding the environment and climate (e.g. birth strike for climate, XR families, Parents for Future, Fridays for Future), drawing attention to how climate change is viewed in an intergenerational frame. Amid concern that the effects of climate and ecological crisis will exacerbate the global care crisis (MacGregor et al 2022), there is a need to investigate how these crises add to and/or reshape caring activities and family practices, as well as the capacities to undertake these activities. Meanwhile, within the environmental policy process, there is a lack of attention to how climate change and care inequalities intersect, resulting in mitigation and adaptation strategies that are unresponsive to the needs of those who undertake care work.

Family relations of care and responsibility motivate protest in the struggle for resources and land around the world, with parental identity often forming deep emotional attachments to just futures, as well as offering a strategic narrative (Conradsen, 2016; Dombroski, 2016; Ramm and Gideon, 2020, 2020). The vital role mothers and other family members play in eco-political struggles has been highlighted; but how involvement in climate and environmental activism shapes the wider realities of family life is less well attended to (see (Howard, 2022). On the other hand, families are often the target of policy and campaigns aimed at reducing household energy use and resource consumption, with the affective and material burden of pro-environmental responsibilities falling disproportionately on (unremunerated) women caregivers (see MacGregor et al 2022).

Despite the emergence of significant work on the family as a site of politics and morality (Holloway, 1998; Hall, 2011; Jupp, 2016; Tarrant and Hall, 2020), geographers have paid little attention to how ethico-political dilemmas of ecological crisis surface and are negotiated within family relations, both within and across generations. This might seem an awkward time to turn the focus to families. Narratives of global ‘fertility collapse’ abound, in some instances attended by a barely veiled antipathy towards single, childless women in particular. The so-called ‘manosphere’ sells a regressive model of family/gender relations as aspirational and desirable, while the rise of ‘pronatalist’ advocacy appears to solidify the marrying of techno-solutionism with eugenics for the ‘baby bust’ era. Nonetheless, whilst family relations are not necessarily superior or more important than other relationships, ‘family remains of importance to people as a distinct, designated aspect of their everyday lives’ (Morgan, 2013). Equally, family is not a singular or neutral concept; it encompasses a multitude of settings and practices, including relations of neglect, uncare and violence. While family settings and relations are highly diverse and do not necessarily include children, family remains a regular touch point in social life, even in its absence. The idea of family continues to matter in different ways across the life course, and the ways in which it comes to matter is thoroughly political.

paper.title: Family Life in Public Pedagogies of Crisis: Children, Care, and Responsibility
Farming Families, Intergenerational and Interspecies Relationships, Biosecurity, and Memory in British Foot-and-Mouth Disease Documentaries’
Growing up in toxicity: towards just environmental health in an unequal world

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Living Maps of Cultural Landscapes
schedule_event.description:
We engage the conference theme of Geographies of inequalities: toward just places by positioning mapping cultural landscapes as a critical practice for advancing spatial and environmental justice. We approach cultural landscapes not merely as physical settings, but as socially produced environments shaped by memory, migration, labour, resistance, and everyday life. Mapping, in this context, becomes a method for revealing how justice—and injustice—are embedded in space.

Conventional cartographies often reproduce dominant territorial impositions privileging state boundaries, property regimes, and extractive economies. In contrast, culturally grounded mapping practices foreground community knowledge, intangible heritage, and lived experience. By emphasising relational connections—between people and place, past and present, mobility and rootedness—mapping becomes both analytical and interpretive. It becomes a tool for preservation, empowerment, and dialogue. Through this approach, landscapes are understood not as static backdrops, but as dynamic and evolving cultural fields.

We ask,  how can cultural mapping expose spatial inequities or histories of dispossession? In what ways can participatory, decolonial, or digital methodologies contribute to more just representations of place? How might mapping practices help reclaim marginalised landscapes or reframe dominant narratives of territory and belonging? By centring cultural landscapes within debates on justice, this session positions mapping as an ethical and political act—one that reshapes how places are understood, governed, and imagined. Through interdisciplinary dialogue, we seek to explore how cultural cartographies can contribute to the making of more equitable and inclusive geographies, by positioning mapping not simply as representation, but as a dynamic process of knowledge production that reshapes how we understand territory, identity, and belonging

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Finding Equality in Coastal and Marine Litter Research and Governance: Learning from Lived Experience on Scotland’s West Coast
schedule_event.description:
Plastic pollution has mobilised the academy across disciplines yet, while its sources are globally shared, its socio-ecological impacts are not. To understand these uneven impacts requires considered integration of lived experience into plastic pollution research. However, such knowledge remains underutilised within scholarly and political landscapes that preferentially pursue the new, the novel, and the international over the current, the everyday, and the local.

Through sustained engagement with communities along Scotland’s west coast, our research has foregrounded the expertise of local knowledge holders working to understand, monitor, and manage marine plastic pollution along their coastlines. While these communities have welcomed academic engagement, they have also challenged dominant research approaches that disconnect marine debris and its impacts from people, overlooking heritage, place-based relationships, and daily life.

Community partners identify standardised scientific methodologies, complex industrial systems, and legislative constraints as key limitations to effective environmental stewardship and sustainable solutions. These limitations are further confounded by practical challenges of time, distance, and money in which this largely volunteer workforce operates. Consequently, the published record presents an environmental summary of marine litter along Scotland’s west coast that is juxtaposed to the lived experiences of those who share their local environments with marine litter year-round.

This panel session responds to this disconnect by centring community perspectives as critical geographical knowledge, providing a platform for community partners to share the spatial, temporal, and political challenges that shape their engagement with plastic pollution on heavily-littered, geographically-distant coastlines. These lived experiences will be situated alongside discussions of research and governance gaps that researchers and CMRG members can contribute to, shaping more just futures for coastal communities. Grounded in principles of ecological citizenship and blue justice, the session positions community partners as panellists and the academy as its audience, fostering dialogue for how geography can better support equitable coastal stewardship.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - New and Emerging Research in Gender and Feminist Geography (1)
schedule_event.description:
The Gender and Feminist Research Group of the RGS (with the IBG) warmly encourages postgraduates and early career researchers to submit papers for our new and emerging research session. The session is ideal for researchers who would like an opportunity to present their research in a supportive and constructive academic environment, and for researchers at all stages of their careers who are interested in presenting papers that actively engage with discussions on current and emerging theoretical or methodological innovations in the field of feminist and gender geography.

‘Gender and Feminist Geographies’ is intended to cover a broad spectrum of research and papers are welcome from any area of feminist and gender geographical inquiry, with the aim of bringing together current and emerging themes, issues and approaches. Papers are especially welcome that connect with this year’s theme of “Geographies of Inequalities: Toward Just Places” at any stage in their research process are welcome, making the session a great opportunity for early career and postgraduate researchers to gain experience presenting their work. The sessions will also provide a forum to meet and discuss emerging ideas with other researchers in a friendly and relaxed environment, as well as opportunities to explore possibilities and relevance of engaging with feminist theory and methods within research.

If you would like to submit a paper for consideration, please send a 300-word abstract to Rituparna Bhattacharyya, rituparna.bhattacharyya@accb.org.uk and Alkım Karaağaç, e.a.karaagac@bham.ac.uk, by Friday, 20th February 2026.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Emerging and innovative pedagogies in geography higher education
schedule_event.description:
The evolving landscape of geography higher education demands pedagogical innovation that is responsive to the diverse needs of students and the challenges of the 21st century. This session invites paper contributions that explore emerging and transformative approaches to teaching, learning, and assessment, offering a platform to share ideas and discuss emerging and innovative HE pedagogies.

Central to the discussion is the integration of inclusive and equitable teaching practices that address the diverse needs of students. By rethinking traditional teaching and learning approaches and exploring alternative assessment models, educators are fostering accessible and engaging learning experiences. These practices are further enhanced by advances in technology, including the use of generative AI. Tools such as AI-driven writing aids, data analysis platforms, and virtual simulations are being leveraged to personalise learning, create innovative assessment opportunities, and prepare students for a digitally-literate world, while also prompting critical discussions about ethics and academic integrity.

The session also explores how pedagogies adapted during the COVID-19 pandemic, such as digital and hybrid teaching, have evolved into powerful tools for the post-pandemic era. Innovative uses of digital platforms and immersive technologies, such as GIS, augmented reality, and online collaborative tools, are changing how students interact with geographical content and their peers and tutors. This transition to flexible and technology-rich learning environments is complemented by a renewed focus on authentic and sustainable fieldwork. By emphasising local contexts, virtual field experiences, and ethical community engagement, educators are balancing pedagogical rigor with social, environmental and financial responsibility.

Recognising the holistic nature of education, this session also considers the affective dimensions of learning. Emotionally supportive pedagogies are emerging as crucial tools in fostering student and staff wellbeing, resilience, and motivation. Equally transformative are student-staff partnerships, where students are empowered as co-creators in curriculum design, assessment practices, and research, ensuring that educational practices remain inclusive, relevant, and reflective of diverse perspectives.

By integrating these themes, the session aims to provide a collaborative space for sharing innovative practices that advance the discipline of geography, preparing students to navigate and address the complex challenges of a changing world.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - New and Emerging Research in Historical Geography (1)
schedule_event.description:
This session provides a space for postgraduates and early-career scholars undertaking research in Historical Geography to present at a major conference in a supportive and relaxed setting. Building upon past successful HGRG postgraduate session, our intention is to foster a supportive environment in which not only postgraduates, but everyone interested in historical geography connects with the wider historical geography community.

We welcome papers from postgraduates / early-career scholars at any stage of their research, that discuss initial research aims/design; engage with methodological and/or theoretical questions; or that draw upon empirical findings. There is no chronological or geographical limit to papers and the sessions will be designed to encourage audience questions, as well as the sharing of experience and advice. In short, this session will demonstrate the vibrancy of postgraduate contributions to Historical Geography.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Unequal Perceptions: Sensuous Geographies and Ecologies of Sensing
schedule_event.description:
In our late-industrial and digitalised world, perception is no longer an exclusively human matter. Today, we find devices, buildings, infrastructures, and even landscapes as perceiving entities (Weizman et al. 2014), next to plants, animals, and other non-human beings. Through sensors, perception becomes a practice of media and technology, ubiquitously distributed over the planet and beyond – often unnoticed in its mode of operation, but generously involved in providing capacities for feeling new socio-spatial realities (Gabrys 2016; Hansen 2011).

Even though human geography has long emphasised senses, perception, embodiment, experience, and meaning as central to place-making (Tuan 1977; Rodaway 2002), questions scrutinising the spatial production – and the producers – of perception itself remain overlooked yet fundamental. If we understand perception as practice, embedded in cultural nexuses, done by humans and non-humans, both bodily and technically, we must also consider spaces and places excluded from these productions – by accident or by design – remaining as sensory blind spots, staying unattended. This session brings these regimes of perception into focus, asking how sensuous, bodily ways of knowing place (Pink 2015; Sumartojo & Pink 2018) intersect and relate to technical and more-than-human forms of sensing (Benson 2010; Ballestero 2019).

Tracing the inequalities these differing perceptual regimes may lead to, we seek to shift the attention towards the preconditions and configurations of environmental sensing, sensuous lifeworlds, and affective atmospheres. How and where are perceptual regimes produced? Who decides what is, or what may become, perceptible, measurable, and detectable? Technical sensing systems may translate environmental conditions into data, thresholds, and alerts, while everyday sensory experiences register feelings of discomfort, risk, attachment, or loss. These modes do not operate separately; they interact, reinforce, and contradict one another, forming their own ecologies of sensing (Parisi 2009) which shape how late-industrial places become liveable or unliveable; reinforcing or alleviating inequalities.

We welcome a wide range of conceptual, empirical, and methodological contributions and case studies engaging with:

  • Sensuous and experiential ways of knowing place and the world
  • Environmental sensing and monitoring as spatial practices
  • Spaces and places of perception production
  • Relations between lived experience, data, and technical sensation
  • Perceptual politics of (in)visibility and ignorance
  • Experimental and interdisciplinary approaches to sensuous and perceptual geographies in practice

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - MENA Research Group Postgraduate Session
schedule_event.description:
Postgraduate students conducting research on the Middle East and North Africa, or on diasporic communities beyond these spaces, will present their work at this session, designed to provide a supportive and relaxed platform at a major academic conference. Our intention is to foster an environment where connections between Masters, PhD students, Early Career Researchers, and the wider community can be made.

Papers will discuss initial research aims/design, engage with methodological and/or theoretical questions, or that draw upon empirical findings. The sessions will be designed to encourage audience questions, as well as the sharing of experience and ideas. By showcasing the dynamic contributions of postgraduate researchers, we aim to create an inclusive and collaborative space for academic growth.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Attuning to More-than-human Seas: Negotiating Multispecies Justice in the Anthropocene Ocean (1)
schedule_event.description:
“What would it mean to take the Anthropocene out to sea?” (Alaimo, 2017, p. 153). With this provocation, Alaimo highlights the persistent neglect of marine environments and species within the terra-centric Anthropocene discourse. Oceans are profoundly marked by human activity, yet continue to be framed as vast, remote, and seemingly inexhaustible spaces for exploitation. Over the past two decades, the ocean ‘out there’ has been promoted as the latest frontier for economic development and an unexplored playground for climate change mitigation, often under the banner of “blue growth” (Johnson and Braverman, 2019; Bennett et al., 2021; Buck, 2019). Critically engaging with Blue Economy ambitions (and their unintended consequences), this session explores how sustainable futures and multispecies justices might be realised in Anthropocene seas. Blue spaces beyond the coastline are typically uninhabited by humans, difficult to access and govern, and frequently located beyond the boundaries of national territories. Such spaces are nevertheless deeply entangled with global political economies, colonial histories, resource extraction, and uneven distributions of environmental risk, responsibility, and benefit. These dynamics increasingly render marginalised coastal communities and marine organisms vulnerable to the cascading impacts of human activities in the ocean. Put simply, Anthropocene seas are inherently unequal places and spaces. 

 Taking seriously the dynamic fluidity of the ocean requires moving beyond static separations between humans and nature, sea and land, or matter and mediation (Steinberg and Peters, 2015; Peters and Steinberg, 2019), to instead spotlight turbulent marine lifeworlds and more-than-human aquatic assemblages that are subjected to – yet also contest, challenge, and resist – unjust oceanic political economies. We thus invite papers that grapple with diverse notions of multispecies justice, blue justice, and blue degrowth to highlight overlooked oceanic geographies and to ask how Anthropocene seas can become sites for just and sustainable multispecies futures. 

 We welcome conceptual, empirical, methodological, and creative contributions that include, but are not limited to, the following themes:

  • Extending critical Anthropocene scholarship to oceanic contexts
  • More-than-human engagements with the Blue Economy and blue (de)growth
  • Indigenous and decolonial perspectives on Anthropocene oceans
  • Afrofuturism, speculative ecologies and imagined ocean futures
  • More-than-human oceanic geopolitics
  • Affect and the ocean: embodied and emotional human-marine relations
  • Blue animal geographies
  • Multispecies marine methodologies

paper.title: Environmental Intimacies: Multispecies Care, Affective Attunement, and Methodological Experiments in Coral Restoration
Aquatic sensing: Encounters with microalgae through sensory methodologies
Stranded: life and death on the Anthropocene’s seashores
For the love of kelp: listening to emotion as multispecies methodology
Plastisphere in Chthulucene. “Staying with the problem” in oceanic natureculture assemblages

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Restorative in/justice for human and more-than-human entities through past, present and uncertain climate futures
schedule_event.description:
Recognising and resolving harms and losses associated with climate change and climate in/action is essential for restorative justice. Indeed, both mitigation and adaptation decisions pose a number of justice dilemmas that can lead to unavoidable losses; for example, communities may need to be permanently relocated, development and land rights may need to be restricted in certain locations, and ecosystems may be sacrificed to make way for climate initiatives (Juhola et al., 2022). Yet, despite being essential to the transformative climate justice agenda (See et al., 2024), restorative justice has remained the least studied dimension of climate justice.

With empirical research still in its infancy, crucial theoretical and practical questions remain unanswered (Newell et al., 2021). How is restorative in/justice experienced and articulated? How is restorative in/justice re/constructed through multi-scalar governance structures and power dynamics? How should restorative in/justices be redressed, for whom, by whom and through what mechanisms (e.g. reconciliation and repair)? How should the agents of injustice and non/agency be considered in these debates? How are restorative matters mis/recognised and de/legitimised through anticipated futures? How do articulations of, or speculations upon, future action shape our politics of the present? With calls to decentre the human and stand in solidarity with the more-than-human world (Tschakert, 2020; Chao et al., 2022; Sebo, 2022), how might restorative justice for nature be voiced and enacted in just transitions? Each of these questions raises interesting ethical and moral questions, as well as theoretical, methodological and empirical challenges. Moreover, the concept draws attention to the temporalities of justice; while ‘re’ evokes retrospective concern with the past, restorative justice should also enable proactive, forward-looking approaches that anticipate and circumvent future harms (See et al., 2024).

Engaging with these critical questions, this session will present theoretical, methodological, and empirical contributions that advance restorative justice debates from human, non- and more-than-human perspectives, allowing for open discussions afterwards.

paper.title: Speculative Multispecies Dialogues for Restorative Justice:  A Cross-Coastal Reading Group Case Study of Eva Meijer’s Sea Now and Ben Smith’s Doggerland
Narrating non-human restorative in/justices on the Norfolk coast
What is a just managed retreat? Future imaginaries and restorative justice in coastal adaptation
Balancing restorative justice for more-than-humans in allocating land for climate adaptation

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Geographies of inequalities and bias in AI
schedule_event.description:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) has entered our daily life and work, transforming how many people learn about the world, as well as the way we approach data analysis in human, digital and physical geographies (Ash et al., 2019; Bennett and De Sabbata, 2023; Gao, 2023; Janowicz et al., 2022; Osborne and Jones 2023). However, AI models and tools have their own geographies, shaped by their creation and deployment (Bender et al., 2021; Miceli and Posada, 2022; Weidinger et al., 2022), and which, in turn, generate and shape disinformation and misinformation, biases, and inequalities. The societal and ethical implications of AI are still underexplored in a continuously, rapidly changing technological and market environment. Crucially, many discussions are confined to (sub-)disciplinary silos, which hinder a broader understanding of this complex phenomenon.

Aligning itself to this year’s Chair’s theme “Geographies of inequalities: towards just places”, this session aims to be an open forum to bridge critical analysis of AI based on theories in human and digital geographies (Sieber et al., 2025), and quantitative geographic analysis on AI safety and alignment (De Sabbata et al., 2025; Janowicz et al., 2025; Van de Weghe et al., 2025).

References

  • Ash, James, Rob Kitchin, and Agnieszka Leszczynski. 2019. Digital Geographies Edited by James Ash, Rob Kitchin, Agnieszka Leszczynski. Los Angeles: Sage.
  • Bender, E.M., Gebru, T., McMillan-Major, A. and Shmitchell, S., 2021, March. On the dangers of stochastic parrots: Can language models be too big?. In Proceedings of the 2021 ACM conference on fairness, accountability, and transparency (pp. 610-623).
  • Bennett, K. and De Sabbata, S., 2023. Introducing a more-than-quantitative approach to explore emerging structures of feeling in the everyday. Emotion, Space and Society, 49, p.100965.
  • Bommasani, R., et al., 2021. On the opportunities and risks of foundation models. arXiv preprint arXiv:2108.07258.
  • De Sabbata, S., Mizzaro, S. and Roitero, K., 2025. Geospatial Mechanistic Interpretability of Large Language Models. arXiv preprint arXiv:2505.03368.
  • Gao, S., 2023. Artificial intelligence and human geography. arXiv preprint arXiv:2312.08827.
  • Janowicz, K., Sieber, R., and Crampton, J. (2022). GeoAI, counter-AI, and human geography: A conversation. Dialogues in Human Geography, 12(3), 446-458.
  • Janowicz, K. et al. (2025). Whose Truth? Pluralistic Geo-Alignment for (Agentic) AI. In Proceedings of the 33rd ACM International Conference on Advances in Geographic Information Systems (pp. 799-803).
  • Miceli, M. and Posada, J., 2022. The Data-Production Dispositif. Proceedings of the ACM on Human-Computer Interaction, 6(CSCW2), pp.1-37.
  • Osborne, Tess, and Phil Jones, eds. 2023. A Research Agenda for Digital Geographies. Edward Elgar.
  • Sieber, R., Brandusescu, A., Sangiambut, S. and Adu-Daako, A., 2025. What is civic participation in artificial intelligence?. Environment and Planning B: Urban Analytics and City Science, 52(6), pp.1388-1406.
  • Van de Weghe, N., et al. (2025). Opportunities and challenges of integrating geographic information science and large language models. Journal of Spatial Information Science, (30), pp.93-116.
  • Weidinger, L. et al. (2022), June. Taxonomy of risks posed by language models. In Proceedings of the 2022 ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (pp. 214-229).

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Towards just places through children’s geographies in, of, and for, education (1)
schedule_event.description:
This session invites discussion about the value of, and possibilities for, further engagement with children's geographies in, and of, education, policy and practice. We focus on education as integral to children's everyday lives and their understanding of the world. From lecture halls to forest schools, education experiences unfold across diverse (in)formal and (un)structured spaces. Education spaces serve not only as sites of teaching and learning, but also as spaces of play, socialisation, resistance, and identity formation (McKendrick & Hammond, 2020; Kraftl, 2022; Potter & Cowan, 2020). 

Yet these educational experiences are shaped by structural forces that create and maintain educational inequalities. School design, curriculum priorities, accountability measures and the legacies of residential schools often work to (re)create and (re)produce (in)equity and (in)justice (Catling & Martin, 2011; Kulz, 2017). We are particularly interested in how structural and institutional conditions and mechanisms intersect and compound across multiple scales, from embodied and everyday experiences in classrooms to regional, national, and global patterns of exclusion and marginalization.

To explore these matters further, we welcome contributions examining possibilities for children’s geographies in education, considering for example, where children are present or absent in curricula or engaged with through pedagogy across different phases and spaces of education (Catling, 2005; Hammond, 2022; Owens et al., 2022). We also welcome contributions that consider children’s geographies of education, exploring for example how children - with different identities - experience and imagine material and social spaces of education spaces and systems (schools, homeschooling-pods, forest schools etc) (Holloway et al., 2010; English, 2021; Brown, 2022; Kraftl et al., 2022; Brundrett, Malone & Rowley, 2025). This includes how children navigate and experience unequal access across and as shaped by digital, physical, and environmental factors. We invite contributions from those researching and teaching in higher education, as well colleagues working in K-12 or alternative education spaces. We would be particularly delighted to hear from children and young people themselves through research or direct contribution.   Through this session, we aim to strengthen connections between children's geographies scholarship and educational practice, working toward more just and equitable educational futures.

paper.title: A Feathered Foundation: Children’s Connections with Local Species, Implications for Research and Education
Girls in the woods: The influence of Forest School on primary aged girls’ perceptions of (invisible) girlhood

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Cartographies of Everyday Inequalities (1)
schedule_event.description:
Cartographies of Everyday Inequalities: Spatial entanglements in urban spaces
Dr. Kiran Bhairannavar and Vishavjeet Dhanda 
Department of Geography, University of Delhi

Key words: urban space, inequalities, just city, informalities, invisibilities, lived space

Who gets stopped while walking? Who gets policed in public spaces? Whose vending is penalized and whose informal practices remain invisible? These questions raise important concerns about how authority is selectively exercised on certain bodies and practices that become targets of regulation, while similar practices by others evade scrutiny entirely. The session examines how everyday spaces—streets, markets, parks, public spaces, homes, neighbourhoods, digital spaces—become sites where spatial inequalities are produced, contested, and navigated (Martínez Rodríguez, 2019; Cohen & Knierbein, 2021). We focus on mapping these inequalities not as static distributions of disadvantage but as dynamic socio-spatial processes.

There has been considerable work on urban inequalities as a key theme, however an everyday episodic lens largely remains distant (Ghertner, 2015; Massa, 2020; Jaffe, 2025). Taking this as a point of departure, we are interested in ‘everyday inequalities’ as emerging through intersecting spatial entanglements produced by state mechanisms, infrastructural arrangements, and socio-cultural hierarchies. (Simone, 2021; Kulz, 2025; Zsinka & Berger, 2025; Shafique, 2025). We seek to better understand how spatial practices further complicate these inequalities through differential toleration, penalization, or regularization depending on who occupies space, where and how; reinforcing hierarchies along lines of caste, class, gender, race, ethnicity, religion, sexuality and ability.

Foregrounding within these ideas, we are looking forward to contributions that help us further unpack the everyday nuances of inequalities. We seek answers to: How can we map everyday spatial inequalities beyond conventional approaches? What methods, tools and frameworks can reveal intersecting logics of agency, power, informalities, survival, and resistance? How do we foreground marginalized experiences ethically and non-extractively within the urban cultural episteme? How do everyday inequalities shape differential lived realities and futures? What role do affective and embodied experiences play in sustaining or challenging spatial hierarchies? How might everyday inequalities as a concept transform urban policy, planning practice, and governance frameworks toward more just cities and urban spaces?

The session welcomes contributions addressing:

  • Mapping everyday inequalities through banalities of access, waiting, affordability, and precarity
  • Everyday carceralities: discretion, extortion, selective enforcement, and penalization in routine practices
  • Public complicity and middle-class activism invoking/resisting state violence to securitize/secure everyday spaces
  • Embodied and emotional geographies of survival, policing, and displacement
  • Materialities and discourses: how everyday spaces are operationalized, contested, and reimagined.

paper.title: The (im)possibility of living together. Pragmatic cohabitation as practice in suburban Johannesburg
Fostering Belonging Despite Displacement: Shadow Care Infrastructure and Constellations in the City
Bodies, Borders, and the Spatialisation of Inequality: A Methodological Proposal
Beyond (in)formal: Generative Practices, Planning Discourse, and the Making of Urban Inequality on Delhi’s Footpaths

Department of Geography, University of Delhi

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - (Re)Imagining Just Climate Futures through Creative Approaches (1)
schedule_event.description:
Climate change is not only a technical or environmental challenge; it is also a struggle of imagination (Yusoff & Gabrys, 2011). Dominant climate futures are increasingly shaped by models, metrics and algorithmic projections, while questions of justice, care, power and responsibility are often sidelined. In line with the theme of the 2026 RGS-IBG conference, Geographies of Inequalities: Towards Just Places, we invite contributions that interrogate these dominant imaginaries and experiment with creative techniques to (re)imagine just climate futures.

Drawing on feminist ethics of care and a reparative orientation (Puig de la Bellacasa, 2017; Myers et al., 2024; Tuck, 2009), we approach future-making as situated, relational labour—work that foregrounds uneven burdens and asks how climate imaginaries may normalise loss and reproduce colonial hierarchies by privileging some lives, knowledges and places over others (Sultana, 2022). Creative methods are not simply alternative modes of presentation here; they are ways of knowing and intervening (e.g. Hawkins, 2015). Working with affect, embodiment and narrative, they can unsettle model- and metric-driven registers and make room for futures that challenge what counts as “realistic.” Contributions in this panel thus analyse and experiment with creative approaches—art–science collaboration, participatory speculation, speculative mapping and also drawing (see e.g. Davies, 2024; Perry, 2018) —while attending to the frictions, ethical dilemmas and power relations involved in imagining otherwise.

The session aims to offer an open space to share reflective and experimental contributions on futuring practices and future-oriented research in geography and allied fields. We welcome work that attends to research processes as much as outcomes, including limitations, frictions, failures, ethical dilemmas and political tensions that emerge when working with uncertain futures, communities and technologies. We encourage a variety of formats, from traditional papers to art-based and practice-based contributions and other experimental formats (e.g., performances, facilitated exercises, video screenings).

We are particularly interested in contributions addressing:

  • Futuring practices and future-oriented methodologies centering the role of conflicts, emotions and positionalities
  • Reparative, caring and justice-oriented approaches to climate futures engaging with feminist and decolonial debates
  • Art–science collaborations and interdisciplinary research
  • Creative, participatory and experimental research methods including the use of drawing, illustration, comics and/or speculative cartography
  • Critical perspectives on AI, data and modelling in climate governance
  • Questions of inclusion, exclusion and power in processes of future-making

paper.title: Harnessing Friction and Care: (Re)Imagining Just Climate Futures through AI
Prompting Future Visualizations in Performance Art: Intermedia, Liveness and AI in Reparative Climate Imaginaries

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - (Re)Imagining Just Climate Futures through Creative Approaches (2): World Cafe
schedule_event.description:
Climate change is not only a technical or environmental challenge; it is also a struggle of imagination (Yusoff & Gabrys, 2011). Dominant climate futures are increasingly shaped by models, metrics and algorithmic projections, while questions of justice, care, power and responsibility are often sidelined. In line with the theme of the 2026 RGS-IBG conference, Geographies of Inequalities: Towards Just Places, we invite contributions that interrogate these dominant imaginaries and experiment with creative techniques to (re)imagine just climate futures.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Writing Minor: Nonrepresentational Inequalities of Asian Diasporas (1)
schedule_event.description:
This session explores the non-representational aspects of Asian diaspora inequalities in two directions. First, we examine existing media portrayals, political discourse, and cultural stereotypes that reduce the polyvocality of Asian diasporic lives and reproduce minority lives as unequally 'minor' (Hong 2020). We look particularly closely at the uneven representations and territorialisations of emotionality, sensibility, and viscerality along lines of race, nationality, and citizenship. Second, we seek alternative, irregular, and 'minor' ways of representing diasporic lives and experiences that challenge these existing discourses and theoretical frameworks. Affirming the force of the minor, we explore creative forms of expression, narratives, and world-making practices that exist in the spaces made within and transcending inequality (Yapp 2021).

Overall, this session examines the importance of representational politics while encouraging alternative ways of writing geographical research on diaspora, with special attention to fluid, affective, and experimental approaches. We encourage submissions that engage with Asian diasporas globally, including but not limited to East Asian, Southeast Asian, South Asian, and Central Asian contexts, and welcome comparative and transnational approaches.

We also welcome diverse formats of writing, including traditional papers, visual essays, creative-critical hybrids, and collaborative presentations that push the boundaries of conventional academic formats.

This session aims to explore directions including, but not limited to:
•     Diasporic home and world-making efforts in the interstices of unequal representations
•     Diasporic geographies of senses, bodies, and intimacy
•     Critical analysis and/or creative re-animations of unequal media representations of diaspora
•     Alternative ways of theorising and representing the diaspora as and beyond 'minor'
•     Intersectional experiences of inequality within and across Asian diasporic communities (including dimensions of class, gender, sexuality, generation, and ability)
•     Historical and contemporary perspectives on Asian diasporic nonrepresentation

References
Hong, C.P. (2020) Minor feelings: a reckoning on race and the Asian condition. London: Profile Books.
Yapp, H. (2021) Minor China: method, materialisms, and the aesthetic. Durham: Duke University Press.

paper.title: Minority as Morally Complex Other: the Problem of Unlikability
Beyond Godly Affinities: Fenling Rituals and Hybrid Localities in Southeast Asian Mazu Diasporic Space-Making

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - The ‘rural crisis’ and social inequalities in housing and other services
schedule_event.description:
The rural dimensions of the contemporary ‘housing crisis’ in the UK are arguably under-stated in national media and political discourses, despite commentators raising serious concerns of rural housing affordability and exclusion (e.g. Colomb and Gallent, 2022; Korden and Naumann, 2024; Shucksmith, 2025; Woods, 2025). Over the last decade, the unaffordability of rural housing for many local social groups has been exacerbated by processes of rural gentrification (Sherman, 2023); increasing second/multiple home ownership (Gallent et al., 2023 ); the proliferation of short-term rentals, including AirBnBs and holiday lets, and; the rising availability of agricultural properties for conversion to residential housing (as farms increasingly wind-down due to harsh economic conditions) (Franks, 2022). The predilection for rural living has also been intensified by opportunities for affluent groups to counterurbanise and work-from-home, and reduce commuting (Wilson et al., 2025). At the same time, state-led pressures for more new-build housing have led to many rural spaces on the fringes of metropolitan, provincial and small market towns, as well as villages and hamlets, being transformed. Although this may provide an opportunity to provide more affordable housing, these new estates often comprise relatively high cost, exclusive housing, with limited proportions of ‘affordable housing’. These processes of rural change are giving rise to widening social inequalities between and within many rural places in the UK, which is also having a cascading impact on the supply and demand for rural services, such as pubs, post offices, health centres and retail. In this session, we seek papers that connect with the broad issue of rural housing and social inequalities, and which examine factors and outcomes associated with growing inequalities in rural housing markets and rural places.

References

Colomb, C., & Gallent, N. (2022). Post-COVID-19 mobilities and the housing crisis in European urban and rural destinations. Policy challenges and research agenda. Planning Practice & Research, 37(5), 624-641.

Franks, J. R. (2022). UK Agriculture at a Crossroads. Outlook on Agriculture, 51(4), 448-459.

Gallent, N., Stirling, P., & Hamiduddin, I. (2023). Pandemic mobility, second homes and housing market change in a rural amenity area during COVID-19–The Brecon Beacons National Park, Wales. Progress in Planning, 172, 100731.

Kordel, S., & Naumann, M. (2024). The rural housing crisis: analytical dimensions and emblematic issues. Housing Studies, 39(12), 3029-3046.

Sherman, J. (2023). “Please don’t take this”: Rural gentrification, symbolic capital, and housing insecurity. Social Problems, 70(2), 491-510.

Shucksmith, M. (2025). The exclusive countryside post‐pandemic. The Geographical Journal, 191(4), e12524.

Wilson, R., Noble, C., & Currie, M. (2025). The changing rural idyll and the ideal migrant: The case of Scotland during COVID-19. Journal of Rural Studies, 114, 103497.

Woods, M. (2025). Rural recovery or rural spatial justice? Responding to multiple crises for the British countryside. The geographical journal, 191(4), e12541.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Failed practices and places of failure – on the peripheralization of failure
schedule_event.description:
Failure seems to be pushed to the periphery. Things go wrong, people fail, but this should rather not happen where it matters. What does this reveal about how normality and professionality are thought of and placed? In this session, we build on recent research in geography and beyond that highlights the role of failure as a social phenomenon, as a part of practice, and of place-related normativities. Together, we will explore preconditions and manifestations/localisations of failure, along with its trajectories and (re)presentations. In this session we ask:

• What role do situational circumstances, structural characteristics and institutions play in the production of failure of people in a marginalized areas?

• What resources are needed to not fail at the periphery or how could failure be addressed in movements between individual care and systemic resistance?

• How and in what ways are the lived experiences and multiplicities of failure as a localised and/or mobile phenomenon expressed in everyday practices – including or especially in socio-spatial areas (professional or institutional contexts) that do not appear to have failed, but rather seem to be or present themselves as exemplary or successful?

• What are potential advantages/disadvantages of failure happening in or being pushed to the margins?

paper.title: Failed Places – On the Production of “places of failure” as a form of territorial stigmatization
The Renegotiation of Failed Materialities: Ethnographic Insights into Reuse Practices, Deconstruction-sites and Asbestos.
When Problems Do (Not) Become Failures: The Peripheralization of Recognition and Accountability
Unpacking The Logistics Town: speculation on warehouses, land and labour

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Exploring the emerging geographies of fintech: from mobile banking to cryptocurrencies to Central Bank Digital Currencies (CBDCs)
schedule_event.description:
There has been an increased interest among geographers in recent years in the rise of ‘fintech.’ Early attention has focused on private fintech innovations such as money transfer apps (Natile 2020), private payment apps for entrepreneurs (Baker 2021), private loan platforms (Langley and Leyshon 2021), and private mobile- enabled insurance products (Migozzi et al. 2021). This literature has variously found that fintech innovations such as mobile money and cryptocurrencies can ‘disrupt’ prevailing consumer markets (Zook and Grote 2024), reduce costs on sending remittances (Cirolia et al. 2022; Robins 2025), improve access to credit (Ding et al. 2018), and widen access to insurance products (Mhlanga 2023). They can in some circumstances however also usher in new forms of digital financial exclusion, surveillance, and indebtedness (Bhagat and Roderick 2020); while also arguably facilitating only a partial – and particularly neoliberalized – conception of ‘development’ (Girard 2022).

Though there has so far been less critical attention on state-led fintech innovations such as Central Bank Digital Currencies (CBDC) and Central Bank- managed instant payment platforms such as Brazil’s Pix, geographers have nonetheless opened up important conversations about these new innovations. This includes investigating the possible political and economic disruption, competition, and ‘disintermediation’ that state-led fintech innovations may usher in (Lai 2020; Huber 2023; Chia and Helleiner 2024), the potential of state-led fintech innovations to drive new spatial patterns of local and regional development (Chu et al. 2025), questions of culture and public acceptance (Baronchelli et al. 2022), and ethical questions about privacy and surveillance (Wenker 2022).

This session aims to build on these emerging theoretical and empirical insights by inviting presentations on geographically-informed papers on fintech innovations. We are particularly interested in those working on fintech from the perspective of the changing role of the state within economy and society. The session aims to address a range of questions about fintech innovations, including (but not limited to):

  1. How and for whom are fintech innovations facilitating financial inclusion, and what are their limitations as financial inclusion tools?
  2. How are fintech innovations contributing to enhanced economic development, especially in overcoming prior market or spatial barriers to development?
  3. To what extent are fintech innovations proving to be a source of competition and disintermediation for incumbent finance actors and practices, such as commercial banks and other finance firms?
  4. How are fintech innovations reconfiguring the global geographies of money and finance, especially where they appear to challenge existing currencies and money transfer infrastructures such as SWIFT and BACS?
  5. How are fintech innovations being perceived and received by the public in different places, especially in the context of concerns over privacy and surveillance?

paper.title: From cryptocurrency to cryptofinance: FTX, disintermediation and the US state
Value extraction in Global Digital Networks
Fintech path creation in China: an evolutionary economic geography perspective
The Geography of the UK’s Fintech Ecosystem: The First Fifteen Years

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Environmental Geography without AMOC (Atlantic Meridional Overturning Current)
schedule_event.description:
What happens to environmental geography if the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Current—AMOC—significantly weakens or collapses? It is a scenario of increasing importance for everybody concerned with how this critical current in the Atlantic Ocean, and which transports warm water to northwestern Europe, is affected by a confluence of anthropogenic forces, of which climate change is a principal driver. What happens to environmental geography—and what is happening already—when irreversible shifts to AMOC are examined, contemplated, and speculatively engaged?

This panel identifies the potential collapse of AMOC as a significant moment across geography, and especially for environmental geographers. The scale, significance, and systemic impacts of shifts in AMOC throw together oceanic, coastal, and terrestrial human-environment relations in ways that do not fit easily with either human or physical geography approaches to them. Yet issues around how futures are imagined or foreclosed, and how scholarship before, during, or after critical tipping points are crossed press for responses. In this context, this panel discusses the ways that AMOC, and its potential collapse, demand conceptual vocabularies and empirical categories sufficient to gain purchase on complex and potentially rapidly shifting human-environment relationships.

paper.title: What is a current? AMOC and the semiotics of flow
Futures thinking and the politics of anticipation
Geography with AMOC
Ice and the AMOC
The future of human–coastal coupled systems

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Geography in Action: Revealing and Reducing Health Inequalities at the UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA)
schedule_event.description:
Public health outcomes are hugely influenced by place. From access to healthcare and green space to exposure to environmental hazards and infectious diseases, geography is an important determinant of health equity. This session explores how spatial thinking, geospatial analytics, and place‑based approaches can help identify disparities, design targeted interventions, and inform equitable public health policy. Through four speaker presentations followed by an interactive panel discussion, participants will gain insight into how geographic perspectives can transform decision‑making and reduce inequity across communities. This session will highlight Health Equity and Inclusion Health at the UK Health Security Agency.

Learning Objectives

  1. Highlight how UKHSA are addressing Health Inequalities through strategic frameworks, visualisation, geospatial analysis and collaborative research
  2. Describe how geography influences public health inequalities, including environmental, social, and infrastructural determinants
  3. Recognise the value of geospatial data and tools in assessing, visualising, analysing and addressing disparities.
  4. Identify collaborative opportunities between geospatial experts, public health professionals, and community partners.

Session Structure 

  1. Welcome & Introduction - James Lewis, Head of Geospatial, UKHSA
  2. Speaker Presentations
  3. Panel Discussion with All Speakers 
  4. Audience Q&A 
  5. Closing Remarks - James Lewis, Head of Geospatial, UKHSA

paper.title: Different Strains: Social Patterning of Antimicrobial-Resistant E. coli
Reframing pandemic preparedness using an equity-centred approach
Driving Health Equity: UKHSA’s Strategy for Reducing Inequalities and Advancing Inclusion Health
Visualising Geographic Health Inequality in England

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Extended urbanisation in South Africa's former homelands
schedule_event.description:
South Africa’s former bantustans (or apartheid-era ethnic homelands) present a dilemma for post-apartheid policy making and governance. The ten bantustans were central to the apartheid plan for racial spatial engineering, and it was a fair expectation that they would wither away with their full reintegration into South Africa at the end of apartheid. However, bantustan geographies proved to be resilient, with a longstanding scholar of their histories, William Beinart, commenting in 2012 that it is “surprising how many people stayed in the former ‘homelands’ and dense rural settlements”. Yet not even Beinart could have anticipated the scale of a seemingly incongruous transformation currently underway.

South Africa’s first three post-apartheid censuses indicate that the population of almost all former bantustan areas remained static or even declined in the fifteen years after apartheid. But the latest Census 2022 results show a reversal – between 2011 and 2022 the former bantustans together had a population growth rate higher than the rest of the county. Some were growing significantly faster than metropolitan municipalities, including crisis-ridden Johannesburg. This growth is being accompanied by dramatic changes in the built environment. Particularly significant is the recent surge of homebuilding on land administered by traditional authorities. These investments are at a scale and intensity, and of a character, that seem incongruous with areas imagined to be rural or represented in policy and planning as rural. This residential construction, in styles and sizes typically associated with middle-class or even luxury suburbs and gated communities in metropolitan cities, is not isolated. The trend has been observed across many parts of the country and is increasingly accompanied by related developments such as local- and regional-scale malls.

This session combines four papers that explore this extraordinary change in direction in South Africa urbanisation path. The papers are all located within the theoretical and analytical frame of extended urbanisation – the processes by which different forms of ‘the urban’ spread beyond conventionally defined cities into territories conventionally understood as ‘rural’, often under the aegis of wider urban forces that may be planetary in scale and scope. Combined, the papers are both informed by, and speak back to inform, the theory of extended urbanisation.

The first paper considers the scale of demographic and spatial change, seeking to account for what is going on in the conjuncture of multiple, overlapping, and mutually reinforcing social, economic, and cultural processes playing out nationally. The second points at regional and local variations across the former bantustans and looks to explain differences in terms of spatial considerations and historical legacies. A third paper looks closely at extended urbanisation in Bushbuckridge, a very rapidly growing ex-bantustan area outside the commuting range of any large urban centre, and accounts for growth in terms of a three dimensional framework of material transformations, regulatory practices, and the production of new experiences and meanings. A fourth paper examines extended urbanisation in areas of dual land governance on the ‘inherited periphery’ of the Gauteng City-Region and eThekwini.

paper.title: Extended urbanisation in the former bantustans: Extent and underlying processes
Differentiated patterns of change across South Africa’s former Bantustans: A closer look at the Limpopo, Mpumalanga and Eastern Cape provinces
What is Bushbuckridge? Extended urbanisation, displaced urbanism, or new urban territories in South Africa

paper_authors.identity_string: Chair of Urban and Spatial Development, Centre for Urbanisation and Peripheralization, Technical University of Munich (Munich, Germany)

paper.title: ‘Homeland’ legacies in South Africa’s city-region peripheries: emerging, consolidating and stagnating urban territories in Gauteng and eThekwini

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Care in Crisis Systems: Engaged research in and of the health system
schedule_event.description:
Renditions of NHS crisis are a normalised feature of public discourse in Britain. Entrenched funding, staffing and infrastructure shortages intersect within narrations of this ‘critical’ crisis (Darzi, 2024; Herrick, 2025). Yet viewing healthcare solely through the lens of cuts can occlude analysis of the shifting institutional arrangements austerity brings: private sector interests increasingly enmeshed in care delivery; technological investments; the promotion of community care models (NHS England, 2025). Considering Herrick’s (2025) spotlighting of the NHS as a vital concern for critical geographers, we ask what role ethnographic and engaged research can play in elucidating situated experiences of institutional reconfigurations.

This session builds on important analyses of discriminatory patterns of work and recruitment (Batnitzky & McDowell, 2011; Irons, 2024), as well as calls from feminist, trans geographers and disability scholars as to the pernicious, potentially violent logics enacted through health system thresholds and dismissals (Bailey et al., 2020; Kavanagh et al., 2021; Todd, 2023; Dhairyawan, 2024). We draw too on analyses which examine prolonged experiences of waithood, which characterise care in a ‘holding pattern’ (Kiely 2021, p. 717), alongside research attending to gatekeeping and threshold practices that compel care-seekers to articulate deservingness (Kiely & Warnock, 2022; Muir & Hawking, 2024).

We are especially interested in research which charts– through ethnography, engaged practices or biographies– how people’s experiences of the health system are entangled with wider quests for good care, both within and beyond it. Across such contexts, this session will explore how resilience is made as an affective and ethical demand differentially imposed on people and communities. Attending to the uneven conditions under which refusal and non-use of care, for instance, become possible foregrounds how unjust systems distribute not only harm and protection, but also the very possibility of contestation (Benjamin, 2016).

paper.title: Under Pressure: Distress, Obligation and Care in Post-Welfare UK
Medication Access Networks: A Research Agenda for Informal Pharmaceutical Flows
Suturing Care: The transnational pursuit of elective weight loss surgery
PrEP as Socio-Medical-Spatial Infrastructure: Delivery and Access Pathways
Possibilities for storytelling as transgressive care in and beyond systems of medico-legal abortion provision

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Dis/En/Abling mobility: Mobile labour and (in)justices in access to mobility
schedule_event.description:
Mobility is a fundamental prerequisite for everyday life in all kinds of spatial patterns. While cities are often portrayed as hubs of movement and accessibility, rural regions are increasingly shaped by a growing necessity for mobility as public services and infrastructures continue to decline. At the same time, all spaces remain deeply dependent on mobility to sustain work, care, and social life.

Yet, what frequently remains hidden is where, how, and whose mobility is enabled. Put differently, who is mobile for whom —and at what cost—so that others can move faster, farther, or more conveniently across spatial scales? What spatial disparities characterize access to mobility? Mobility services and infrastructure are neither evenly distributed, nor just in the ways they are built and used. Rather, mobility systems are produced, maintained, and enabled by political decisions and the labour, time, and bodies of others, while mobility itself also depends on individuals’ capabilities and resources.

This session therefore foregrounds frictions and tensions in the provision of and access to mobility services and infrastructures, drawing attention to the often invisible labour, spatial arrangements and social relations that are the backbone of mobility provision systems. From bus drivers who enable everyday accessibility, to platform-based delivery cyclists and couriers whose work is an integral part of cities’ transport logistics, mobility is continuously produced. In rural contexts, access to essential services, beyond those available locally, often depends on community-based, informal, and private transport provision. All of these mobilities are essential but frequently undervalued, precarious, and unevenly recognized. Whilst questions of accessibility and mobility provision have given rise to new approaches to urban planning, they are even more acute in rural areas, where being mobile is even more a necessity.

In this session we critically explore the intersections of who is mobile, by whom mobility is enabled, and resulting socio-spatial inequalities and injustices. We examine not only mobility practices themselves, but also the hidden power relations, inequalities, and dependencies embedded in the (re-)production of mobility systems. In doing so, the session seeks to contribute to ongoing debates on mobility justice (Sheller, 2018) and mobility poverty (Lucas, 2016) across different spatial scales.

Planned contributions:

1. Wojciech Kębłowski, Monika Maciejewska, Merlin Gillard: Making transport work under material scarcity: lessons from Santiago de Cuba

2.  Amrutha Viswanath: Missing Mobilities: Investigating Last Mile Connectivity to Public Transport in Underserved Neighbourhoods of Shivaji Nagar, Mumbai and Clichy-sous-Bois, Greater Paris Region

3. Dhita de La Roche: From Access to Dependence: Informal Mobility Systems and the Reproduction of Transport Unfairness for Women with Disabilities in Yogyakarta, Indonesia

4. Sarah Davidson: Immobilising the Marginalised: Transport Infrastructure and Mobility Injustice in Belfast

5. Samuel Nello-Deakin, Guillem Viñas Salvador: Exploring the equity of urban traffic fines: Disciplining dangerous or vulnerable transport modes?

paper.title: Immobilising the Marginalised: Transport Infrastructure and Mobility Injustice in Belfast
Missing Mobilities: A Comparative Investigation of Last Mile Connectivity to Public Transport in Underserved Neighbourhoods of Shivaji Nagar, Mumbai and Clichy-sous-Bois, Greater Paris Region

paper_authors.identity_string: Tata Institute of Social Sciences, Mumbai & Sciences Po, Paris (former affiliation), Technical University of Munich (current affiliation)

paper.title: Making transport work under material scarcity: lessons from Santiago de Cuba
Exploring the equity of urban traffic fines: Disciplining dangerous or vulnerable transport modes?
