schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Driven Out: Municipal displacement and the health consequences of urban restructuring
schedule_event.description:
How can we make just places when we start from no place? Displacement is a long-established artefact of urban gentrification and is one that is central to any understanding of health inequalities and urban social justice. Displacement as a process is inimical to any notion of justice, involving the classed removal and reordering of people within and beyond given urban settings. Not only is the process in itself unjust, but notwithstanding its long pedigree in the urban studies literature, it continues to exist as a matter of epistemological injustice for practical reasons. We know much of how places change over time, but we know less of the trajectories of individuals who are displaced from them. This has always been an empirical challenge for research but matters now more than ever for the ways in which an increasingly spatially-polarised country is leading to more and more extreme variants of the phenomenon. Across the western world rolling programmes of austerity under successive governments have hit deindustrialised urban areas hardest whilst (globalised) flows of wealth have continued to residualise in increasingly unliveable and unsustainable core metro-regions. This context is also leading to new forms of long-range displacement as local authorities in London and the south-east look to ‘offload’ unwanted populations elsewhere as surplus urban human capital.

This session will address the manifold and evolving dimensions of displacement, with a focus on the health impacts of various forms of displacement, taking a diversity of stances which will consider both more orthodox short-range and evolving longer-range forms of displacement through both qualitative and quantitative approaches, the contemporary and the historic. We pose the question here: if elective socio-spatial mobility needs to be considered a portable form of capital for the individual, how can we better account for both the personal and collective effects of (forced) displacement on marginalised people and the further marginalised spaces they are always decanted to?

paper.title: Displacement beyond class: Urban class instability as demographic crisis
"People are just being pushed further and further out": displacement, slow violence, and health inequalities in four English coastal towns
“If you can afford to move, you move.” Residential mobility and regional health inequalities in England
Health narratives and territorial stigma in the gentrification of two neighbourhoods in Middlesbrough, UK

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Foodscapes for healthy, just, and sustainable food systems?
schedule_event.description:
Foodscapes is a concept widely used in geography (1) and anthropology (2) to explore relationships between food, people, and place in everyday life (3). While it has generated rich insights into how food is materially situated and socially experienced, its potential contribution to the growing field of food systems science remains underexplored. Food systems science has emerged as an influential interdisciplinary field with a mission to support healthy, sustainable, and just food systems globally (4). However, prevailing approaches often rely on universalising narratives of desirable food systems that privilege particular forms of knowledge, values, and expertise, whilst marginalising others (5). What constitutes a desirable food system, and how problems are framed are deeply situated and context-specific. This session asks whether a foodscapes lens can help navigate the complexity of contemporary food systems by critically examining how food systems are defined, experienced, and valued in place.

A foodscapes framework offers a pluralistic lens for understanding how desirable food systems are negotiated. It foregrounds the intersections between cultural meanings and representations with material and environmental contexts (6). Foodscapes also foreground the meaningful and often reciprocal relationships among humans and between humans and non-humans (7). By examining whose interpretations of sustainability, health, and justice are legitimised, and how these narratives are produced, circulated, and contested, a foodscapes lens enables critical engagement with questions about power, governance, and knowledge (8, 9).

This session invites papers on the foodscapes concept and practice: what analytical value does it offer for understanding food systems; how can it help expose and challenge inequalities embedded in dominant food system narratives; and what possibilities does it open for reimagining sustainable and just food futures? We welcome empirical, conceptual, and methodological papers, and its implications for food systems research, policy, and practice.

paper.title: Inside the box: The lunchbox as a negotiation space for emotional practices of nutrition, parenting, and security

paper_authors.identity_string: Department of Human Geography and Centre for School and Education Research, Martin-Luther-University Halle-Wittenberg

paper.title: The multiple dimensions of urban foodscapes: an analysis of food markets and urban food policy in London

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Cartooneering your research
schedule_event.description:
An opportunity to learn and try out a simple but powerful method for thinking creatively about your research. No drawing skills required – just a willingness to give it a go.

In this workshop, participants will be invited to sketch out their research in cartoon format, aiming to create short stories from their research during the session. The workshop is an interactive opportunity to practice ‘cartooneering’ your research in a fun and engaging manner. There will also be opportunity to reflect on how cartooneering could be used as a tool for planning your research, or for telling the story of your research to a range of audiences. Please bring your own pen/pencil if possible.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Citizen science and co-production as routes to plurality and justice
schedule_event.description:
In this panel we discuss papers that outline and analyse non-extractive research methods to examine innovative forms of research design that better attend to plural knowledges, land-based struggles and community inclusion. We are particularly interested in how principles of citizen science or co-production can “promote inclusion (and) interrogate power relations and hegemonic knowledge systems (Vincent, 2022:730; see also Kesby et al., 2013: Kesby, 2007; Kindon et al., 2007).

In short, we will learn from projects that prioritize collaborative, participatory, co-productive methodological work and seek papers on methods designed to support “self-determination, decolonization and social justice” (Smith, 1999:4).

paper.title: Citizen Science without Citizenship: Co-Production and Environmental Justice in a Refugee Camp

paper_authors.identity_string: Malawi University of Science and Technology (MUST)

paper.title: Digital, analogue, and the creative space in-between: a youth-led multi-method citizen science exploration of nature at night
“Everybody trains, everybody gains”: Exploring homescapes in the urban South
A multi-lens approach to doing and evaluating the co-production of urban climate adaption – experiences from the “future proof neighbourhoods” project

paper_authors.identity_string: Centre for Environmental Policy, Imperial College London
Centre for Environmental Policy, Imperial College London

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Genomic Politics: Precision Breeding, Genetic Engineering, and the Future of a Resilient Food System.
schedule_event.description:
Conveners:

Theo Stanley, University of Southampton

Emma Roe, University of Southampton

Damian Maye, University of Gloucestershire

Panel Abstract

Recent scientific and biotechnological breakthroughs in genomic sequencing, gene editing, and microbial engineering are driving innovation in industrial food systems. Advances have rendered animal and plant genomes more knowable and more manipulable than ever before. Genetic engineering techniques such as CRISPR promise to unlock higher yields, pest and disease resistance, enhanced nutrition, and greater productivity. This session asks:

- What is the potential role of these applied genomic technologies in the food system?

- How might they transform the dynamics and interactions which constitute it?

- What impact might they have on consumption patterns, environmental degradation, public health, and national food system resilience?

Applied genomic technologies are routinely legitimised, naturalised, and normalised through the narratives that they are speeding up “natural” selection and evolution. Genetic engineering is thus framed as a softer and far more politically palatable approach than genetically modified organisms (GMOs) in ethical and regulatory debates. In England (and notably not in Wales and Scotland), the 2023 Precision Breeding Bill distinguishes many organisms produced through precision breeding from GMOs, emphasising that the technology is “a key tool for UK food security”.

This session critically interrogates how genomic technologies reshape food geographies by asking what kinds of harms, risks, vulnerabilities, and opportunities are produced through a growing reliance on genomic technologies. We wish to understand more about the obscurity and visibility of different genomic technologies – including but not only genetic engineering - that are in use or potentially could come into use in different parts of the food system.

We welcome papers on a range of topics, including (but not limited to) issues of: biopolitics, metabolic governance, climate and disease resistance, enzymes, animal feed and supplements, precision breeding, GMOs, biosecurity, and the politics of ‘precision’ in contemporary food systems. We encourage applicants to think about how these themes relate to inequality across human and more-than-human divides.

paper.title: Decoding differently: Gene sequencing and Simondon’s genetic method
Millets, Biofortification, and the Persistence of Nutritionism in Anti-Hunger Politics
Metabolic Resilience in the UK Poultry Industry: A New Frontier in Genomic Biotechnologies
Climate Emergency and the Politics of Speed: Gene Editing in Temporal Perspective

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Leveraging education settings as sites of sustainability transition: Research for Transforming Practice into Policy
schedule_event.description:
Education settings are important sites of action on climate and nature recovery. With over 24,000 schools in England, the educational estate covers a significant area in terms of grounds and built environment, which alongside a student headcount in excess of 9m and staff workforce FTE close to 1m (GOV.UK, 2025) leads to significant environmental impact in terms of carbon emissions and wider impacts, as well as representing a significant potential leverage point for sustainability transition and policy lever for tackling national sustainability priorities.

In England, the Department for Education’s Sustainability and Climate Change strategy, aims to unlock the potential of sustainability transformation of education settings, but this requires support in terms of expertise and resource to the vast majority of the 24,000 schools, plus early years settings and further education colleges across England. The Climate Ambassadors programme, funded by the Department for Education is a critical pillar in the delivery of the DfE’s Sustainability and Climate Change strategy, situated within a complex ecosystem of four other national-scale aligned programmes. The programme supports education settings (from early years through to further education colleges) in England to develop and implement climate action plans through linking support from volunteer ‘climate ambassadors’ with appropriate capacity and expertise, to the needs of individual education settings. Climate action plans take a whole-institution approach focused around the four pillars of Adaptation and resilience, Biodiversity, Climate education and green careers, and Decarbonisation. The programme is centrally co-ordinated and delivered through nine regional hubs, engendering place specific elements of delivery.

Alongside the delivery of the programme, the impact of the programme on education settings and climate ambassadors is being measured, while gaps in setting engagement based on geography, socio-economic characteristics and climate vulnerability and gaps in climate ambassador recruitment and engagement on the basis on geography and expertise are analysed on an ongoing basis. Therefore, in addition to the direct impact of the activities themselves on the settings, their learners and local communities, this programme and the data it is collecting has significant potential to inform future policy and practice on the role of education settings in driving sustainability transitions while tackling inequality.

This panel aims to explore what the Climate Ambassadors programme can contribute to research and policy at the intersection of education settings and action on climate and nature recovery. Some of the questions to be explored include: What is the role of place in engagement with education settings and volunteers? How do localised climate vulnerabilities influence engagement? How do existing inequalities play out in settings’ engagement and impact? Are volunteers a sustainable model for support? How is climate action framed with other agendas and priorities? The audience is invited to contribute to further the research agenda setting at the intersection of practice, research and policy for action on climate and nature recovery through education settings.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - NGOs and Sustainable Fashion Transitions: Challenges, Strategies and Success Stories  (2)
schedule_event.description:
It is well-established that the fashion industry is one of the most environmentally and socially unsustainable industries in the world (Bick et al., 2018; Niinimäki et al., 2020; United Nations Environment Programme, 2023). Despite these impacts being well known, the industry is resistant to change and, with the acceleration of ultra-fast fashion, may be getting worse. While there is an urgent need for change, the question remains: how will this occur?

Transforming the industry requires intervention from multiple levers concurrently, including government legislation, consumer shopping behaviours, manufacturing processes, and brands’ decision making. Non-governmental organisations (NGOs) are frequently positioned as key agents of change due to their ability to operate across scales and engage diverse actors. However this reliance on NGOs warrants critical examination, particularly regarding their capacity, legitimacy, accountability, and potential co-optation within existing power structures.

This session is interested in understanding the strategies and role of NGOs influencing the pro-socio-environmental behaviours that value chain actors (i.e. raw materials producers, manufacturers, brands, consumers, recyclers) are pursuing, including challenges and enablers. For the purpose of this session, NGOs are defined as organisations of any size and prominence, involved in activities which push for positive environmental and social change in the fashion industry such as setting certifications and standards, developing industry schemes, campaigning, and reporting on industry activities. Examples include Textile Exchange, Fashion Revolution, Oeko-Tex, Zero Discharge of Hazardous Chemicals (ZDHC), Oxfam, Clean Clothes Campaign, Fair Wear Foundation, Laudes Foundation, Sustainable Apparel Coalition, and Or Foundation.

In this session, the fashion industry is used as a lens to analyse ethical labour and production, product design and materials, recycling and disposal, and over-consumption. This session brings together research from different contexts to critically examine how NGOs influence socially and environmentally sustainable practices across fashion value chains, including limitations, potentially unintended consequences, and success stories to inspire across geographies. Particular attention is given to how NGOs navigate tensions between collaboration and confrontation, voluntary standards and regulatory change, and incremental improvements versus systemic industry transformation.

paper.title: Sustainability Intelligence (SI): NGOs as Systemic Enablers of Sustainable Fashion Business Transformation – A Case Study of a Netherlands-based Denim Industry Collective
Stitching for Change: Negotiating Power and Livelihoods through NGO-led Skills Upgrading in India’s Informal Fashion/Garment Sector
Governing fashion sustainably: What hope for NGOs?

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Making Home Over Time: How does time and temporality reshape our understanding of home?
schedule_event.description:
Home is often seen as a stable place, yet it is an unfinished project made through time (Brickell, 2012). Homemaking unfolds through rhythms and routines, life-course transitions, and the shifting work of attachment and repair long emphasised in critical geographies of home (Boccagni, 2014; Merla & Smit, 2023). However, this domestic time is increasingly fractured. It is shaped by migration temporalities that stretch, suspend, or intensify family life across borders (Walsh, 2011; Li, 2025), and by housing trajectories that connect lives across unequal spaces (Brickell et al., 2024; Born et al., 2025). Increasingly, domestic time is mediated by digital systems, from smart home technologies (Gram-Hanssen & Darby, 2018) to rental platforms (Maalsen & Gurran, 2022). These temporal processes shape who can settle, stay, move, or reunite, and under what conditions home can be sustained.

This session asks: What does it mean to treat time not merely as a backdrop to homemaking, but as a key medium through which home is made and governed? How do housing infrastructures and bordering practices produce uneven temporal conditions for dwelling (e.g., conditions of waiting, interruption, repetition, or acceleration), and how are these temporalities lived, contested, and negotiated in everyday homemaking?

We use time-making as a broad lens and welcome papers that connect domestic temporal experience to wider political-economic, legal, infrastructural, and technological formations. We also welcome contributions that extend the discussion through life-course perspectives and emerging work on platformed/algorithmic infrastructures. We seek to convene presentations and discussions surrounding:

  • Lifecourse, Age, and Intergenerational Homemaking
  • Home as Temporality: Rhythms, Ruptures, and Domestic Time
  • Migration, Mobility, and Distributed Homemaking
  • Hope, Futures, and the Temporal Politics of Homemaking
  • Affective, Gendered, and Embodied Home Spaces

paper.title: 'Sailing' through Precarity: Home-making of Single Chinese Female Professionals in Tokyo and Shenzhen
How overseas returned talents negotiate multiple identities in everyday home-making in Shanghai China?

paper_authors.identity_string: East China University of Science and Techonology

paper.title: 'Home' at the dinner table: Food and the simultaneity of homes
Home is where the hope is: a Photovoice study uncovering women’s experiences of homelessness in London
Between Tides and Clock Time: Reconfiguring Domestic Temporality among Boat-Dwellers’ Resettlement in Coastal China

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Out of Office: Geographies of Leave
schedule_event.description:
While paid and unpaid working lives have long been a central concern within Geography (Bürkner and Lange 2020, Dutta 2016, Herod 1997, Strauss 2020), periods of absence from paid work remain comparatively under-examined. Yet leave is a critical site through which attachments to work are made, challenged, and reconfigured, particularly in neoliberal contexts where paid employment is often closely tied to identity, productivity, and self-worth (Jones 2025, Pettit 2024, Thieme 2017). Periods of leave often involve complex transitions into and out of working life, the renegotiation of temporal and spatial boundaries, and contested expectations around care, rest, recovery, and responsibility.

Geographers have explored work through lenses of labour, care, precarity, temporality, and everyday life (Bissell 2022, Carswell 2016, Marotta 2021, Richardson 2018, Worth 2016). Building on this work, this session foregrounds leave as a distinctive analytical focus, attending to how time away from work is lived, (under)valued, and spatio-temporally organised. Drawing particularly on feminist and economic geographies, we seek contributions that attend to the uneven distribution of leave, the gendered and relational labour it entails, and its entanglement with labour markets, welfare regimes, and household economies.

This session explores the relationship between paid work and leave, broadly framed as periods of time away from paid employment. Leave is taken here to encompass a wide range of experiences, including maternity, paternity and adoption leave, sickness absence, bereavement leave, furlough, unemployment, retirement and un-retirement, and the spatio-temporal limitations placed around work and leave by migrant visas.

paper.title: “Out of Rhythm” – An Exploration of the Disorientation Felt by Workers Transitioning Into and Out of Leave
“Leave not more than 180-days outside the UK”: An Autoethnography of Maternity Leave on a Skilled Worker Visa
Between Many Worlds: Precarious Positionalities Out of Office
Work, Identities, Transition: feminist geographies of parental leave

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Podcasting for Urban Studies
schedule_event.description:
Podcasting is an increasingly common form of audio media in popular culture and media alike. Urban researchers and educators have made clear calls for attention to this medium over the past decade. This Urban Studies-sponsored roundtable convenes a variety of established and new initiatives putting podcasting at the heart of urban research and dissemination. Building on experiences like City Road, the Urban Radar, USJ’s Walking the City With… or The Urban Political, the roundtable reflects on the challenges and opportunities for podcasting to advance and shape our (inter)disciplinary futures. Can we formalise podcasting as method, reading it as input and format in scholarship production not just dissemination? Could we revalue slow podcasting, and is there still time for “thick time” podcasting in today’s ever fast academia? Should podcasting be institutionalised or stand independently of stringent academic structures? To unpack these urban podcast hosts go behind the scenes of their experiences, dialogues and innovations.

Participants:

Michele Acuto (University of Bristol, chair); Caitlin Morrissey (University of Manchester); Beth Perry (University of Sheffield and Urban Radar); Ayona Datta (University College London); Tom Goodfellow (University of Manchester and Urban Radar); Dallas Rogers (University of Sydney and City Road Podcast); Hanna Hilbrandt (University of Zurich and Urban Political).

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Platform tourism and the making of home: reproductive labour, domestic imaginaries and digital lives
schedule_event.description:
Short-term rental platforms represent one of the most consequential tourism phenomena over the last two decades. Research has linked platforms such as Airbnb, VRBO, and Booking.com to a range of socio-spatial inequalities, particularly affecting housing affordability and availability. However, many aspects of temporary renting are often neglected, particularly those that affect its impact on the institution of home and reproductive labour.

Short-term rental platforms are a multiscalar and home-making phenomenon that actively shape domestic spaces. These practices engage emotions, values, and discourses related to home, with particular emphasis on the gendered organisation of everyday life. They reflect a fantasy of “being at home” worldwide and commodify homely experiences and atmospheres. More research is needed to understand how platforms influence perceptions of home, inhabitation and reproductive labour.

The session examines the intersection of short-term rental platforms with labour, the home and the everyday lives of those who inhabit them. It explores how digital platforms mediate private spheres and shape domestic imaginaries. Drawing on feminist and digital geographies, it aims to extend critical scholarship on short-term rental platforms.

paper.title: When Homes Become Hotels: Platform Economies and the Material Transformation of Domestic Spaces
Platform Tourism and the Making of Home in Central São Paulo - Brazil
‘Don’t forget, you are guests’: Airbnb, the homely and the ‘uncanny’.
Negotiating Hospitality and Boundary Work in the Airbnb Home

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Teaching migration in the contemporary world
schedule_event.description: With point of departure in the recently published book Migration: A Critical Introduction (published by Wiley (2026), as part of the Critical Geographies series), this panel brings together scholars to discuss the importance and challenges of teaching migration in the contemporary world. This panel provides a critical conversation about the challenges we face as educators interested in the geographies of migration, how those challenges have persisted or changed, and our strategies for responding to those challenges in the classroom and beyond. In doing so, this panel will explore critical geographical approaches to teaching migration and related topics including but not limited to borders, belonging, diaspora, displacement, global climate change, labor, and migration justice.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Shadow Geographies of Care and Migration
schedule_event.description:
We are living through intensely un-caring times. And yet, perhaps in reaction to this, we are seeing increased interest in the concept and practice of care across disciplines.

In relation to migration, research on care has tended to focus on care work by migrants. However care as a ‘species activity’ (Fisher and Tronto 1990) extends beyond migrants working as carers. It involves a range of practices that create interconnections across different scales, concerned with caring about, caring for, caregiving and care receiving (Tronto 1998), and with questions of “how the world ought to be”, and “how we could imagine our shared world through radical ethics of care” (Raghuram 2016:514; Clark-Kazak 2022).

While anti-immigrant activity tends to be highly visible, much care of people on the move, by both migrants themselves and by non-migrants, happens in the shadows, for reasons of safety or legality, or simply through quiet and everyday ways of countering hostile environments and policies. We are interested in these hidden or ‘shadow’ geographies of care, the often invisible, and sometimes extra-legal, forms of care developed by migrants themselves, and by individuals, groups and organisations that support them in various ways.

We are also interested in work which takes into account the ‘plurality of care knowledges’ (Sheringham 2025), challenging understandings of care, beyond western or European norms, or rethinking what care means for different people. We would like to hear from scholars researching in this area, but also activists, practitioners and artists who are working with/challenging ideas of care and migration.

paper.title: Creating space, holding care: fostering solidarity and connection through creative encounters with refugees and people seeking asylum
Negotiating, navigating and circulating care within pathways of inclusion in Scotland
Sharing Food, Building Belonging: Food-centred Methodologies in Understanding Belonging and Care in Migrant Provision Spaces
Quiet Resistances: Migrant Women’s Care Practices and Counter-Infrastructures in the City

paper_authors.identity_string: Urban Studies Foundation Research Fellow, Department of Geography, University of Manchester

paper.title: MobiliseCare: Reframing the migration debate through building a ‘care-tography’

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Scaling Conservation, Scaling Injustice? Power, Values, and Equity in Expanding Nature-based Credit Market Interventions
schedule_event.description:
Nature-based credit markets have been positioned as a solution to financing conservation interventions needed to meet the fast-approaching targets of the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework and the Paris Agreement (Swinfield et al., 2024; IPCC, 2023; CBD, 2022). Nature-based credits are quantified units of ecosystem services (e.g. carbon) that can be sold in voluntary or compliance markets. Their rapid expansion - often associated with the commodification of nature, standardisation of land governance and management approaches, and enduring colonial legacies - raises pressing questions about equity, power, and justice (Milner-Gulland, 2024; McAfee, 2023; Chausson et al., 2023).

This session explores the trade-offs between urgency, scalability, and justice in nature-based credit markets. We ask: Whose values, knowledge, and priorities shape how these markets are designed and implemented? Can conservation be scaled equitably through credit-based mechanisms? If credits fall short, what alternative tools and mechanisms might help address the climate and biodiversity crises within the constraints of current political and economic systems?

Focusing on the current evidence, the first half of the session draws on academic presentations and critical provocations that present:

(i) empirical case studies (including multi-scalar analyses and first-hand practitioner accounts) that explore trade-offs and synergies between scaling conservation through credit-based mechanisms and advancing social justice and equity.

(ii) reflections on adjustments to, or alternatives beyond, nature-based credits that seek to redistribute power, embed equity, and sustain place-based justice. This may include adaptations to the design of credit-based mechanisms (e.g. community-owned carbon projects and locally defined monitoring frameworks), as well as other market-based instruments (e.g. green bonds) and non-market or redistributive approaches (e.g. rights of nature, unconditional cash transfers).

These contributions will inform a facilitated discussion during the second half of the session. Speakers and participants will collectively reflect on the cases presented, comparing experiences across contexts and critically assessing the potential and limitations of proposed adjustments and alternatives to credit-based markets. The aim is to create a shared space to explore common patterns and design challenges, and to identify priority areas for future research and practice.

Discussion themes may include:

  • How credit-based interventions reshape rights and power relations among actors, and how the associated risks and responsibilities are distributed as these interventions expand (e.g. among NGOs, governments, local communities, Indigenous Peoples, businesses, and financial institutions).
  • How standards, metrics, and monitoring systems reconfigure what counts as “good” conservation, including whose values and forms of knowledge are prioritised.
  • How different strategies for “working at scale” influence equity in recognition, decision-making, and benefit-sharing, including the role of governance arrangements (e.g. centralised versus networked) and implementation strategies (e.g. replicating standard models versus adapting to local contexts).
  • What political, legal, and institutional conditions are necessary for credit-based (or other market-based) mechanisms to avoid reinforcing existing power asymmetries.
  • Under what conditions non-market or redistributive approaches may be able to deliver at the scale and speed implied by the urgency of the biodiversity and climate targets.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Social and Cultural Geographies in Policy and Practice: A Practical Workshop
schedule_event.description:
In November 2025, the Social and Cultural Geographies Research Group (SCGRG) held an online event to explore the translation of academic social and cultural geography into policy and practice. This workshop at AC2026 seeks to continue this conversation, inviting participants to respond to the reflections that emerged from our November event and contribute their own. No experience of having worked at the policy or practice interface is required in order to participate, and you don’t need to have attended the first event; rather, our aim in facilitating this ongoing conversation is to distil key guidance for social and cultural geographers seeking to engage policymakers or practitioners in future.

This workshop will incorporate a World Café in which participants will be invited to review and comment on the guidance emerging from the conversation so far. Participants will also have the opportunity to apply their learning from the session by planning their own preliminary steps in engaging with policy or practitioner audiences. Our aim as session convenors is to learn from, and with, participants in order to inform development of resources to support social and cultural geographers at all career stages with policy and practitioner engagement.

Workshop discussion will cover topics including:

  • How to begin thinking about how to connect your research with policy or practice
  • How to bridge methodological divides between contemporary geographical research and external contexts with different epistemological cultures
  • How your research might play a constructive part in critiquing policy or practice, or challenging its processes or parameters, in ways that might ultimately improve them
  • How to ‘speak the language’ of policy and practice

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Twinning European Cities
schedule_event.description:
The development of ‘digital twins’ of objects and processes is emerging as a key practice in the current phase of the digital revolution. In the urban context, this trend has given rise to Urban Digital Twins (UDTs), aimed at representing, modelling and simulating entire cities. UDTs mark a new phase in discourses around smart cities, technology-driven urbanism, and AI-based technological solutionism.

In the European context, a distinct UDT agenda is emerging, often emphasizing novel opportunities for participation, transparency, and reliability in urban decision-making, envisioned to reduce the gap between technical expertise and citizenship. In practice, research has shown how UDTs emerge within specific local contexts and techno-political trajectories, being entangled with specific geographies of power relationships.

This call seeks to critically engage with these implications by gathering case studies of European UDTs through the prism of the conference’s theme of geographies of inequalities. Where and how do UDTs produce (in-)equalities? How do the topics of inequality and injustice look like through the lenses of the various different disciplines that have formed the UDT field? How can responsible European UDTs that actively take these aspects into account be designed in practice? We welcome both theories and practices related to UDTs; these examples should not only be considered from a technological point of view but be connected to the political, social, and ecological issues related to their development.

This panel includes contributions on European UDTs highlighting the multiple aspects that contribute to their design and implementation (technology, governance, politics, ecology, city branding, urban economics, among others) and from different perspectives, including but not limited to:

  • Urban Geography
  • Science and Technology Studies
  • Urban Planning
  • Computational Social Sciences
  • Philosophy of Technology
  • Urban Sociology

Amoore, L. (2025). Because the twin is not a copy: On the politics of digital twins. new media & society, 27(8), 4549-4564.

Ferré-Bigorra, J., Casals, M., & Gangolells, M. (2022). The adoption of urban digital twins. Cities, 131, 103905.

Kitchin, R., & Dawkins, O. (2025). Digital twins and deep maps. Transactions of the Institute of British Geographers, 50(1), e12699.

Knopf, S., Macq, H., & Wentland, A. (2025). Urban futures in the mirror of technology? The politics of urban digital twins. new media & society, 27(8), 4385-4401.

Nochta, T., Wan, L., Schooling, J. M., & Parlikad, A. K. (2021). A socio-technical perspective on urban analytics: The case of city-scale digital twins. Journal of urban technology, 28(1-2), 263-287.

paper.title: European Digital Twins on a Global Stage: Insights from Munich and Beyond
A Twin out of Control. The Case of Bologna Digital Twin
Reconstructing Urban Realities Through the City’s Digital Replica: The Politics of Representing and Reproducing Urban Digital Twins
Policy in Silico: Urban Digital Twins and the Governance of Just Health Futures

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - The Game: Counter-mapping Informal Refugee Mobilities along the Balkan Route (1)
schedule_event.description:
This panel session brings together contributions from the ERC-project “The Game: Counter-mapping Informal Refugee Mobilities along the Balkan Route” to present its preliminary findings and foster critical discussion and exchange. The project constitutes the first transnational, multi-sited, and extensive qualitative study of the so-called "Balkan Route", which is the most important overland informal migration corridor in Europe, taken by thousands of refugees every year. Linking Greece to Western Europe, across Bulgaria, Romania, Albania, North Macedonia, Kosovo, Montenegro, Serbia, Bosnia-Herzegovina, Slovenia, and Croatia, the Route is a complex geography of formal and informal, visible and invisible sites, an assemblage of diverse actors, camps, borders, violence, solidarity, and a multiplicity of micro-routes, constantly adapting and shifting. Across and between these interconnected spatialities, refugees forge their trajectories towards Europe through "The Game", the term they use to refer to their clandestine journeys and border crossings. Through The Game, refugees themselves produce a counter-geography that consists of a constellation of strategically organised refugee spatialities and imaginative geographies, marked by the hopes and ambition of seeking a better life in Europe as well as by the pain and fear experienced through perilous journeys. Across the precarious, ambivalent, and ever-changing circumstances of the Balkan Route, an archipelago of formal and makeshift camps has emerged, hosting thousands of refugees as they make repeated attempts to enter the EU. Key in producing and sustaining The Game, these sites serve as temporary shelters, nodes of services and information, where refugees meet smugglers, wait, and plan the next move.

Drawing on the project team’s rich, multi-sited ethnographic research, this panel session interrogates the complex counter-geographies of the Balkan Route by investigating its distinct spatialities, archipelago of camps, informal mobilities, ambivalent legal geographies, and border geopolitics.

The panel is organised into two sessions. The first session brings together contributors from the edited volume “Thinking Like a Route: Counter-geographies of Informal Migration in the Balkans”, who will reflect on their chapters while outlining the subsequent developments of their research. The second session features empirically grounded presentations by four researchers of the project conducting fieldwork at key junctures along the Balkan Route, including Austria, Greece, North Macedonia, and Slovenia.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - The Game: Counter-mapping Informal Refugee Mobilities along the Balkan Route (2)
schedule_event.description:
This panel session brings together contributions from the ERC-project “The Game: Counter-mapping Informal Refugee Mobilities along the Balkan Route” to present its preliminary findings and foster critical discussion and exchange. The project constitutes the first transnational, multi-sited, and extensive qualitative study of the so-called “Balkan Route”, which is today the most important overland informal migration corridor in Europe, taken by thousands of refugees every year. Linking Greece to Western Europe, across Bulgaria, Romania, Albania, North Macedonia, Kosovo, Montenegro, Serbia, Bosnia-Herzegovina, Slovenia, and Croatia, the Route is a complex geography of formal and informal, visible and invisible sites; an assemblage of diverse actors, camps, borders, violence, solidarity; and a multiplicity of micro-routes, constantly adapting and shifting. Across and between these interconnected spatialities, refugees forge their trajectories towards Europe through The Game, the term they use to refer to their clandestine journeys and border crossings. Through The Game, refugees themselves produce a counter-geography that consists of a constellation of strategically organised refugee spatialities and imaginative geographies, marked by the hopes and ambitions of seeking a better life in Europe as well as by the pain and fear experienced through perilous journeys. Across the precarious, ambivalent, and ever-changing circumstances of the Balkan Route, an archipelago of formal and makeshift camps has emerged, hosting thousands of refugees as they make repeated attempts to enter the EU. Key in producing and sustaining The Game, these sites serve as temporary shelters, nodes of services and information, where refugees meet smugglers, wait, and plan the next move.

Drawing on the project team’s rich, multi-sited ethnographic research, this panel session interrogates the complex counter-geographies of the Balkan Route by investigating its distinct spatialities, archipelago of camps, informal mobilities, ambivalent legal geographies, and border geopolitics.

The panel is organised into two sessions. The first session brings together contributors from the edited volume “Thinking Like a Route: Counter-geographies of Informal Migration in the Balkans”, who will reflect on their chapters while outlining the subsequent developments of their research. The second session features empirically grounded presentations by four researchers of the project conducting fieldwork at key junctures along the Balkan Route, including Austria, Greece, North Macedonia, and Slovenia.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Reimagining Water Geographies (1)
schedule_event.description:
Water is routinely invoked as a universal, connective substance. Yet the ways it is narrated and conceptualised in geography can also reproduce exclusion, erasure, and spatial injustice.

In this session, we will discuss how critical geography might turn the tide by thinking with water as relation, medium, and more-than-human material force, while staying attentive to uneven power, deterritorialization, and colonial legacies. Contributions will revolve around works that reconnect conversations often kept apart (island/ocean studies and freshwater scholarship; maritime geopolitics and hydro-feminist approaches), and that develop concepts and methods capable of tracking entanglements across scales and watery environments.

Participants will rework key concepts such as planetary waters, islands, coasts, basins, and watery bodies. We will engage with “water places” where injustice is produced or contested (infrastructure, extraction, conservation, borders, mobility/immobility, militarization, tourism, and disasters), presenting methods for analyzing water as relation: fresh–salt continuums, land–sea interfaces, multi-sited approaches, mapping/visual methods, more-than-human methodologies, and more. Finally, by exploring the plural material dimensions of water, its essentiality in human-made environments, and its relationship with infrastructure and climate change adaptation, we will address compelling dilemmas in epistemic politics: how water categories travel, whom they privilege, and what alternative, decolonial geographies they enable.

paper.title: Liquid hyperplaces: Flateyri and Þingeyri, uneven resilience trajectories in the Westfjords of Iceland

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Dialogues in Human Geography Conversation Forum: The psyche, sense and the psychoanalytic: a conversation about the self in geographic thought.
schedule_event.description: How is the ‘self’ being thought in contemporary human geography? Somewhat vague perhaps, indistinct, fuzzy, a bit bewildered? Between now established work on embodiment, more recent moves to affect and emotion, and a resurgence of interest in psychoanalysis, questions of the self, of self-hood, of the psyche - of what is denoted with the adjectives ‘I’ or ‘you’ - are once again open questions. Yet there is a long tradition of engaging with these issues outside our contemporary frames, including through psychologism, humanism, phenomenology, and of course traditional metaphysics. The aim of this panel is to foster a conversation on the different ways in which the psyche might be approached, deliberated and perhaps even defined. Is the psyche a locus-point of conscious and unconscious thought? A site of motivation and instinct? A substrate of perception? An ephemeral effect of inhuman forces? A focus of affective of emotive response? Bringing together scholars in different conceptual traditions, the panel seeks to explore what the psyche is or could be, and how different framings can potentially shape our understanding of the human, human spatiality and community in geography.

paper.title: Psychoanalysis, Geography and the Psyche
Elemental phenomenologies
On cringing and creatureliness
Co-constituting expanded selves: affects and others

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Chris Philo's Geographies: A Reflection and Celebration
schedule_event.description: This panel reflects on and celebrates the work of Chris Philo, marking his retirement this year. Chris's work has made central contributions to a range of geographical questions and concerns. Over several decades, he has produced pivotal work across social and cultural geography, historical geography, rural geography, children's geography, animal geography, carceral geography, population geography, the geographies of madness and mental health, and the history of geography, as well as key contributions to how we work with thinkers from Foucault to Adorno. He has been a passionate advocate for geography as a discipline and has exemplified a spirit of intellectual generosity and curiosity. The panel brings together a range of geographers to reflect on and celebrate different aspects of his work and contributions.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Towards Geographies of the Middle (1)
schedule_event.description:
This session explores the middle as a productive, open, and under-theorised conceptual vantage point in geography. It emerges in dialogue with recent calls in the geographies of ageing literature to attend more closely to the middle (Barron, 2025) and middle-age by extension (Lulle, 2024). However, the middle is pertinent far beyond age and ageing literature. This session takes this insight as a starting point, opening the conversation to ‘the middle’ in all its forms and to all (inter)disciplinary vantage points.

The middle appears in social classifications (mid-career, medium-educated, middle-class) and in spatial logics of scale, proportion, and measurement. It is also profoundly processual. To be in the middle of something (a journey, a text, a negotiation, a household task) is to inhabit unfolding processes, unfinished projects, and open present horizons. The middle is the thick present in which pasts accumulate and futures are actively crafted.

Theoretically, scholars have often approached the middle indirectly, through concepts such as liminality, waiting, impasse, or transition, foregrounding affective states and moments of change. Methodologically, too, the middle is ever-present but rarely named. Research turns occur mid-project; insights emerge through interruption. Fieldwork, analysis, and writing repeatedly unfold in unsettled mid-states that shape knowledge production in important ways.

Our aim is to think critically and creatively about what a geography of the middle might look like. We invite abstracts that explore any geography of the middle, including (but not limited to):

  • Life-course and biographical middles, including midlife and middle age, and approaches that think through the middle to reframe youth or later life
  • Social, political, and classed middles, such as centrism and political ambivalence, partial citizenship, precarity, mobility, and being ‘caught in the middle’
  • Transitional and processual middles, including gender transitions, decarbonisation and socio-technical change, recovery, waiting, and suspension
  • Affective and relational middles, such as ambivalence, burnout, care responsibilities, sandwich generations, and negotiated belonging
  • Everyday and temporal middles, such as midweek, midday, mid-career, and other rhythms of the ‘thick present’
  • Methodological middles, including research conducted mid-project, through interruption, partial findings, or reorientation

References:

Barron, A. (2025). Making sense of ‘middle-age’: thinking from and through the middle. Social & Cultural Geography, pp.1-20.

Lulle, A. (2024). Midlife Geographies: Changing lifecourses across generations, spaces and time. Bristol. Bristol University Press.

paper.title: Making sense of ‘middle-age’: thinking from and through the middle
Menopause and the Geographies of the Middle: Everyday Experiences of Reproductive Ageing
Bodies in the Middle: the menopause as a socio-spatial experience
Affective Futurity of Middle Age:  Strength Training as Everyday Futurity-Work and Anticipated Ageing

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Governing Transitions, Distributing Consequences: Locating Institutional (In)justice in Food Systems (1)
schedule_event.description:
Food geographies have generated rich insights into the spatial politics of production, consumption, and alternative food networks. Food systems scholarship, meanwhile, has advanced understanding of interconnections across scales and sectors. Yet both literatures tend to concentrate analytical attention at the "ends" of food systems, whether on farming landscapes and agrarian change or on ‘the food environment’ (retail environments, households, and eating practices). This session turns attention to the spaces in between: the processing facilities, distribution networks, wholesale markets, and public provisioning systems that constitute the "missing middle" of food system governance. These are places where (in)justice takes shape, yet they remain underexamined as sites of geographical inquiry.

We invite contributions that examine how inequalities are produced, reproduced, and contested in these institutional spaces, attending to both the process and outcomes of food system transformation. How are transformation pathways governed, and whose interests shape them? And crucially, what happens after? Even transformations pursued in the name of justice generate consequences: shifts in where food is produced and processed, new winners and losers across global supply networks, and reconfigured relations between humans, animals, and environments. How are these consequences distributed across places, species, and countries, and how might governance arrangements anticipate and address the new inequalities that just transitions themselves produce?

We particularly welcome contributions that extend justice frameworks beyond the human while remaining structurally and institutionally grounded. Rather than focusing on ethical consumption or community-based multispecies encounters, we seek analyses of how human-animal-environment relations and multispecies futures are shaped by institutional and non-human factors. Topics might include: public food procurement; wholesale, processing, and distribution governance; lock-in and path dependency; transition governance and its distributive effects; institutional arrangements for animal welfare; and anticipatory approaches to just transition.

paper.title: Historical transitions and anticipated futures for live bird chicken trading in India
Delivering One Health in Wales Through Food Systems in Transition:  Infrastructure, Wholesalers, and Following The Money
Sustainability in the broiler chicken industry: trade-offs, lock-in, and path dependency
Justice in the Missing Middle: Transformation Statements and Institutional (In)justice in Papua New Guinea’s Cocoa Supply Chain

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Author meets critics: Gwyneth Lonergan's Borders, Citizenship, and Pregnancy: Migrant women’s experiences of pregnancy and maternity care in the UK
schedule_event.description:
Borders, Citizenship, and Pregnancy explores migrant women’s experiences of pregnancy and maternity care in the north of England, and what these experiences reveal about the relationship between reproduction, citizenship, and bordering processes at this juncture. Based on interviews and focus groups with migrant mothers, third sector workers, and NHS staff, this book uses the analytical framework of reproductive justice to examine how racialised and gendered discourses and policies around health, austerity, belonging, motherhood, in conjunction with the expanding border regime, interact to create a highly stratified and complex system of exclusions, contributing to migrant women’s relatively poor maternal outcomes.

It has long been recognised that bordering processes are informed by anxieties around migrants’ reproductive practices, and serve to discipline these practices (c.f. Klug, 1989; Luíbheid 2013). For the nation-state to survive, citizens must reproduce themselves both biologically, by giving birth to the next generation of citizens; and socially, by inculcating their children with the values of ‘good citizenship.’ Anxieties around the national reproductive future shape which migrants are seen as un/desirable, and the conditions under which migrants are allowed to enter and remain in the UK, and access resources and state services. These anxieties target women especially, because as women are constructed as responsible for reproducing the nation-state (Yuval-Davis, 1997); moreover, because citizenship is hierarchical and multi-tiered, the impact of these policies vary considerably depending on the social location of individual women, and their perceived ability to reproduce the nation-state ‘correctly.’ However, the recent proliferation, multiplication, and intensification of bordering processes has strengthened the reach of the state in policing these practices, and new forms of reproductive injustice are emerging as a consequence. At the same time, as bordering processes expand to multiple sites, they encounter, and may be shaped, mediated, or even undermined by the practices and philosophies associated with these sites, and new possibilities for resistance may emerge.

This book breaks new ground in considering migrant women’s experiences of pregnancy as shaped by citizenship and bordering processes, and arguing that reproduction must be seen as central to expanding border regimes. In this panel, leading scholars of the geographies of bordering processes and/or reproduction will engage with the author in a discussion of Borders, Citizenship, and Pregnancy, and the ways in which bordering processes are informed by, and impact, discourses, policies, and experiences around pregnancy and reproduction, and the resulting patterns of injustice.

Panel members include:

Dr Gwyneth Lonergan, Northumbria University

Professor Jen Bagelman, Newcastle University

Professor Kathryn Cassidy, Northumbria University

Professor Sarah Marie Hall, University of Manchester

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Epistemic Injustice: Geographical Data and the Urban Pedagogy
schedule_event.description:
This panel examines the implication of justice in the circulation of the knowledge in urban space; focusing particularly on the gathering, processing, archiving, and teaching of data. It asks: What is the role of epistemic justice in the creative urban pedagogies toward the just city? With the everchanging geographical landscape of economic, socio-political, and environmental conditions affecting urban life, ‘data’ has an inevitable role in how urban life is lived, governed and taught. Nonetheless, persistent questions remain about how ‘data’ is utilised in geographical and urban practices, such as defined and produced (by experts, institutions, algorithms, residents), taught and circulated (through curricula, maps, policy documents), stored and archived (what is preserved, forgotten, or erased), and mobilised and contested (for planning, governance, advocacy, or resistance).

Several layers of questions arise when working with ‘data’ in practice. Data can be described as inherently biased; however, it can also be argued that data is a formless alienated object awaiting human interpretation, while human interpreters are themselves inevitably biased. How then can more representative, less biased, and pluralistic approaches to data collection be achieved? How can data be processed and incorporated into policy making without undermining local wisdom, empirical data, experiential knowledge, and embodied mappings? How can human emotions and intangible motivations be archived without rendering them static and fixed? In the educator–student relationship, on what basis do you trust educators to ‘know better’? Whose data is collected, heard, preserved, and taught? How do systematic exclusions along lines of gender, age, race, nationality, and shifting judgments about what counts as credible or verifiable knowledge shape this?

This enquiry is also challenging the way we see justice. What is the limitation of justice itself? And what does epistemic justice truly mean in an urban space? Is genuine democratised and pluralised data even achievable? Can we imagine a world with an alternative way of seeing data and/or without data? Concerning Epistemic Injustice in an Urban Context, this panel seeks a deeper discussion on the temporality, practicality, and limitation of data gathering, processing, and application. It invites geographers and beyond to rethink how our practices engage with the non-tangible and invisible elements of the data, and how the nature of such data shapes their ways of seeing justice.

paper.title: Counting What Shapes the City: Capital-Flow Data, Assetized Streets, and the Politics of Enclosure
Shaping Just Cities through Digital Urban Heritage Narratives: Mobilising Local Knowledge under Epistemic Constraints
From Heterotopia to Utopian Order: Epistemic Construction and Exclusion in the Urban Public Art of Putrajaya and Kuala Lumpur

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Just Urban Transitions: Geographies of Inequalities and Governance Pathways to Sustainable Futures (1)
schedule_event.description:
Urban sustainability transitions are urgent, uneven, and deeply political (Goh, 2021; Gonçalves et al., 2025). As cities are increasingly reframed from sites of environmental degradation to key arenas for climate mitigation and adaptation (Parnell, 2016), governments and communities face profound challenges in balancing ecological limits with socio-spatial justice and the creation of liveable urban futures (Kaufmann et al., 2024). These challenges are particularly acute in cities shaped by entrenched inequality, informality, and infrastructural precarity, where climate crises intersect with longer histories of colonial dispossession and uneven development.

This session critically engages with the concept and practice of Just Urban Transitions. While the term “just transition” emerged from labour struggles linked to energy transitions (McCauley & Heffron, 2018), a growing body of scholarship calls for a more expansive urban lens that foregrounds the full range of resources, services, and opportunities required for urban life, including water, sanitation, housing, mobility, and land (Hughes & Hoffmann, 2020; Maboye et al., 2025).

Bringing together empirically grounded and conceptually rich contributions, the session examines how just urban transitions are co-produced, governed, contested and lived across diverse urban contexts. Particular attention is given to the governance dilemmas and “complex trade-offs” (van Aswegen & Matamanda, 2025) that emerge as climate mitigation and adaptation efforts intersect with existing forms of precarity and privilege. The session highlights how decision-making draws on both political and technical forms of knowledge across multiple levels and actors, shaping access to basic services and urban opportunities.

Centred on cities, particularly in the Global South, the session explores urban spaces as complex and dynamic arenas where governance is enacted through state and non-state actors, and where crises of energy, water, housing, employment and mobility are deeply entangled.

Themes addressed include governance and multi-scalar coordination; justice in climate adaptation and mitigation; everyday practices, informality and hybrid infrastructures; methodological innovations; and rethinking metrics and imaginaries of sustainable urban futures.

paper.title: Growth Before Justice? Economic Rationales and Urban Climate Governance in Sri Lanka
Governing a Just Urban Transition
Negotiating informality and urban resilience: implications for equity

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Author meets critics: Thomas Dekeyser's Techno-Negative: A Long History of Refusing the Machine
schedule_event.description:
This panel is an Author Meets Critics session focused on Thomas Dekeyser's recent book, Techno-Negative: A Long History of Refusing the Machine (2026, University of Minnesota Press). See the book's description below. 

A radical history of technology told through acts of resistance, not progress

A radical history of technology told through acts of resistance, not progressThe history of technology is often told as a history of progress, moving optimistically and inevitably from one emancipatory invention to the next. Techno-Negative turns this story on its head, taking us on a journey to the critical junctures where people have pointedly rejected and tried to undo, rather than adopt, new technologies. Beginning with Archimedes’s decision to destroy his own war machines, this book explores the will to negate technology as a deep—but persistently condemned—current in history.

As he presents a new theory of technological power, Thomas Dekeyser argues that technologies, never neutral, operate as “ontological policing,” drawing the boundaries of humanness as they are unequally leveraged by select groups. Looking beyond the Luddites to medieval monks banning tools, seventeenth-century loom burners, revolutionary lantern smashers, and computer arsonists, Dekeyser shows how people have long recognized and resisted the machine as a violent, sometimes deadly force implicated in defining who counts as human and whose lives (and ways of life) are worth saving.

Against the ubiquitous demands to reform or accelerate technological “advancement” that have failed to disrupt our present, Dekeyser proposes a spirited alternative: abolition. He challenges us to rethink the terms of our technological present and future. In a time when Big Tech grows increasingly enmeshed with authoritarian control, Techno-Negative is a conceptual declaration, and source of inspiration, for those searching for a new paradigm of technological politics.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Unconventional Geographies of Solidarity (1): Ephemeral Alliances
schedule_event.description:
In Multitude (2004), political philosophers Michael Hardt and Antonio Negri proposed the possibility of the “multitude” as a “living alternative” that grows from within the biopolitical production of shared knowledges, relationships, sentiments, and collaborations that facilitates globalized networks of capitalism. Unlike political units of “the people,” “the masses”, and “the classes” which signify the possibility of organized collective action based on commonality (of race, history, economic status etc.), the “multitude” is constituted through difference; an elusive, unpredictable and fundamentally ungovernable collective of singularities that may synchronize temporarily for specific goals or movements (cultural, social, political or otherwise), but without ever becoming fully unified and/or articulable.

Twenty years later, the world facilitated by globalized networks like social media is ever more complex than when they wrote this book. At the same time as people across the world have become intensely connected, the world has also become fragmented by political polarization, identity politics, and growing social isolation. Yet, the conceptualization of the "multitude" still resonates with geographical scholarship over the last two decades that has questioned conventional understandings of social movements and drawn attention to relational, affective, and improvised forms of solidarity produced through everyday practices and encounters (Amin, 2004; Bayat, 2013; Featherstone, 2008; Simone and Rao, 2012).

This session invites theoretical, empirical and methodological interventions that explore unconventional, fragile and often overlooked geographies of solidarity that fall outside established frameworks of social movements, activism, or institutional politics, yet permeate everyday life in subtle and unexpected ways (Neimanis and Walker, 2014; Tsing, 2015).

paper.title: Collectively creating the informal geographies of care in Lisbon’s alternative nightlife

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Elections and electoral geography in turbulent times
schedule_event.description:
Despite growing political volatility and far-right advances across many parts of the world, geographers have had surprisingly little to say about what remains the most obvious manifestation and mechanism of these political shifts: democratic elections. This session seeks to stimulate a debate on the significance and dynamics of elections in the current global conjuncture.

Some twenty years ago, following a period in which the number of nominally democratic systems had dramatically expanded, Barnett and Low (2004) observed “how little processes of democratisation, or democracy as a broader theme, have had on research agendas in human geography”. It is striking that as many democratic systems come under strain today, democracy, at least understood as a set of organised institutions and processes – including elections –, continues to be conspicuously absent from contemporary geographic debates.

The subdiscipline of electoral geography in particular still struggles to shake off its reputation of an “unrepentant positivism” (Johnson & Warf, 2025), contributing to its status as a minority and largely siloed pursuit within political geography. However, the interactions between social geographies and electoral systems have been extensively theorised, ranging from established debates around the role of “context” and place in moulding political attitudes (Agnew, 1996; Johnston & Pattie, 2004) to more recent exploration of how institutional actors seek to enable or suppress the representation of different groups (Forest, 2018). Meanwhile, some scholars have sought to bring frameworks and themes of broader interest to bear on electoral geography, from gender (Secor, 2004; McGing, 2015) and sexuality (Chapman, 2011) to emotional (Schurr, 2013) and digital geographies (Temple, 2023). While Anglophone electoral geography largely maintains a provincial focus on global North, there has been growing attention to electoral geographies elsewhere (Gethin et al., 2021; Halvorsen, 2020; Richmond & McKenna, 2024).

Meanwhile, the widespread electoral turbulence of the last decade has drawn attention to the uneven and shifting geographies of support for different political projects. Attention has focused most emphatically on what Rodríguez-Pose (2018) has called “the revenge of places that don’t matter” – the apparent drift of neglected post-industrial regions towards support for nativist parties, embodied paradigmatically by the “revolt of the rustbelt” in the US (McQuarrie, 2017) and the fall of the Labour Party’s “red wall” in the Midlands and North of England (Mackinnon, 2020). However, far more complex and varied shifts in voting patterns can be observed across different contexts, from support for diverse “populisms” across different regions of Italy (Agnew & Shin, 2017; Rossi, 2018) to electoral surges of both right and left in Texas’ border region (Davis, 2020). While geographers have explored the spatial imaginaries and affective registers of populist and far-right movements (Lizotte & Kallio, 2023; Anderson & Secor, 2022), far more could be said about how they interact with elections, understood as routine democratic exercises with their own institutionalised, lived and imagined geographies.

This session invites contributions that analyse contemporary electoral geographies and geographical insights into the study of elections.

paper.title: Socio-spatial inequality and urban electoral geography: The cases of São Paulo and Rio de Janeiro
Advancements in effectively measuring the Global Rural-Urban Electoral Divide
Gambling on Political Futures: Geographies of Election Betting
Exploring Women Candidates’ Experiences on the Campaign Trail: Campaign Ethnographies in the 2024 UK General Election

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Author meets critics: Joe Gerlach's Spinoza's Geographical Ethics
schedule_event.description:
Spinoza’s Geographical Ethics by Joe Gerlach is the first book length treatment of Spinoza’s work in geography. It offers a careful, inventive and always spirited reading of how Spinoza offers a re-writing of earth and world. Gerlach brings this geographical version of Spinoza to bear on the problem of how to practice a geographical ethics today, in the midst of twenty-first century societal, environmental and political transformations and violent upheavals. As such, Spinoza’s Geographical Ethics grapples with issues long at the heart of geography, including cartography, rights and hope, re-encountering and re-working them through a geographical Spinoza.

The panellists will draw out themes, propositions and positions from Spinoza: A Geographical Ethics, connecting it with their work, and opening a conversation about Spinoza in geography, ways of thinking spatially in the early twenty first century, and how to conceptualise and practice a geographical ethics.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Political discontent and ‘left-behind’ people and places: perceptions, narratives, and regional futures
schedule_event.description:
The uneven impacts of global megatrends – technological change and digitalisation, demographic shifts such as urbanisation, ageing and migration, and climate-related transformations – have increasingly interacted with recent economic and geopolitical shocks, notably rising energy costs and persistent cost-of-living pressures. Together, these processes have intensified spatial inequalities across regions and social groups in many advanced economies (OECD, 2022).

While much existing research has conceptualised political discontent through the lens of left-behind regions, recent work suggests that this framing risks oversimplifying a far more granular geography of disadvantage. Growing attention has therefore turned to how these uneven transformations are experienced, interpreted, and narrated within specific places. Emerging evidence shows that experiences of deprivation, declining opportunity, and weakened social infrastructure are often highly localised, cutting across rural and urban settings and appearing even within otherwise prosperous regions. Left-behindness, in this sense, is not a fixed territorial category but a place-specific condition,shaped by combinations of access to services, demographic change, labour-market dynamics, and social fabric (e.g. RodríguezPose, 2018; Houlden et al., 2024; de Vega Perancho et al., 2025).

This session builds on the literature on the ‘geography of discontent’ (Los et al., 2017; McCann, 2018; 2019), but explicitly moves beyond explanations centred solely on socio-economic decline or compositional disadvantage. Instead, it foregrounds the perceptual, emotional, and narrative dimensions through which spatial inequalities are understood and mobilised, and examines how these dimensions interact with policy responses and regional development strategies.

We invite contributions that explore how political discontent is shaped by spatial imaginaries, collective memories, and local identities, and how these processes vary across places and scales. The session pays particular attention to the implications for place-based policy: how policies are perceived by local actors, how they may unintentionally reinforce feelings of neglect or mistrust, and how alternative approaches might better engage with the lived experiences of socalled ‘left-behind’ places.

Themes and questions the session seeks to address include (non-exhaustive):

- How are experiences of left-behindness constructed, perceived, and narrated at local scales?

- What do recent multidimensional and granular approaches reveal about hidden or unexpected geographies of disadvantage?

- How do perceptions of neglect and misrecognition interact with long-term economic and demographic change?

- In what ways does political discontent function as a spatial feedback loop, reinforcing territorial inequalities?

- How can concepts such as liveability and the foundational economy enrich understandings of political discontent?

- What kinds of place-based policies can engage with everyday experiences of inequality without reproducing mistrust or territorial stigma?

Conceptual, theoretical, and empirical contributions – qualitative or quantitative – are welcome. We particularly encourage papers that connect political geography, regional studies, and public policy, and that speaks to contemporary debates on democracy, spatial inequality, and regional futures.

Submission details

If you are interested in participating in this special session, please submit an abstract via the general abstract submission system of the conference by the stated deadline (03/03/2026).

For any questions, please contact the session organiser

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - China and Beyond: the Contentious Politics of Infrastructure and Real Estates Development in Asia
schedule_event.description:
Research on the Belt and Road Initiative (BRI) has claimed that Global China has given shape to a new, groundbreaking, form of urbanisation (Apostolopoulou, 2020; Curtis and Klaus, 2024; Wiig and Silver, 2019). Drawing on research conducted in the framework of the program « Cities Along the New Silk Roads in Southeast Asia », this panel argues instead that Chinese investments in large infrastructure and real estates are « grafted » into a contentious politics of spatial and economic development that is path-dependent on nationally driven policies, processes of territorial transition, and national elites’ strategies of capital accumulation. The panel mirrors together cases from three Southeast Asian countries — Cambodia, Malaysia, and Thailand — to show how urban and territorial development in which Chinese transfers are involved accentuates patterns of spatial inequality that take root in decades-long processes of territorial transition. Grassroots movements have carved variable geometries of contestation space to those processes that determine how, and to what extent, alternatives for fairer development models can(not) take shape. The case of corridor development in India allows to question the specificity of corridor development as led by Chinese transfers. It also expands further the reflection on local agency, by looking at the governance mechanisms that may allow the expansion of public participation at the sub-national scale.

Cited references: 

Apostolopoulu, Elia. 2020. The uneven geographies of China’s Belt and Road Initiative: Authoritarian neoliberal urbanism, social inequality and grassroots resistance in London, Athens and Colombo. Antipode 53(3): 831-858.

Curtis, Simon and Klaus, Ian. 2024. The Belt and Road City. Geopolitics, urbanization, and China’s search for a new international order. New Haven, London: Yale University Press.

Wiig, Alan and Silver, Jonathan. 2019. Turbulent presents, precarious putures: Urbanization and the deployment of global infrastructure. Regional studies 53(6): 912-923.

paper.title: Beyond the Belt and Road Initiative Narrative: Offshore Urbanism and Underground Financialization in Cambodia
