schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - New and Emerging Research in Food Geographies (1)
schedule_event.description:
In line with RGS-IBG 2026's conference theme of Geographies of inequalities: towards just places, we invite submissions on new and emerging research within Food Geographies. We particularly encourage submissions from early career (ECRs) and postgraduate researchers (PGRs), as well as scholars working in or on the Global South, including research that challenges dominant Euro-American framings of food geographies. This session aims to provide a supportive and constructive space to share work in progress, early findings, or new conceptual and methodological approaches and can be a wonderful opportunity to foster collaborative work, positive dialogue and advance research agendas within the field of food geographies and food justice (Deschact, 2021; Heynen et. al., 2012; Slocum, 2018; among others).

Food is deeply entangled with social, cultural, economic, racial, gendered, and environmental inequalities, from access to land and labour, to nutrition, health, and everyday food practices (see Gibson-Graham et. al. 2013; Cadieux & Slocum, 2015). Research in food geography has shown how food systems across scales often reinforce uneven power relations, while also offering sites of resistance, care, and alternative futures (Goodman et al., 2012; Holt-Giménez, 2017).

For this session, we are particularly interested in papers that critically examine food-related inequalities and explore pathways that broaden the scope of justice and just spaces within Food Geographies. Papers can be innovative about their research aims/design, demonstrate creative methodologies and/or theoretical questions, or draw upon empirical findings. We especially welcome work that closes the gap between food production and consumption, attends to diversity within diets, advances a de/postcolonial approach to foodways, and engages with emerging practices of gastrodiplomacy and gastrosolidarity in digital and physical spaces (Low, 2021).

Some themes include:

  • Food access, poverty, and everyday food inequalities
  • Labour, migration, and justice in food and agricultural systems
  • Race, gender, caste, and colonial injustices in food geographies
  • Indigenous, local, and alternative food knowledges
  • Environment, sustainability, and climate inequalities in food systems
  • Food, health, and uneven outcomes across places
  • More-than-human relations, care, and food justice
  • Resistance, activism, and food-based social movements
  • Affect, emotion, and embodied geographies of food
  • Gastrodiplomacy, gastrosolidarity, and food as soft power
  • Food, place-making, heritage, and cultural memory
  • Methodological innovations in researching food and inequality

References

Cadieux, K. V., & Slocum, R. (2015). What does it mean to do food justice? Journal of political ecology, 22, 1.

Deschacht, L. (2021). What role do urban food strategies play in fostering justice? (Doctoral dissertation, Ghent University).

Low, K. E. (2021). Gastropolitical encounters and the political life of sensation. The Sociological Review, 69(1), 190-205.

Gibson-Graham, J. K., Cameron, J., & Healy, S. (2013). Take back the economy: An ethical guide for transforming our communities. U of Minnesota Press.

Goodman, D., DuPuis, E. M., & Goodman, M. K. (2012). Alternative food networks: Knowledge, practice, and politics. Routledge.

Heynen, N., Kurtz, H. E., & Trauger, A. (2012). Food justice, hunger and the city. Geography compass, 6(5), 304-311.

Holt-Giménez, E. (2017). A foodie's guide to capitalism. NYU Press.

Slocum, R. (2018). Must everything be called “food justice?”. Local Environment, 23(11), 1103-1105.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - New and Emerging Rural Researchers
schedule_event.description:
This Rural Geography Research Group session, held annually, is a vibrant platform for exploring cutting-edge concepts in rural geography and for showcasing the endeavours of postgraduate and early-career researchers. 

Given recent transformative shifts in rural landscapes, our focus aligns with the overarching theme of “Geographies of inequalities”, which examines spatial inequalities and injustices across society, culture, politics, the economy, community, climate and the environment. In the evolving narrative of rural dynamics, landscapes have undergone significant transformations in response to global and national shifts. 

This session aims to discuss some of the inequalities facing rural areas and how they have responded to these inequalities, such as the climate crisis and COVID-19, to name a couple of examples.

We invite you to delve into the diverse landscape of rural geography, and we are interested in hearing from a diverse range of research, including how these inequalities have been mapped, monitored and tracked, for example. 

The aim is to stimulate insightful discussions centred on inequalities in rural geographies, thereby fostering a nuanced understanding of the inherent complexities within the rural landscape.

This in-person session will provide a friendly and encouraging forum for researchers to present their ideas and results and discuss them constructively with all attendees.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Contesting Housing Injustice: Mobilising Materials of Concern (2)
schedule_event.description:
Mould has recently become a potent symbol of domestic inequality in postwelfare states (Kane, 2023; Shurety, 2025; Storer et al., 2025). Similar to other uncharismatic intrusions, mould reveals a material dimension to longstanding geographical concerns for the domestic as a site of struggle, permeated by, and very much a part of, discriminatory ideologies and sociopolitical forces (Blunt and Dowling, 2006; Brickell, 2012; Massey, 1994; Sibley, 1995; Speer 2017). But mould is not just a site of despair, it is also political – this substance has simultaneously been mobilised by activists to articulate reform to discriminatory and neglectful modes of housing provisioning (NEF, 2024). Within the still unfolding mould epidemic, materiality bleeds into metaphor –mould renders visible structural and historical processes of marginalisation lost in technocratic and austere planning regimes (Simpson et al., 2025).

Mouldy struggles not only reveal any bounded notion of home to be a bourgeois fantasy (Kaika, 2004), but politicise the unequal risks and labours sprung from disrupted socio-natural boundaries over which tenants have little control. Whilst Kaika writes of water, we might add concerns relating to a multiplicity of transgressive organic and chemical matter – mould, pests, pollutants – all which enter, grow with, leak from the lining of, and cohabitate our homes at markedly uneven rates. Indeed, precursors to the politicisation of mould can be found in struggles for housing justice which centre cladding (Smith, 2023), lead (Fennel, 2015), dust (Noterman, 2023), and pollution (Schneller et al., 2022). These non-human and uncharismatic intrusions reshape inhabitation, carrying the potential to destabilise security and even to unmake the home under regimes of precarity (Baxter and Brickell, 2014).

A growing chorus of empirical studies draws our attention to racialised-classed logics which shape the distribution of matter out of place (Douglas, 1966). This panel is interested in how people live with, and contest uncharismatic, toxic, or ‘feral’ non-humans and materials. It explores the interface between lived experience and the work of contesting potentially harmful materials – in the practical and evidentiary practices through which matter becomes concerning within and beyond domestic space.

We are interested in the processes through which people, communities and activists ‘scale’ with uncanny domestic intrusions to confront grinding forces of in-situ dispossession. We likewise welcome coalitional scholarship which traces political contestations which form around materials – both in early struggles for recognition where harm is often contested and contentious (Murphy, 2006), and where later, following the “designation of the unseeable” (ibid: 9), matter comes to be used to make claims against hostile and dismissive states, landowners, and institutions for fairer and juster habitation. We hope to draw together scholars engaged in activist work, or studying the quieter work of persevering in conditions of housing distress amidst biomedical, legal and regulatory structures which limit claims and compensation.

paper.title: ‘London housing means mice’: contesting the everyday injustice of domestic pest infestation
Airy infrastructure: Decarbonising residential densification
Caring with and against the breakdown. Maintenance and repair under conditions of neglect
"A horrible dank ... rotting": Renting with Mould in London

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - “Are you doing your work?” Care-full methods and ethics of care in (and beyond) the field (2)
schedule_event.description:
Care is now firmly on the agenda in human geography. Yet too often, it appears as a topic to be studied, rather than an ethic that is actively practiced (Middleton & Samanani, 2021). When Audre Lorde asked the question, “Are you doing your work?” She was referring to the introspection and deeper conversations that need to be had to confront how structures of power shape embodied experiences. The understanding of care not simply as a social practice or as a concept but as an ethic has been taken up by Lawson (2007, 2009), who argues programmatically for geographers to take up a “critical care ethics for our epistemological, ontological, methodological, and daily life practices (as professionals and citizens)” (2007, p. 3). Caring is hard work. As an ethic, it forces us to become entangled and complicates our relationships in the field: with communities, interlocutors/friends, research assistants, and ourselves. Sometimes it is discomfiting. Omer Aijazi (2024, p. 178) asks, “what does thinking from the heart offer? What if we see the world this way?” In this panel, we examine how treating care as both an ethic and a form of relational labour reshapes the everyday work of research and fieldwork.

This session asks a simple, uncomfortable, hopeful, and necessary set of questions: Are we doing our work with care? What is an ethics of care, and how might it show up in the everyday practices of fieldwork, writing, and collaboration? What might it enable (or constrict) when we foreground an ethics of care in our methodological and personal approaches? When does care become a method? Can it disrupt ‘extractivist’ models of research? What do researchers refuse in the name of care? And what refusals are rendered impossible? What happens when care is betrayed? We invite papers that examine the practical, emotional, and moral consequences of care in the field and beyond, focusing on the relationships through which care is enacted: with research assistants, interlocutors/friends, family, community members, and other collaborators involved in the research process. We highly encourage contributions from scholars working in or with contexts in the Majority World and from precarious academic contexts. Particular attention will be paid to works grounded in feminist, decolonial, queer, anti-caste, Southern, and indigenous approaches.

This session is conceived as the foundation for a special issue on care-full methodologies. Contributors will be invited to develop their papers into a collective publication that advances practical, ethical, and critical approaches to conducting research with care. This is the second part of a 2 part discussion.

paper.title: To Care and Be Cared For: Gendered Relational Labour in Ethnographic Fieldwork with Women Workers in Delhi
Additive Research Methodologies: A Prefigurative Care-Full Approach to Participatory Research Design
Beyond positionality, between researcher/practitioner: Embodied modes of capturing, analysing, representing, and translating dance
Shared Rhythms, Situated Ethics: Learning to Care with “Dancing Aunties” in Shanghai
Co-creative Care: Exploring Complexities and Possibilities through Reflexive, Relational Practice
Khyaal Rakhna Unka [Take Care of Him]: Care-full relations and writing with Care

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Reclaiming street space from cars (1): new instruments and experiences with the socio-ecological transformation of urban neighbourhoods and cities for better liveability and more sustainable transport
schedule_event.description:
In recent years many cities worldwide have embarked on a transformation of the transport system to improve living conditions and reduce negative aspects of transport. Among other issues, central objectives of these transformations are to use urban space for non-car purposes to improve liveability and promote the use of non-motorized or shared modes of transport. Various cities have promoted concepts like the 15-min-city, superblocs or improved cycling and walking infrastructures. To implement these concepts, car spaces (parking, streets) need to be re-used for other purposes. Despite questionable overall effects of these new instruments on a city-wide level, the relevance and emergence of these types of policies and related instruments for urban transition processes are clear.

The objective of this session is to assemble research evaluating and assessing transport, social, economic or liveability aspects of instruments and tools employed for transforming urban areas. The instruments might involve restructuring the built environment (including transport infrastructures), communication and participation strategies or relevant financial instruments. We also seek research addressing the availability and redistribution of parking spaces by financial or other instruments or by policy packages combining the instruments mentioned. Preferably, we are seeking empirical contributions using sound theoretical reflections on the effects and changes initiated by relevant policy instruments. Real world laboratories or other means of transdisciplinary research are highly encouraged to present their findings.

paper.title: Citizen Participation through Mobility Benefit Districts - for Increased Streetscape Liveability
Governing Mobility Benefit Districts: Aligning Parking Management and Local Streetscape Transformation in Vienna
Public Acceptability toward Mobility Benefit Districts: Insights from Darmstadt, Germany
Operationalising Street Justice: How London Boroughs Translate Mobility Justice into Street Transformations

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Current and emerging research in transport (1): Inequalities
schedule_event.description:
This session responds to the theme of this year’s conference by considering how transport geographers can further integrate ideas of inequality, justice or equity analysis into the work they do. We welcome abstracts from across transport geography that respond to this theme. Below, we have looked to outline some questions to provoke discussion across qualitative, quantitative, and theoretical perspectives on inequalities.

  • How does access to transport vary based on age, gender, ethnicity, or other protected characteristics (and how can we correct these inequalities)?
  • How does access to transport vary by mode, and how acceptable are the inequalities this invites? How can inequalities in transport be measured, such that they account for the diverse needs of individuals (and the wide-reaching impact transport has on societies)?
  • Is GeoAI compatible with transport justice? Can machine learning assist with computing and uncovering transport inequalities?
  • What is transport justice? How should it be theorised? What has the canon of transport justice missed to date?

The session provide a relaxed, supportive atmosphere for postgraduates at any stage of their research to present work in progress and share ideas with one another. Nonetheless, all are welcome to attend.

The TGRG traditionally rewards a postgraduate prize for the best postgraduate presentation in any TGRG session at the RGS-IBG Conference. All postgraduate presentations in this session will automatically be eligible for the prize.

paper.title: Justice in Shared Mobility: Conceptual Fragmentation, Emerging Perspectives, and a New Analytical Framework

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Transport and Mobility Justice: Current Trajectories and Future Directions
schedule_event.description:
Transport and mobility justice scholarship has long been concerned with uneven access and opportunity. Early work treated accessibility as a social indicator for evaluating public policy (Wachs and Kumagai, 1973), while the spatial mismatch hypothesis exposed how racialised housing discrimination and uneven automobility constrained access to suburbanising employment (Kain, 1968). These contributions established distributional inequality as a central concern and positioned transport as constitutive of social participation rather than a neutral technical domain.

Over time, the field deepened and diversified. Feminist research revealed the spatial containment and entrapment experienced by women across racialised and classed positions (Inwood et al., 2015), linking everyday mobility constraints to labour markets and care responsibilities (Gilow, 2020). Studies of transport subsidies, infrastructure provision and appraisal practices demonstrated how benefits and burdens are unevenly allocated, often legitimised through ostensibly neutral metrics (Pereira et al., 2019). Work on transport disadvantage and social exclusion conceptualised mobility constraints as both cause and consequence of marginalisation (Lucas et al., 2016). In parallel, mobilities scholarship reframed the debate, introducing concepts such as motility and interrogating the discursive and material production of uneven mobility regimes (Kaufmann et al., 2004). Normative interventions, notably Martens’ (2016) sufficiency-based framework and Sheller’s (2018) articulation of mobility justice across scales, sharpened questions of justice, power and political economy.

Taken together, this body of work has substantially reoriented transport scholarship. It has displaced narrow performance metrics, foregrounded distributional and recognitional claims, and situated everyday mobility within wider regimes of power, political economy and environmental change. Transport and mobility justice are now recognised as constitutive of social and spatial ordering, not marginal correctives to technical planning.

This roundtable seeks to broaden and pluralise debates on transport and mobility justice by bringing transport and mobilities scholars into sustained dialogue. In particular, it invites reflection on how transport and mobility justice can engage more substantively with decolonial thought and Global South urbanisms, and what it would mean to take intersectionality and care seriously at the level of method and institutional practice. As decarbonisation and digitalisation reshape infrastructures and mobility regimes, the session will also consider how justice claims should be negotiated across scales, from local accessibility to transnational circulations, without reducing political contestation to technocratic metrics. The objective is to expand the conceptual, geographical and normative horizons of the field while consolidating a forward-looking research agenda.

References

Gilow, M.(2020). “It's work, physically and logistically”. JoTG, 85,102693.

Inwood, J.F., et al.(2015). "Where Do We Go From Here”. Southeastern Geographer, 55(4),417-433.

Kain, J.F.(1968). Housing segregation, negro employment, and metropolitan decentralization. TQJoE, 82(2),175-197.

Kaufmann, V., et.al. (2004). Motility. IJURR, 28(4), 745-756.

Lucas, K., et.al. (2016). Transport poverty and its adverse social consequences. PICET, 169(6),353-365.

Martens, K. (2016). Transport Justice. Routledge.

Pereira, R. H., et.al. (2019). Distributional effects of transport policies on inequalities in access to opportunities in Rio de Janeiro. JTLU, 12(1),741-764.

Sheller, M. (2018). Mobility Justice. Verso.

Wachs, M., & Kumagai, T.G. (1973). Physical accessibility as a social indicator. SEPS, 7(5),437-456.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Tracing Symbolic Boundaries among Islands, Rivers and Borderlines : Improvising Embodied Inequalities in a Fluid World
schedule_event.description:
Migration studies are often narrated through water. Yet in a world marked by rising seas and hardening borders, water does not simply connect—it differentiates. For some, rivers are lifelines of mobility; for others, they become barriers of containment. This workshop interrogates water as a geography of inequity. We ask: How do waterlines—coastlines, rivers, oceanic routes—create and reinforce social inequalities? How are these inequalities embodied, felt differently across islands, borderlands, and vertical landscapes. 

Moving beyond water as mere metaphor, this session engages participants in improvising embodied inequalities. Through a participatory improvisation workshop, we will explore how the ability to cross water (or be stopped by it) is unequally distributed; how ecological degradation and climate uncertainty deepen existing vulnerabilities; and how diasporic memory holds both trauma and resilience. Bridging political ecology, Island geographies, feminist geography, post-humanism, and postcolonial critique, we examine water as a site where power flows unevenly.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - The Missing Building Workshop
schedule_event.description:
This session uses making to explore inequality, belonging, and spatial justice. It is informed by feminist theory, which understands knowledge as lived, situated, and shaped through everyday experience. It draws on The Missing Building, a participatory research framework that invites participants to design a small model of a building they feel is missing in their community. The objective is not to highlight design solutions, but to use the process of making (ABR) as a constructive dialogue between people, nature, and the city, and to attend to what these absences reveal about the places we live and about ourselves as living within them. The session approaches the built environment as relational and practiced, shaped through movement, care, and unequal conditions of access and recognition. It is informed by bell hooks’ engaged pedagogy and her understanding of hope as a lived practice, where making is understood as an ethical and political act. In a world marked by intensified struggles over land, ownership, displacement, and the right to claim space, absence becomes a way of listening differently, attending to who belongs, who decides, and who is left out, while resisting top-down approaches to place and decision-making.

The workshop will start with a brief introduction to The Missing Building, a participatory research project taking place in the UK, the Netherlands, Portugal, Canada, and Mozambique, which invites people to consider which buildings or spaces are missing in their communities. Delegates will spend time creating a small conceptual model of a building they wish existed in their neighbourhood or city. We will finish with a group discussion, reflecting together on belonging, inequality, care, and spatial justice. The session is designed as a space for shared inquiry and making, with opportunities for ongoing collaboration after the conference.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Big Tech, the State and Emerging Governance Assemblages (2)
schedule_event.description:
Recent scholarship highlights the growing influence of technology corporations in shaping political, social, and state practices. Big Tech platforms and AI technologies are increasingly entangled with state power, authoritarian logics, and new forms of governance, raising fundamental questions about political authority, public accountability, and democratic life (Jarret et al, 2025). The concept of “Big Tech authoritarianism” (Akbari 2025) foregrounds how tech giants not only mediate information but also align with or reproduce authoritarian logics of power, surveillance, and control. Likewise, emerging frameworks of AI authoritarianism (Dekeyser et al. 2025) argue that key features of AI-such as scalability, algorithmic incorporation of difference, and techno-determinist ideologies - actively reshape political governance and authoritarian dynamics across geopolitical contexts.

This session responds to a growing need to critically interrogate the political thrust of technological power and the growing political influence of tech capital at a time when digital platforms and algorithmic systems are increasingly central to shaping state practices, public discourse, citizen engagement, and the possibilities for resistance. Rather than treating “the digital” as a neutral domain, we invite geopolitically informed analyses that situate technology in the relational dynamics of power, space, and governance. This aims to build on existing scholarship theorizing data power (Thatcher & Dalton, 2021), technofascism (McElroy 2025; Coeckelbergh, 2026), or digital colonialism (Kwet, 2019), and aligns with recent calls to rethink governance frameworks in light of AI’s active modulation of political processes and the political synergies between Big Tech and state actors.

This session explores how Big Tech and AI are implicated in reconfiguring the practices, techniques, and imaginaries of governance. We seek contributions that situate these developments within broader socio-spatial processes, attending to power, resistance, and the uneven geographies of digital infrastructure and political authority (Akbari 2026 -upcoming). We invite submissions to this paper session on issues related to:

• Big Tech, political power, and emerging forms of governance

• AI, platforms, and the reconfiguration of state authority

• Uneven geographies of digital power

• Practices and logics of digital authoritarianism

• Democratic frictions and political contestation in digital governance

• Manifestations of Big Tech or AI authoritarianism across distinct spheres of governance

References

Akbari, A. (2026-upcoming). Uneven datafication. Dialogues on Digital Society.

Akbari, A. (2025). Big Tech authoritarianism: Political synergies of an emerging power. Dialogues on Digital Society, 1(3), 317-321.

Coeckelbergh, M. (2026). Technofascism: AI, Big Tech, and the new authoritarianism. AI & SOCIETY, 1-14.

Dekeyser, T., Lynch, C. R., & Maalsen, S. (2025). AI Authoritarianism: Towards an Analytical Framework. Transactions of the Institute of British Geographers, e70048.

Jarrett, K., Kitchin, R., Hiah, J., & Knight Steele, C. (2025). Authoritarianism in the digital age. Dialogues on Digital Society, 1(3), 243-251.

Kwet, M. (2019). Digital colonialism: US empire and the new imperialism in the Global South. Race & class, 60(4), 3-26.

McElroy, E. (2025). Siliconization, technofascism, and their unbecomings. Environment and Planning D: Society and Space, 02637758251401437.

Thatcher, J. E., & Dalton, C. M. (2021). Data power: radical geographies of control and resistance. Pluto Press.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Who cares? What does it mean to care in critical geopolitics?
schedule_event.description:
"Who cares?" This simple question asks whether people care at all, who is expected or allowed to care, and which issues, places, and lives are rendered worthy of care. As care is unevenly distributed, valued, and governed, it plays a central role in how injustice and inequality are (re)produced across time and space. The notion of care has conventionally been examined within social and healthcare scholarship, focusing on physical health and emotions within caregiving (Skinner & Herron, 2020). Feminist geographical work foregrounded the gendered organisation of care labour and its entanglement with social reproduction and power (McEwan & Goodman, 2010). Subsequent research across moral, social, and rural geographies has extended care ethics to interrogate ethical consumption, human rights, intergenerational relations, the restructuring of care under neoliberal political economies, and collective practices of care as mutual aid during the pandemic (Olson, et al., 2020).

Despite this rich body of work, care has been less explicitly theorised and explored within critical geopolitics. Political geographers have deeply cared about marginalised spaces and people, precarious lives, and unequal power relations (Campos-Delgado & Côté-Boucher, 2024; McDowell, 2022). Yet little research has explained why people care (or not) about politics and/or political events. In a world marked by the ongoing genocide in Palestine, pandemic afterlives, the rise of right-wing nationalism and anti-migration rhetoric, and Trump’s ‘ego-politics,’ care is unevenly mobilised, fatigued, displaced, or refused.

Care in (geo)politics is also deeply affective. While some forms of care are mediated through attentiveness, empathy, and responsibility (Holton, 2025), others emerge through disaffection, boredom, indifference, resignation, or emotional exhaustion (Anderson & Secor, 2025). Not caring, in this sense, is not simply a moral failure but an affective condition shaped by media saturation, crisis fatigue, and structural distance. How would the world continue if geopolitical issues are often perceived as too distant, overwhelming, or normalised to care? At the same time, environmental change foregrounds forms of care that extend beyond human–human relations into more-than-human relational worlds (Krzywoszynska, 2019). Algorithms, artificial intelligence, digital infrastructures, and cybersecurity, raise new ethical questions about care, responsibility, and governance in socio-technical worlds (Maalsen, 2023).

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Geographies of Impact
schedule_event.description:
How do research 'impact' agendas shape, and are shaped by, the questions geographers ask about power, politics, and inequality? Research impact has become central to contemporary academic institutions, shaping how research in geography and related disciplines is designed, evaluated, valued, and funded. Broadly understood as the positive effects of research on society, impact is increasingly treated as measurable, auditable, and policy-relevant, forming part of an impact culture or agenda (Chubb & Reed, 2018; Rogers et al., 2014). Research that delivers tangible benefits beyond the academy reflects geography’s commitment to justice, equity, and participatory approaches (Hopkins, 2021; Pain et al., 2011). However, dominant frameworks often prioritise particular forms of knowledge, reproduce inequalities, and incentivise research focused on outputs that are pre-defined, quantifiable, measurable (Darby, 2017; Gandy, 2014). In doing so, they can incentivise research oriented towards pre-defined, auditable outputs, while marginalising slower, relational, and less easily measurable forms of engagement (Paul et al., forthcoming).

This session brings together scholars reflecting on the diverse geographies of impact within and beyond the academy. Rather than rejecting the notion of impact, this session creates space for critical and constructive dialogue about how impact is understood, enacted, and experienced across different contexts. We approach impact as a situated and contested practice, emerging through relationships between researchers, institutions, collaborators, and places. Engaging directly with the conference theme of Geographies of Inequalities, the session examines how contemporary impact agendas intersect with broader landscapes of power, exclusion, and marginalisation across academic research. Critics have argued that dominant framings of research impact valorise those impacts that are ‘substantial, visible, muscular, large-scale, auditable, heroic, and readily narratable’ (Horton, 2020, 4). Such framings privilege particular types of ‘impactful’ researcher and position participation as a strategic instrument, while more humble, emergent or relational forms of impact often remain obscured.

The ‘Geographies of Impact’ session runs across two timeslots and emphasises active participation. This first session includes four fifteen-minute presentations selected through an open call, followed by a 40-minute panel discussion, facilitated by Dr. Caitlin Hafferty, with our authors to explore the diverse forms of impact raised in the papers.

The contributions within our session explore diverse practices of research impact, from policy advisory roles within government to community-led relational collaborations and solidarity work in authoritarian contexts. Drawing on fieldwork with disabled communities, governmental engagement shaping health and transport policy, and documentation of racialised violence in authoritarian regimes, the papers examine how impact unfolds through both formal institutional channels and relational, care-full practices. Our presenters reflect on the possibilities of working inside policy spaces, weighing the benefits of proximity to decision-making against potential constraints on academic freedom, alongside the relational and emergent practices of impact to explore how meaningful change occurs through everyday interactions during research. The panel will create space for participatory reflection on these themes. Together, these discussions encourage critical dialogue about how diverse impacts are conceptualised, practised, and experienced across different contexts.

paper.title: Geography and research impact: reflections on academic engagement in government policy
Solidarity as impact in authoritarian geographies
Poetic Resistance: Mushaira Gatherings and Indigenous Commons Rights in Chitral, Pakistan.
The Agile Initiative: Exploring a new model for policy-oriented research
The Imperative for Engaged Research in the Face of Existential Politics: Against the Perverse Incentives of Modern Academia
From Action Research to Research Returns: Reframing Impact through Quilombola Climate Resilience in the Brazilian Amazon

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Entanglements of Change: More-Than-Representational and Practice-Based Approaches to the Spatialities of Socio-Ecological Transformation
schedule_event.description:
Transformation toward a sustainable, post-fossil, and just society is not a master plan. Socio-ecological transformations unfold – and happen – in everyday life, in concrete spaces, through bodies, objects, emotions, and relations. Transformations are neither linear nor purely technological; rather, they are contradictory, contested, and spatially differentiated processes. They emerge within historically sedimented power relations, producing disturbances, detours, and losses, while simultaneously opening up new spaces of possibility. Transformation thus becomes a lived practice (Walker 2020; Görmar & Kinossian 2022), giving rise to diverse modes of subjectivation and agency. Building on this conceptualization, the session seeks to sharpen a more-than-representational, practice-theory, and spatial perspective that understands change as a situated relational entanglement. By foregrounding the political characteristics of space – friction and renegotiation, diversity and encounter, situatedness and relationality – together with the potentials of practice-theory approaches (Hui et al. 2017; Schäfer & Everts 2019), the session aims to make visible both the conflictive and differentiated nature of transformations as well as their creative dimensions. 

Central to such an approach is a focus on the everyday dimensions of transformation (Schurr & Strüver 2016; Domínguez-Guzmán et al. 2023): routines, improvised practices, and modes of engaging with change that unfold “under the radar” of dominant regimes of attention. Equally important is the exploration of the emotional dimensions of transformation (Emery 2020; Martiskainen & Sovacool 2021), including unevenly distributed phenomena such as ‘transition pain’ (Bogner et al. 2024), climate emotions (Neckel & Hasenfratz 2021), as well as hope, and utopian imaginaries (Feola et al. 2023). Finally, a spatial and practice-oriented approach enables grasping diversity/difference within transformations, illuminating how categories such as gender, class, or migration background shape local negotiations of transformational movements, within which different subjects embody different relationships to change (Meier & Aytekin 2018; Braunger & Walk 2022). Hence, transformation is an ongoing re-negotiation of relationships between diverse subjects and groups, individuals and environments, self and other (Zhang 2024). 

The session turns to several interrelated questions. It explores how transformation processes are made visible, tangible, and understandable in everyday life. What do differently situated subjects point to, and where do they lead us when they aim to show transformation(s) and its conflicts? Relatedly, the session asks about the relations between dominant transformation imaginaries, and lived experiences of everyday change. A spatial and practice-theory perspective is also sensitive to the experiences of multiple, overlapping changes. Moreover, the session situates the manifestations of transformation within local assemblages: transformation processes – their visibilities, imaginaries, subjectivities, and capacities – are shaped in and through a “thrown-togetherness” of heterogeneous elements (Massey 2005; Dihlmann & Helbrecht 2024). Transformation must be conceived as emerging from the messiness of everyday life. Such a perspective captures constellations of (in)visibility and (dis)attachment (Anderson 2022), viewing transformations as contentious assemblages. Finally, the session conceives of transformation as a process that generates multiple uncertainties – including epistemological disruptions (Altstaedt, Fladvad & Hasenfratz 2022). It thus invites analyses of the ways in which subjects engage with the “geographies of uncertainty” (Senanayake & King 2021).

paper.title: Diffracting Democratic Spatialities with Practices of Making-with: On the entanglements of invited and invented spaces of participation in socio-ecological transformations in Luxembourg
Cultivated assemblages in transformations: quiet degrowth entangled with persistent allotments
“On the way” to sustainable last-mile: contested mobilities of using light electric freight vehicles
Sensing Traces on a Tomato Plant: A Neurodiverse Approach to Practicing Sensory Multispecies Ethnography

It will foreground research that combines quantitative and theoretical insight, showing how geographers can use diverse forms of evidence to engage with questions of inequality, space, and environment.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Co-creating Coastal Resilience: Participation, collaboration, and cross-sector working for just coastal futures (1)
schedule_event.description:
Coastal risk is not simply the outcome of sea-level rise, erosion, flooding or pollution. It is also produced through the politics of governance – who defines risk, whose knowledge counts, who is allowed in the room, and how responsibility is allocated, shared or quietly offloaded across sectors and scales. As coastal hazards increasingly intersect with housing shortages, ageing infrastructure, habitat loss, public health crises and fragile local economies, collaborative working has become a central expectation within shoreline management and flood risk governance (Sayers et al., 2022; Lane et al., 2011). Yet in practice, participation and partnership is often expensive, slow, fragile and messy – shaped, and sometimes derailed, by uneven capacity, institutional inertia, funding constraints and histories of mistrust (Blunkell, 2017).

This pair of linked sessions brings together researchers, partners and community collaborators involved in the Resilient Coastal Communities and Seas (ReCCS) programme, a £15m UKRI–Defra programme involving 100+ co-leads and charged with doing something ambitious: generating knowledge, action and resilience for UK coastal communities and seas in just 5 years. At the programme’s midpoint, these sessions offer space not for polished narratives, but for honest, critical and constructive reflections on what it really means to attempt participatory coastal research at scale and across sectors with diverging priorities, constraints and cultures.

paper.title: Planning for Uncertainty: Building Adaptive Coastal Planning Capacity in England and Northern Ireland
Co-constructed Community Dialogue – ethnographic vignettes and empathy mapping on Shared User Pathways (SUPs) in Wales.
Building Coastal Community Resilience in the East of England: Reflecting on an Asset-Based Approach
Relational Resilience: Impact and Learning in Transdisciplinary Settings

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - NGOs and Sustainable Fashion Transitions: Challenges, Strategies and Success Stories (1)
schedule_event.description:
It is well-established that the fashion industry is one of the most environmentally and socially unsustainable industries in the world (Bick et al., 2018; Niinimäki et al., 2020; United Nations Environment Programme, 2023). Despite these impacts being well known, the industry is resistant to change and, with the acceleration of ultra-fast fashion, may be getting worse. While there is an urgent need for change, the question remains: how will this occur?

Transforming the industry requires intervention from multiple levers concurrently, including government legislation, consumer shopping behaviours, manufacturing processes, and brands’ decision making. Non-governmental organisations (NGOs) are frequently positioned as key agents of change due to their ability to operate across scales and engage diverse actors. However this reliance on NGOs warrants critical examination, particularly regarding their capacity, legitimacy, accountability, and potential co-optation within existing power structures.

This session is interested in understanding the strategies and role of NGOs influencing the pro-socio-environmental behaviours that value chain actors (i.e. raw materials producers, manufacturers, brands, consumers, recyclers) are pursuing, including challenges and enablers. For the purpose of this session, NGOs are defined as organisations of any size and prominence, involved in activities which push for positive environmental and social change in the fashion industry such as setting certifications and standards, developing industry schemes, campaigning, and reporting on industry activities. Examples include Textile Exchange, Fashion Revolution, Oeko-Tex, Zero Discharge of Hazardous Chemicals (ZDHC), Oxfam, Clean Clothes Campaign, Fair Wear Foundation, Laudes Foundation, Sustainable Apparel Coalition, and Or Foundation.

In this session, the fashion industry is used as a lens to analyse ethical labour and production, product design and materials, recycling and disposal, and over-consumption. This session brings together research from different contexts to critically examine how NGOs influence socially and environmentally sustainable practices across fashion value chains, including limitations, potentially unintended consequences, and success stories to inspire across geographies. Particular attention is given to how NGOs navigate tensions between collaboration and confrontation, voluntary standards and regulatory change, and incremental improvements versus systemic industry transformation.

paper.title: Governing Hazardous Chemicals in the Australian Fashion Industry: The Role of NGOs

Institute for Sustainable Futures, University of Technology Sydney

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Centering intersectional feminist approaches in mobility planning (1)
schedule_event.description:
The lack of free, safe, and accessible mobility limits access to opportunities and restricts full participation in urban life. In response, transforming transport systems towards more sustainable and just models has become urgent, guided by principles of equity and inclusion that underpin “just mobility transitions” (Oliveira Soares & Glaser, 2025; Schwanen, 2021; Sheller, 2018). Within this process, the gender equality perspective has been crucial in making visible the structural inequalities faced by women and other historically marginalised groups in urban planning and mobility. However, while some cities worldwide have implemented gender-sensitive urban mobility policies, the transformative potential of intersectional feminist planning as an integral part of just mobility transitions is yet to be realised. Gender-focused/gender-mainstreaming policies targeting women have been a key starting point for addressing existing inequalities (Jaimurzina et al., 2017; Sánchez de Madariaga, 2013). However, without an intersectional dimension and integral approach, such policies may have a narrow focus and limited impact, thus benefitting some groups more than others or be at risk of being watered down or even hijacked by populist agendas. Additionally, even within feminist movements and organisations pursuing mobility justice for various groups, intersectional thinking is sometimes not integrated, leading to exclusions, omissions and missed opportunities for demanding a broader change. A more radical revision of mobility planning regimes that intersectional feminist lens could inspire is yet to happen. This revision would challenge assumptions and values underpinning mobility planning beyond just making selected services and places ‘women friendly’. Instead, applying intersectional feminist lens to rethinking mobilities could inspire centering care, accessibility for all and combatting intersectional exclusions of multiple marginalized groups, linked to income, disability, ethnic and religious background, gender identity, sexual orientation, etc. (Castañeda et al., 2024; Jirón et al., 2022; Nikolaeva & Shakthi, 2025).

This challenge resonates with what epistemic justice scholarship in mobility planning has highlighted: the epistemic frameworks that shape urban and transport planning remain largely gender-insensitive (Nikolaeva & Shakthi, 2025), thus making it difficult to effectively integrate this approach. Conceptual frameworks, tools, and indicators that guide planning policies are still anchored in dominant ways of producing data, designing policy, and evaluating mobility systems. These technocratic and androcentric paradigms prioritise quantitative evidence, productive (work-related) mobility, and a conception of the human being as a rational, utility-maximising agent. In doing so, they marginalise relational, collective, and everyday experiences of mobility, care-related and recreational mobilities, and erase the diverse positionalities from which the city is experienced. Additionally, politics and geographies of academic and professional knowledge production and circulation do not offer an even ground for ideas, practices and activist efforts to receive exposure. 

Bringing intersectional feminist and epistemic justice perspectives together, the session interrogates dominant gender-insensitive planning paradigms – quantitative, productivity-centred, androcentric, and oriented around the rational, utility‑maximising individual – that systematically marginalise care, relational, recreational and collective everyday (im)mobilities. It asks how knowledge hierarchies in academia, policy, and professional practice shape what counts as evidence, which actors and geographies are heard, and whose experiences of the city remain invisible.

paper.title: Challenging the epistemic foundations of mobility planning from an intersectional feminist perspective in Amsterdam, Barcelona and Santiago de Chile
Between Mapping and Meaning: Intersectional Feminist Reflections on Spatialising Urban Safety
Care, Control, and Capability: Challenging the Epistemic Foundations of Mobility Planning
Planning as Care: Reimagining Transport Through Relational Mobilities
Rendering Mobility of Care (In)Visible: Epistemic Injustice in Mobility Data

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Putting people, place and power at the centre of just transition debates in the UK
schedule_event.description:
Mainstream approaches to climate policy in the UK tend to fixate on individual consumer choice, ignore social and spatial injustices, and put social and environmental needs in competing siloes. Top-down, one-size-fits-all strategies fail to change dominant power relations or reduce carbon emissions at the pace and scale needed, while bottom-up approaches that just assume communities can step in when the state does not (eg 'the Big Society') are inadequate. It is well past time for a paradigm shift. 

Place-sensitive, collective strategies are the best hope for moving toward a fairer and more democratic low carbon future. These strategies must meet climate ambitions at the same time as tackling myriad problems caused by socio-economic inequalities in one of the most spatially unequal countries in the world. But to facilitate this shift, we need not only to understand what works where, how and for whom -- and what could work in different circumstances or with more or different resources -- but also how to communicate positive examples that have broad public resonance. 

We propose a conference panel dedicated to discussing the role of place-sensitive, participatory research in realising these urgent needs. How can co-productive research demonstrate the transformative potential of an inclusive and just transition to a lower carbon society? What is needed to enable citizens to collectively decide what a just climate future means for them? Which stories and whose voices should be amplified to rebuild trust and counter the rising resistance to net zero, especially from the populist right?

In this proposed panel, researchers in the ESRC-funded Centre for Joined-Up Sustainability Transformations (JUST) will attempt to answer these questions. We will discuss findings from our place-sensitive case study research with communities in five areas of North of England, highlighting  the substantive themes (including principles of justice, social infrastructure, social and solidarity economies, democratic innovations and policy, governance and change) and the methodological challenges and creativity that have emerged so far. 

The panel's framing themes and questions will be introduced by the Director of the JUST Centre (MacGregor, chair of the session), before five ‘snapshot’ presentations about case studies in West Cumbria, Merseyside, Greater Manchester, West Yorkshire and the North East. There will then be a chaired discussion with all panellists and session participants, which invites additional examples and reflections on how participatory, co-produced research can contribute to the development of hopeful narratives that put people, place and power at the centre of just transition debates.

We plan to fund five early career researchers who have been working on place-based case studies to attend the RGS Conference to give short presentations (not papers so they are not submitting individual paper proposals). Case studies include: Cumbria Citizen's Climate Assembly, Todmorden Learning Centre, Dwellbeing Shieldfield, Growing Together in Levenshulme, and Kindred (Liverpool).

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Geographies of the Middle East and North Africa Keynote Lecture
schedule_event.description:
Geographies of the Middle East and North Africa research group are honoured to have Dr Munira Khayyat as the inaugural keynote speaker for the research group. Khayyat is an anthropologist focused on life in war, intimate genealogies of empire, and theory from the South. Her first book, A Landscape of War: Ecologies of Resistance and Survival in South Lebanon (University of California Press 2022) examines resistant ecologies in a world of perennial warfare. Drawing on long-term fieldwork in frontline villages along Lebanon’s southern border with Israel, she examines war not only as a place of death and destruction, but also necessarily, as an environment of living.

Khayyat is currently working on a second book that fleshes out the complex heart of empire in Saudi Arabia. Heart of Black Gold draws on a personal archive meticulously created by her maternal grandfather, who was among the first Arabian employees of ARAMCO, the Arab American Oil Company. How has oil — its extractive, shiny infrastructures, camps, big men, politics and corporations, its global ecologies — shaped lived environments? Insisting on a feminist and multidisciplinary rearranging of the archive, the book inhabits history-in-the-making as it unfolds in domestic scenes, lived quarters, the affective terrains of oil.

Title and Abstract TBC

paper.title: The Fire Will Never Cease: Defiant Ecologies in South Lebanon

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Chair's event: Challenging racism and racial inequalities
schedule_event.description:
This panel session focuses on the important work being done in geography and related fields to tackle racism and to challenge racial inequalities.

Drawing on different international examples – and speaking from diverse positions - the panel focuses on how research is exposing the harm being done by racism in different geographical contexts, the ways in which researchers and their collaborators are working to resist this, and how different approaches to antiracism feature within this work.

Featuring Professor Laura Pulido (London School of Economics and Political Science), Professor Claire Hancock (Paris), Dr James Carr (University of Limerick), Dr Rachel Sharples (University of Western Sydney), Dr Margaret Byron (University of Leicester), and chaired by Dr Alanna Kamp (University of Western Sydney).

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Leisure as an infrastructure of care and belonging in contexts of migration and displacement
schedule_event.description:
In the context of global migration, forced displacement, and transnational mobility, questions of belonging, wellbeing, and inclusion have become increasingly central (Minca et al., 2022; Phillimore, 2024). This session examines leisure as a collective infrastructure of care and belonging in contexts of migration and displacement, foregrounding how leisure is shaped through community leadership, collaboration, and voluntary labour involving migrant and refugee communities. Leisure spaces and experiences, such as festivals, community events, volunteering initiatives, and other immersive and embodied forms of leisure, are not simply sites of participation or consumption, but are actively produced through relational organising across community, institutional, and public domains. While such infrastructures can enable care, cultural continuity, and social connection (Atalay & Korkut, 2025; De Martini Ugolotti, 2022; Hassanli, Walters, & Williamson, 2021; Hassanli, Walters, & Friedmann, 2020; Kim et al., 2021; Murad & Versey, 2021; Spaaij et al., 2023; Stodolska, 2025; Zhang et al., 2022), they are also shaped by governance arrangements, funding precarity, and uneven distributions of labour and recognition, and may therefore both support wellbeing and reproduce social and spatial inequalities (De Martini Ugolotti, 2022; Hassanli, Walters, & Williamson, forthcoming; McGee & Pelham, 2018).

Building on this framing, the session brings together leisure and tourism geographies to examine how leisure infrastructures are organised, governed, and sustained, and with what consequences for care and inequality. Leisure is approached through an infrastructural lens, understood as the interconnected networks of people, organisations, spaces, practices, and resources that enable leisure to take shape in relation to migrant and refugee communities. We call for contributions that move beyond individual participation (demand side) to examine who organises leisure, who performs the labour of care, and how organisational and governance conditions shape the capacity of leisure to function as collective care (supply side). In doing so, the session aligns closely with the conference theme Geographies of Inequalities: Towards Just Spaces.

paper.title: Leisure as an infrastructure of care and mental health in rural–coastal communities in the UK
Watching Mardi Gras: Embodied observations on leisure, infrastructure and inequality
The Shadow Hustle: Migrant Domestic Workers, Volleyball, and Informal Urban Economies in Singapore
Hospidarity and leisure places of care

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Film screening and discussion: Saodat: Another History of the Soviet Empire (2026)
schedule_event.description: This Author meets Critics session centres on the new 21-minute mixed-media film Saodat: Another History of the Soviet Empire, written and directed by feminist geographers and sociologists Negar E. Behzadi and Lucia Direnberger, with animation direction by Kate Jessop. Combining filmed interviews, archival footage and photography, and animation, the film tells the story of Saodat, a woman from Tajikistan whose social and professional mobility was supported by the Soviet system in its borderlands. Through Saodat’s life and intergenerational memories, the co-authored film offers an embodied and situated history of Soviet colonialism in Central Asia. It traces how her life was intertwined with broader geopolitical transformations while also revealing the forms of ‘embodied violence’ embedded in the Soviet colonial project. The film foregrounds how Saodat navigated and embraced norms of Soviet modernity as a pathway to advancement, situating her experience within the wider history of empire. Building on this work, Behzadi and Direnberger have also developed a research article ‘A Feminist Geopolitics of Soviet Colonialism’, which draws on Saodat’s story to explore the production of ‘borderland bodies’. The session will include a screening of the film followed by a discussion with three invited scholars. Critics will explore how the filmic project challenges epistemological and ontological inequalities in histories and geographies of colonialism, particularly the marginalisation of Soviet colonialism in post- and decolonial debates. They will also consider the role of feminist situated histories and visual methodologies in decolonising geographical knowledge and contributing to the making of more just places through a praxis of visibilisation and reparation.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Chair's event: How to generate ‘thinking environments’
schedule_event.description:
How can we enable students, researchers, collaborators, and colleagues to do their very best thinking, for themselves? This session addresses this question by sharing the ‘time to think’ approach developed by Nancy Kline (1999, 2009, 2020).

In this session, we will be joined by Time to Think Global Faculty member Claire Andrews who will introduce this approach and run through the ten components involved in creating a ‘thinking environment’. We will then focus on the practical applications of this including a live demonstration of a ‘time to think’ session in action.

Featuring Claire Andrews (independent), Professor Jen Bagelman, Renzo Szkwarok, and Professor Peter Hopkins (all Newcastle University).

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Home–school borderlands: family, discipline, and the making of unequal childhoods
schedule_event.description:
Discipline and shame rarely stop at the school gate. They move through homework routines, attendance monitoring, fee reminders, parent–teacher meetings, messaging apps, and neighborhood reputational circuits—reworking who is held responsible for “good” behavior and “good” schooling, and with what emotional, social, and material consequences. These home–school borderlands can operate as infrastructures of inequality, enrolling families into regimes of respectability, compliance, and accountability while unevenly distributing blame, risk, and care across households, teachers, platforms, and institutions.

Bringing together children’s, youth and family geographies with geographies of education and emotional/digital geographies, this session examines how unequal childhoods are produced and contested across home–school relations. We ask: How do discipline, shame, fear, and humiliation travel across school, home, and everyday urban space? What “infrastructures” (bureaucratic, spatial, and digital) intensify or redistribute educational pressure? How do these dynamics intersect with class, caste, gender, disability, migration status, language, and precarious labor—and how do children and families navigate, refuse, or rework them?

The session connects four complementary papers. First, an ethnographic case study of low-fee private schooling in an “unauthorized” colony in Delhi examines shame-based discipline as a form of affective governance that extends into the home via parental threats and reputational pressure. Second, a Kerala-focused paper traces how legal reasoning can re-legitimate corporal punishment as “character formation,” contrasted with an alternative schooling model that rejects subjugation and adult authority as the basis of order. Third, an EdTech paper analyzes “coerced consent,” showing how school requirements and social pressure push families into educational apps while producing stratified privacy harms for children. Fourth, a China-based participatory study explores how smartphones and classed digital parenting practices reshape children’s mobility, supervision, and “affective inaccessibility” across the home–school borderland.

paper.title: ‘Beizzati’ beyond the school gate: shame-based discipline and home–school borderlands in a low-fee private school in Delhi

paper_authors.identity_string: Geographies of Children, Youth and Families Research Group

paper.title: Coerced Consent: Shame, Social Pressure, and the Erosion of Children's Privacy in Educational Technology

paper_authors.identity_string: Department of Politics and International Studies , Pondicherry University

paper.title: “Dad Drives, I Scroll”: Smartphones, Parental Discipline, and the Making of Classed Childhoods across Urban China's Home–School Borderlands

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Feminist Geographies of Heat, Health and Thermal Inequality (1)
schedule_event.description:
Feminist Geographies of Heat, Health and Thermal Inequality

Climate change is increasingly recognised as a defining health challenge of our time, yet its harms are profoundly uneven. Extreme and chronic heat, in particular, is often framed through biomedical thresholds and behavioural risk models, obscuring how exposure, endurance and recovery are structured through gendered labour, racialised housing markets, care infrastructures and political economy. This session intervenes by bringing feminist geography into direct conversation with critical health geography to reposition heat as a form of slow violence, an embodied, relational and deeply political determinant of health.

Building on feminist care ethics (Tronto), intersectionality (Crenshaw), and climate justice scholarship, we conceptualise heat as a form of thermal violence: a slow, cumulative force that reorganises daily rhythms, constrains rest and hydration, intensifies unpaid care work, exacerbates reproductive and mental health vulnerabilities, and exposes workers in precarious sectors to disproportionate risk. Who is able to cool, rest and recover? Whose suffering is rendered visible in heat metrics, and whose remains normalised?

We invite theoretical, methodological and empirical contributions from across the Global South and North that engage with themes such as:

  • Gendered and intersectional heat-health inequalities
  • Cooling infrastructures, energy poverty and thermal justice
  • Labour, care and everyday endurance under chronic heat
  • Housing, informality, mobility and public space in warming cities
  • Feminist political ecology and climate-health governance
  • Innovative feminist and mixed methods for researching heat-health
  • Mapping heat hot spots and vulnerability using geospatial approaches

These topics are illustrative. We encourage any contributions that critically engage the nexus of gender, health, and environmental change.

The session will run in a mixed format combining paper presentations with a facilitated discussion designed to surface shared questions and future directions. We warmly encourage Early Career Researchers and scholars working across disciplines to submit.

Submission Details: Given the short timeline, abstracts (max 250 words) are requested promptly by 4th March 2026. We welcome work in progress, conceptual interventions and empirically grounded studies. We will review submissions on a rolling basis due to the tight deadline. Accepted presenters will be notified quickly in order to meet the RGS-IBG programme deadline of 6 March 2026.

Beyond the conference, this session aims to open space for an ongoing collaborative conversation. Participants will be invited to join a developing feminist heat-health reading group to continue dialogue and explore where this agenda might lead.

Contact: If you are interested in contributing, please get in touch with session organisers: Dr Mildred O. Ajebon (m.o.ajebon@durham.ac.uk) and Dr Caitlin Robinson (caitlin.robinson@bristol.ac.uk).

Let’s use this moment to sharpen the debate and build something lasting.

schedule_event.title: Journal of Geography in Higher Education Reception: Celebrating 50 years of JGHE
schedule_event.description:
Come and join members of the editorial board and friends of the Journal of Geography in Higher Education to celebrate 50 years promoting, enhancing and sharing geography learning and teaching in all institutions of higher education throughout the world. 

The journal provides a forum for geographers and others, regardless of their specialisms, to discuss common educational interests, to present the results of educational research, and to advocate new ideas.

The reception follows the Journal of Geography in Higher Education, Teaching Place Name Justice Under Pressure: Curriculum Hacking and Infrastructures of Care Between Critical Geography and K–12 Classrooms’ by Derek H. Alderman, Department of Geography & Sustainability, University of Tennessee, Knoxville USA

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Making Life Possible: Rethinking the South and ‘Unprecedented’ Climate Risk
schedule_event.description:
Amid accelerating climate breakdown and deepening socioecological crises, cities are increasingly at the forefront of what is being theorized as the urban South—not as a static geography, but as a relational condition of unevenly burdened climate risk, everyday repair practice and resistance under a global political economic system of finance capital. This session seeks to extend and critically engage with emergent debates that look to rethink Southern urbanism through the lens of repair practice (McElvain & de Coss-Corzo, 2025) mediated by finance and within emergent climate economies (Webber et al, 2022).

Through empirically-grounded provocations, this panel explores how repair—understood as the entangled material, social, and political labour practices that make life possible—both reproduces and contests conditions wrought by climate-driven crises across Southern urban landscapes. We ask:

  • How do communities in Southern cities improvise, endure, and organize in the face of "unprecedented" climate and political shocks?
  • How do climate breakdown and ‘unprecedented’ climate risk reconfigure the geographies of the urban South?
  • How do state and extra-state forces enforce uneven risk through financialized governance, militarized care, and speculative reconstruction?
  • And how might Southern urban experiences of repair inform reparative, decolonial, and abolitionist futures beyond Eurocentric models of resilience and development?

References

McElvain, B., & de Coss-Corzo, A. (2025). Rethinking the urban South? Centering “unprecedented” risk and repair. Urban Geography, 0(0), 1–15. https://doi.org/10.1080/02723638.2025.2561962

Webber, S., Nelson, S., Millington, N., Bryant, G., & Bigger, P. (2022). Financing Reparative Climate Infrastructures: Capital Switching, Repair, and Decommodification. Antipode, 54(3), 934–958. https://doi.org/10.1111/anti.12806

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - ‘Ihsaan’ in the Urban Realm: Socio-spatial geographies of more-than-justice (1)
schedule_event.description:
What if justice is only the minimum condition for our collective urban futures — not the horizon? Much contemporary urban theory is organised around rights, recognition, redistribution, and repair. These frameworks remain vital. Yet across cities worldwide, everyday urban life is also sustained through quieter practices that exceed calculation, obligation, and contract: care, generosity, patience, mutual holding, and collective striving. These are not easily captured by policy categories — yet they keep cities liveable where institutions fall short. This session invites scholars working with “more-than justice” frameworks in the urban realm, geographies that resist capture.

Answering a pluriversal call to bring alternative ontological infrastructure, we are using “ihsaan” as a conceptual holder to refer to forms of relating and acting that go beyond what is required—practices of giving, sustaining, and enabling life that are not reducible to legal mandate, policy design, or transactional ethics. It appears in the quiet infrastructures of everyday life: neighbours holding each other up, communities extending care beyond duty, and collective acts that make the city liveable. How can we theorise them otherwise?

Building on recent theorisation of ihsaan in politics (Khan, 2019) and urban studies (Shafique, 2024), this session treats ihsaan not as abstract virtue, but as urban praxis. It directs attention to the gap between formal justice and lived socio-material reality, and to the everyday labour that quietly sustains urban life where institutions fall short. We welcome theoretical and empirical contributions that rethink urban ethics, politics, and practice beyond rights-based imaginaries.

We ask:
// What dimensions of urban life escape the language of justice, and why do dominant urban theories struggle to grasp everyday practices of care, generosity, and dignity?
// What forms of urban ethics and value exist beyond justice — beyond entitlement, repair, and compliance — and how can these be theorised without collapsing into moral idealism?
// How can we reconceptualise the city as shaped by affirmative flows of desire, generosity, and collective striving, rather than only by conflict, scarcity, claim-making, and protection — and what alternative urban logics of giving, sustaining, and enabling does this reveal?

If this resonates with your work, we warmly invite your contribution.

paper.title: Ihsan as urban moral agency
Whose Public Space? Place Valuation, the Politics of Visibility, and Everyday Practices in Shanghai’s Xuhui Riverside
Coproducing sustenance through repair work: Spatializing care infrastructure within electronics economy across Hyderabad and Delhi
​​From the “Cannot”: The Emergence of Ethical Know-How through Water Infrastructural Tinkering in Nairobi

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Reimagining Health, Wellbeing and Inclusion: Responsible Food Geography Research and Innovation
schedule_event.description:
This session aims to examine how food systems research engages with health, wellbeing, inclusion, and equitable access to food as they are shaped by intersecting inequalities, including racialised labour relations, gendered food work, and uneven access to healthy and culturally appropriate food. Food geography as an emerging research area, professional practice, and agri-food movements addresses questions of multiple justice, inequality, sustainability, identity, and multispecies relations (Pradhan and Pant, 2026). As the field grows, so does the need for research, policy, and practices that are co-designed, co-created, ethically grounded, inclusive, and attentive to power and politics (Pant, 2019; Shaw et al., 2024).

The session invites participants to present research results while critically examining their own positionality, reflecting on the ethical dimensions of their methods, and considering how food geography research can support more just and sustainable food system futures.

While we are open to novel ideas, the following are indicative topics participants may consider.

  • Participatory and community‑led research, co-design, and co-production addressing the triple burden of malnutrition (under/over nutrition, nutritional deficiency)
  • Decolonial and anti‑oppressive methodologies in food systems research
  • Multispecies and more‑than‑human food geographies
  • Food justice, food security, sovereignty, and labour politics
  • Reflexivity, positionality, and responsible food systems research and innovation

References

Pant, L.P. (2019). Responsible innovation through conscious contestation at the interface of agricultural science, policy, and civil society. Agriculture and Human Values 36, 183–197. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10460-019-09909-2

Pant et al. (2025). A global scoping review of alternative food movements calls for food justice and justice beyond individual humans. Global Food Security, 46, p.100877. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gfs.2025.100877

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Memory, Memorialization, and Resilience in Displaced Lives
schedule_event.description:
Memories are sites where identity, belonging, and resistance are enacted. For refugees and displaced communities, memory sustains connections to their lost homes and once celebrated cultural heritage. In contexts of displacement, where social and cultural identities are under constant pressure, memory becomes a vital resource to preserve their history. Through shared acts of remembering, they come together to memorialize their struggles and reconstruct collective histories to bridge the gaps between remembered places and present realities. They come together to make a place in the out-of-places to resist their erasure.

Yet, their memory also carries the weight of trauma. Their experience of violence can also produce silence that tends to forget the painful events. It raises critical questions about memorialization. Could memorialization be a selective process of drawing boundaries between self and the other to marginalize certain experiences, legitimize social hierarchies, and reinforce or challenge dominant narratives?

We begin from the assumption that memory is neither a straightforward resource for resilience nor a passive terrain of suffering. Instead, we seek to invite papers that acknowledge the complex reality that memory work among displaced communities can be simultaneously generative and contested; healing and fragmenting; collective and exclusionary. As forced displacement reaches unprecedented levels globally, we seek papers that aim to understand how memory can operate as a terrain of survival and as a site of power struggles. In other words, we are interested in examining memory as an active, ongoing political process that seeks to define not just belonging but possible futures as well. We welcome empirical, theoretical, and methodological studies that make critical interventions to existing scholarship drawing from a broad range of memory practices and contexts, including storytelling, material objects, rituals, poetry, cultural performance, among others.

The papers may specifically questions including but not limited to the following:

- When there is so much trauma involved in their lived experience, how do
occluded memories find ways in their places?
- Can memory and memorialization foster resilience by creating spaces for
recognition, continuity, and collective healing?
- How memory is practiced, produced, and mediated by power?
- Who gets to remember publicly, and whose memories remain (in)visible
- How do everyday practices—storytelling, rituals, material objects, and
cultural performances—trace and sustain memory over time?

paper.title: Hope is Home: Land, Memory, and the Politics of Counter-Mapping
Refugee Camp as Memoryscape
Memory as Spatial Struggle: Displacement, Reconstruction, and Contested Futures in Sur and Dêrsim
Journalistic Witnessing- Testimony Gathering and Collection in Mediterranean Border Zones

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Energy justice and the longue durée of coloniality within energy systems (1)
schedule_event.description:
Achieving energy justice is often assumed possible through technological substitution attained by fast speed at vast scales. Undoubtedly, technologies are important. However, without critically engagement, lower-carbon energy systems can also entrench or exacerbate existing injustices. These concerns are sharply exposed in studies at the intersection between energy change and historical and contemporary experiences of colonisation. Powering colonial-capitalism has long been afforded through extractivism and exploitation. In post-colonial and carbon-conscious contexts, for example, lower-carbon energy has been used as a medium for dispossession and marginalisation (Rignall 2016; Stock 2023) while settler-colonial focused studies illustrate energy justice is often contingent on alignment with dominant social and economic structures (Chandrashekeran 2021; Cain 2024; Hoicka et al. 2021). Neocolonial power is also projected through contemporary systems of international development (Castán Broto et al. 2018; Munro et al. 2017) and global capitalism (Dunlap and Arce 2022), often predicated on universalist understandings of the world centred in Euro-American ways of being and knowing (Kumar 2022). Energy systems are also used to further authoritarian agendas predicated on patriarchal and ethnoreligious dominance. Such epistemological stances seemingly infiltrate even "non-colonial" contexts, for example, in sustaining energy ontologies that are presented as the norm but inhibit justice (Cain 2025; Damgaard et al. 2022). Energy justice, therefore, must reckon with the longue durée of coloniality within energy systems.

In this session, we invite papers from all disciplines that bring a (de/post/anti)colonial lens to energy justice studies to collectively and critically reflect on the colonial ways of knowing, producing and living with energy. Papers might examine, but are not limited to, the following themes and topics:

  • Land and place, particularly concerning local and Indigenous peoples and their relationship with land;
  • Policy, governance and decision-making which enacts or inhibits justice;
  • Ontologies and/or epistemologies of energy and energy justice;
  • Alternative, justice-centred energy futures;
  • Innovative or praxis-oriented methods to study energy in ways that empower marginalized groups.

paper.title: Once burnt, twice shy: Charcoal politics and energy justice in the longue durée
Can energy justice be salvaged in Oceania?
Exploring the enduring coloniality of large-scale energy projects through collective memory and sacrifice narratives
The Illusion of Additionality and the Hidden Opportunity Costs of Carbon Backed Energy Cooking in Urban Ghana
Just Transitions or Militarised Mobility? The Political Economy of Bus Electrification
