schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Interactions between residential location, housing, and mobility: the challenges of a cross-cutting theme (2)
schedule_event.description:
A wealth of literature has developed in Anglo-Saxon and European countries over the past 20 years on the interactions between residential location, housing, and mobility. It has highlighted the importance of a systemic approach that combines housing and transportation costs at the household level (Haas and Osland, 2013; Coulombel, 2018). Some studies have adopted a macroscopic approach to show the central role of the state of socio-technical systems, including a political economy of car dependency that enables space-consuming and energy-intensive land use patterns (Le Goff et al., 2025). Other more sociological approaches have focused on households’ overall residential strategies (Desvallées and Haine, 2025, Zanolini and Morency, 2025). Finally, most of this research has focused on the social issues raised by this double expense. They highlight the interconnection between vulnerabilities in housing and mobility, which overlap in households facing a combination of socioeconomic difficulties and spatial marginalisation (Mattioli et al., 2018; Simcock et al., 2021).

In addition to this social issue, which can be further explored through changes in lifestyles (e.g., teleworking, electric cars), the topic of household spending on housing and mobility is now giving rise to new avenues of research, broadening and enriching the questions being asked.

1) Firstly, from a methodological point of view, statistical data on housing and mobility are very often constructed in thematic silos, and the development of statistical tools to observe these expenditures is currently very limited. Some recent work has attempted to overcome this limitation, either by using databases that had not been exploited from this perspective until now (Makarewics et al., 2020), or by developing original collection methods (Nicolas et al., 2025).

2) Still on the issue of the cross-cutting nature of the topic, the treatment of mobility-housing interactions raises key questions about spatial planning and public policy choices in terms of anticipating and controlling urban sprawl. It therefore seems important to examine public policy measures in order to diagnose the persistence of the siloed treatment of these two issues, but also to report on the concerns of local authorities regarding the role of this double expenditure on regional planning.

3) Exploring the environmental dimension of these interactions broadens the discussion beyond social issues to include impacts in terms of emissions and shifts the focus to other audiences, potentially moving from low-income households to the residential footprint of wealthy households. It also questions the tools used to represent the interactions between transportation and urban planning in situations of strong urban dynamics.

4) Finally, there is a wide variety of fields that have been explored from this angle of interactions between residential location, housing, and mobility, with opportunities to cross-reference different spatial contexts. The idea is to offer regional perspectives on North and South America, Europe, Asia, Africa, and the Pacific region/

The session we are proposing will therefore be an opportunity to take stock of recent academic work on the theme, with the intention of launching a collective reflection in the social sciences, engineering sciences, environmental sciences, and health sciences.

paper.title: Residential Location, Housing Precarity, and Mobility Constraints: A Cross-Cutting Framework for Identifying Dual Energy-Mobility Cost Burden
The Impact of Short-Term Rentals (STRs) and STR Regulation on Neighborhood Change in New Orleans
Double Energy Vulnerabilities in France: When Residential Choices Lock Households into Patterns of Energy Dependence

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - “Are you doing your work?” Care-full methods and ethics of care in (and beyond) the field (1)
schedule_event.description:
Care is now firmly on the agenda in human geography. Yet too often, it appears as a topic to be studied, rather than an ethic that is actively practiced (Middleton & Samanani, 2021). When Audre Lorde asked the question, “Are you doing your work?” She was referring to the introspection and deeper conversations that need to be had to confront how structures of power shape embodied experiences. The understanding of care not simply as a social practice or as a concept but as an ethic has been taken up by Lawson (2007, 2009), who argues programmatically for geographers to take up a “critical care ethics for our epistemological, ontological, methodological, and daily life practices (as professionals and citizens)” (2007, p. 3). Caring is hard work. As an ethic, it forces us to become entangled and complicates our relationships in the field: with communities, interlocutors/friends, research assistants, and ourselves. Sometimes it is discomfiting. Omer Aijazi (2024, p. 178) asks, “what does thinking from the heart offer? What if we see the world this way?” In this panel, we examine how treating care as both an ethic and a form of relational labour reshapes the everyday work of research and fieldwork.

This session asks a simple, uncomfortable, hopeful, and necessary set of questions: Are we doing our work with care? What is an ethics of care, and how might it show up in the everyday practices of fieldwork, writing, and collaboration? What might it enable (or constrict) when we foreground an ethics of care in our methodological and personal approaches? When does care become a method? Can it disrupt ‘extractivist’ models of research? What do researchers refuse in the name of care? And what refusals are rendered impossible? What happens when care is betrayed? We invite papers that examine the practical, emotional, and moral consequences of care in the field and beyond, focusing on the relationships through which care is enacted: with research assistants, interlocutors/friends, family, community members, and other collaborators involved in the research process. We highly encourage contributions from scholars working in or with contexts in the Majority World and from precarious academic contexts. Particular attention will be paid to works grounded in feminist, decolonial, queer, anti-caste, Southern, and indigenous approaches.

This session is conceived as the foundation for a special issue on care-full methodologies. Contributors will be invited to develop their papers into a collective publication that advances practical, ethical, and critical approaches to conducting research with care.

paper.title: Moving with Care and Discomfort: Co creating Embodied Health Knowledge in Women’s Outdoor Activities

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Urban inequalities and transformative interventions (1)
schedule_event.description: Cities are increasingly paradoxical spaces, concurrently representing vast wealth concentration and deeply embedded socio‑economic and spatial disparities. Set within a context of intensifying global crises – from climate change to financial precarity – traditional geographical perspectives are being encouraged to extend diagnosis to critically examine transformative interventions that can reshape urban policy and practice. This session brings together papers that not only identify urban injustices but also critically examine effective solutions to those injustices, thereby challenging the status quo. The core challenge addressed across the papers is how interventions translate from academic critique into tangible political or planning initiatives, thereby ‘shaping agendas’. Many of the papers draw innovative methodologies (for example, comparative longitudinal analysis, participatory action research, and advanced spatial modelling) to critically assess the effectiveness and scalability of these interventions. Ultimately, these papers collectively address the question: How can geographical research on inequality, through rigorous and forward‑thinking analysis, serve as a force for radical urban change, shaping legislative agendas and governance toward a more just urban future?

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Authors meet critics: Geoffrey DeVerteuil and Andrew Power's Social Geographies: Relationalities, Encounters and Hope
schedule_event.description: This session brings together the authors of Social Geographies: Relationalities, Encounters and Hope (Geoffrey DeVerteuil and Andrew Power) for an evening “author meets critics” discussion of the book’s key themes and contributions. The book explores how relational thinking, everyday encounters, and practices of care and solidarity shape social geographies, while also reflecting on the possibilities for wellbeing and hope in contemporary social life. Drawing on diverse empirical and theoretical contributions, it considers how relationships between people, places and environments are continually made and remade through everyday interactions.Following a short overview of the book by the authors, invited discussants Eleanor Jupp and Gavin Andrews will offer critical reflections on the book’s themes, before opening the discussion to the audience. The session aims to generate a lively conversation about the future directions at the intersection between social and health/wellbeing geographies and the role of relational approaches in understanding contemporary challenges in people's lives.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - New and Emerging Research in Food Geographies (2)
schedule_event.description:
In line with RGS-IBG 2026's conference theme of Geographies of inequalities: towards just places, we invite submissions on new and emerging research within Food Geographies. We particularly encourage submissions from early career (ECRs) and postgraduate researchers (PGRs), as well as scholars working in or on the Global South, including research that challenges dominant Euro-American framings of food geographies. This session aims to provide a supportive and constructive space to share work in progress, early findings, or new conceptual and methodological approaches and can be a wonderful opportunity to foster collaborative work, positive dialogue and advance research agendas within the field of food geographies and food justice (Deschact, 2021; Heynen et. al., 2012; Slocum, 2018; among others).

Food is deeply entangled with social, cultural, economic, racial, gendered, and environmental inequalities, from access to land and labour, to nutrition, health, and everyday food practices (see Gibson-Graham et. al. 2013; Cadieux & Slocum, 2015). Research in food geography has shown how food systems across scales often reinforce uneven power relations, while also offering sites of resistance, care, and alternative futures (Goodman et al., 2012; Holt-Giménez, 2017).

For this session, we are particularly interested in papers that critically examine food-related inequalities and explore pathways that broaden the scope of justice and just spaces within Food Geographies. Papers can be innovative about their research aims/design, demonstrate creative methodologies and/or theoretical questions, or draw upon empirical findings. We especially welcome work that closes the gap between food production and consumption, attends to diversity within diets, advances a de/postcolonial approach to foodways, and engages with emerging practices of gastrodiplomacy and gastrosolidarity in digital and physical spaces (Low, 2021).

Some themes include:

  • Food access, poverty, and everyday food inequalities
  • Labour, migration, and justice in food and agricultural systems
  • Race, gender, caste, and colonial injustices in food geographies
  • Indigenous, local, and alternative food knowledges
  • Environment, sustainability, and climate inequalities in food systems
  • Food, health, and uneven outcomes across places
  • More-than-human relations, care, and food justice
  • Resistance, activism, and food-based social movements
  • Affect, emotion, and embodied geographies of food
  • Gastrodiplomacy, gastrosolidarity, and food as soft power
  • Food, place-making, heritage, and cultural memory
  • Methodological innovations in researching food and inequality

References

Cadieux, K. V., & Slocum, R. (2015). What does it mean to do food justice? Journal of political ecology, 22, 1.

Deschacht, L. (2021). What role do urban food strategies play in fostering justice? (Doctoral dissertation, Ghent University).

schedule_event.title: Research Excellence Framework (REF) 2029 - meet the sub-panel chairs
HYBRID - Big Tech, the State and Emerging Governance Assemblages (1)
schedule_event.description:
Recent scholarship highlights the growing influence of technology corporations in shaping political, social, and state practices. Big Tech platforms and AI technologies are increasingly entangled with state power, authoritarian logics, and new forms of governance, raising fundamental questions about political authority, public accountability, and democratic life (Jarret et al, 2025). The concept of “Big Tech authoritarianism” (Akbari 2025) foregrounds how tech giants not only mediate information but also align with or reproduce authoritarian logics of power, surveillance, and control. Likewise, emerging frameworks of AI authoritarianism (Dekeyser et al. 2025) argue that key features of AI-such as scalability, algorithmic incorporation of difference, and techno-determinist ideologies - actively reshape political governance and authoritarian dynamics across geopolitical contexts.

This session responds to a growing need to critically interrogate the political thrust of technological power and the growing political influence of tech capital at a time when digital platforms and algorithmic systems are increasingly central to shaping state practices, public discourse, citizen engagement, and the possibilities for resistance. Rather than treating “the digital” as a neutral domain, we invite geopolitically informed analyses that situate technology in the relational dynamics of power, space, and governance. This aims to build on existing scholarship theorizing data power (Thatcher & Dalton, 2021), technofascism (McElroy 2025; Coeckelbergh, 2026), or digital colonialism (Kwet, 2019), and aligns with recent calls to rethink governance frameworks in light of AI’s active modulation of political processes and the political synergies between Big Tech and state actors.

This session explores how Big Tech and AI are implicated in reconfiguring the practices, techniques, and imaginaries of governance. We seek contributions that situate these developments within broader socio-spatial processes, attending to power, resistance, and the uneven geographies of digital infrastructure and political authority (Akbari 2026 -upcoming). We invite submissions to this paper session on issues related to:

  • Big Tech, political power, and emerging forms of governance
  • AI, platforms, and the reconfiguration of state authority
  • Uneven geographies of digital power
  • Practices and logics of digital authoritarianism
  • Democratic frictions and political contestation in digital governance
  • Manifestations of Big Tech or AI authoritarianism across distinct spheres of governance

References

Akbari, A. (2026-upcoming). Uneven datafication. Dialogues on Digital Society.

Akbari, A. (2025). Big Tech authoritarianism: Political synergies of an emerging power. Dialogues on Digital Society, 1(3), 317-321.

Coeckelbergh, M. (2026). Technofascism: AI, Big Tech, and the new authoritarianism. AI & SOCIETY, 1-14.

Dekeyser, T., Lynch, C. R., & Maalsen, S. (2025). AI Authoritarianism: Towards an Analytical Framework. Transactions of the Institute of British Geographers, e70048.

Jarrett, K., Kitchin, R., Hiah, J., & Knight Steele, C. (2025). Authoritarianism in the digital age. Dialogues on Digital Society, 1(3), 243-251.

Kwet, M. (2019). Digital colonialism: US empire and the new imperialism in the Global South. Race & class, 60(4), 3-26.

McElroy, E. (2025). Siliconization, technofascism, and their unbecomings. Environment and Planning D: Society and Space, 02637758251401437.

Thatcher, J. E., & Dalton, C. M. (2021). Data power: radical geographies of control and resistance. Pluto Press.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Religious Nationalisms and the spatial politics of the Far Right
schedule_event.description:
‘The far right’, argue Strømmel and Schmiedel, ‘has a theology... and we need to respond to it theologically’ (2020: 146). From the US Christian Nationalist right to Hindutva ideology in India, the combination of religion with extreme forms of nationalism has become increasingly prominent in global politics. Nominally secular parties and political movements of the far right – such as in Turkey, Germany, or the UK – have also sought to enlist and build alliances with ultraconservative elements of religions to strengthen their causes.

These developments are arguably producing both new geopolitical orderings at global and macro-regional scales, alongside (often-violent) changes in the everyday life of places and communities affected by them. Such groups draw on a variety of techniques with distinctly spatial characteristics: well-established geographical imaginaries related to religious war and the clash of civilisations over geographical ‘pivot’ points; palingenetic odes to national rebirth through theocratic piety and purification of national space; policing strict social hierarchies by making claims to a ‘natural’ order and enforcing those claims with crusader-like, militaristic men at its apex.

This session brings into dialogue scholarship on the changing geographies of religion (Kong et al., 2025; Hopkins, 2025), religious geopolitics (Albrecht and Sturm, 2025), anti-fascist geographies (Pries, 2019; Santamarina, 2020; Ince, 2019), and work on the affective politics of authoritarianism and right-wing populism (Luger, 2022; 2024; Anderson and Secor, 2025). The present conjuncture requires critical reflection on how an effective, pluralistic anti‑fascist politics might operate. As far‑right actors mobilise religious affects, practices, and imaginaries, resistance must engage the theological and emotional registers through which these movements acquire meaning and force. At the same time, this moment opens out new ethical and political possibilities - interfaith solidarities, religious activism, and postsecular collaborations across ideological and religious difference (Cloke et al 2019) - both to contest far‑right religious politics and cultivate alternative moral and political futures.

We invite papers that attend to the geographies and spatial politics of religious nationalisms, in any context, and/or diverse efforts to confront, evade, and exceed them.

paper.title: Piety and Rebellion: The Rise of Ethnonationalism in the Ultra-Orthodox Jewish World
Faith, folk and the far right: racist and anti-racist Heathenry and Occultism in Britain
A ‘sacred’ campus: Hinduism, Hindu nationalism and the unmaking of secular universities
Negotiating Constraints and Practising Resistance: Lives of Muslim Girls and Women in Mumbai

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Just Rural Development, Innovative and Partnership Approaches : towards facilitating resilient communities and/or influencing policy and planning (1)
schedule_event.description:
Rural challenges persist in challenging acdemic research and practice based innovation though presenting wicked issues that often require or are best addressed though partnership and or place based local solutions and innovative approaches. This session calls for sharing of such examplar research that are currently involved in bringing about integrative and more just policy for rural areas as sustainably planned places. Often such work draws from an integrated acdemic – practitioner understanding of rural lived experience, policy contexts and stakeholder framimgs of the challenges faced. This may support activities and give voice to or representations of normally challenged and/or marginalised groups e.g. creative practice, community based Carbon descent, inclusive heritage, cultural innovations, digital communication, marginalised faming families, housing development, food hubs, rural training and skills innovations and hyper-local relocalisation and health-based and wellbeing projects.

Papers are especially sought from collaborators involved in seeking just outcomes for rural communities. These may include community development organisations, local pilot case studies and evaluations and also wider academic-practitioner networks. Contributions from underpresented groups are warmly welcomed to enable knowledge sharing.

The first session welcomes international case studies and calls on early career researchers working in this theme through innovative approaches, building local partnerships and / or influencing rural governance and funding.

The second hybrid panel session will invite members to engage in a facilitated lively discussion of innovative case studies, partnerships accressing rural wellbeing and good policy development.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Orbiting and Hovering: Critical Remote Sensing and Volumetric Regimes
schedule_event.description:
The use of remote sensing technologies, including drones and earth observation sensors, is increasingly becoming part of how we map, monitor, track, measure and regulate various environmental and social challenges. Accompanying this growing relevance of remote sensing have been calls for work critiquing the epistemological foundations and the use of remote sensing technologies (Kroth 2025; Lappe et al. 2025). Critical remote sensing (CRS), as such an approach is called, accounts for the politics of sensing practices and sensing infrastructures. CRS attends to how both shape inequalities and injustices and how they remote sensing can be used to counter them. A growing number of scholars appeal to critical remote sensing by questioning the politics and everyday implications of the volumetric regimes that earth observation and other remote sensing techniques produce (Goodchild et al. 2022). Situated at the crossroads of political geography and many other fields including (urban) security studies (Klauser 2021), environmental modelling (Braun 2024), land use management (Paris 2025), biodiversity research (Milner et al. 2023) and many more, critical remote sensing is currently marked by disparate networks scattered across numerous domains.

This RGS-IBG session brings together critical remote sensing scholars to proactively collaborate and network. The session features scholars whose work examines and critically evaluates the use of remote sensing data and infrastructures. Questions that papers link to include, for example:

  • What role does remote sensing play in amplifying and silencing different knowledges?
  • What epistemological foundations is remote sensing built on and what is missing?
  • How does remote sensing contribute to processes of modulating environments?
  • How can we study and make sense of volumetric regimes that link technologies in earth's orbit to local practices of sense making?
  • How does remote sensing play into ongoing crisis narratives and what can we learn from this about sustainability and digital transitions?
  • What would practices of counter-remote sensing look like and how could they be realized?
  • What are the geopolitical dimensions and implications of remote sensing?
  • What role do remote sensing technologies play in creating new scientific ties between public, private and academic partners?


References:

Braun, A. C. (2024). More accurate less meaningful? Progress in Physical Geography: Earth and Environment, 48(3), 343–367. https://doi.org/10.1177/03091333241248055

Goodchild, M. et al. (2022). A White Paper on Locational Information and the Public Interest. American Association of Geographers. https://doi.org/10.14433/2017.0113

Klauser, F. (2021). Police Drones and the Air. Swiss Political Science Review, 27(1), 158–169. https://doi.org/10.1111/spsr.12431

Kroth, L. (2025). Remote sensing and feminist critique. Environment and Planning F, 4(1), 3–19. https://doi.org/10.1177/26349825241283838

Lappe, R., Calovi, M., & Winther, H. (2025). The view from somewhere. Progress in Physical Geography: Earth and Environment, 03091333251409690. https://doi.org/10.1177/03091333251409690

Millner, N. et al. (2023). Exploring the opportunities and risks of aerial monitoring for biodiversity conservation. Global Social Challenges Journal, 2(1), 2–23. https://doi.org/10.1332/TIOK6806

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Organizing (in-)justice through infrastructures
schedule_event.description:
Infrastructures have long been understood as a foundational condition of social, economic, and political life. In geography and across other disciplines, infrastructures are increasingly conceptualized not merely as technical systems or material backbones, but as powerful socio-material assemblages. They organize life spatially while simultaneously being shaped and maintained, but also contested by social interaction and individual practice. Infrastructure research already emphasized the need to jointly consider material and social dimensions of infrastructural productions and operations (Jaglin & Rateau, 2020; Lawhon et al., 2018; Rosen & Gribat, 2025). However, while this insight is widely acknowledged, empirical and methodological approaches have often struggled to specify how exactly materiality, social practices, institutions, networks and discourses interact in concrete settings. As a result, the complex relationship of infrastructures as organizing and organized elements of life with its underlying power-relations, materialities and socialities have not yet been explored sufficiently. This becomes particularly evident in processes of negotiation and conflict over infrastructures, highlighting disbalances of resources such as (political) power, money or time, contributing to the production of (in-)justices.

Newer theoretical and empirical insights that focus on the entanglements of infrastructures and (in-)justices offer approaches to address this still existing gap in research on infrastructures, while also highlighting the complex relationships of structural power imbalances and individual rationalities of actors. These debates draw attention to the role of infrastructures in producing and reproducing spatial inequalities with its violent effects and the structural production of vulnerabilities and inaccessibilities along lines of class, race, gender and (dis-)ability. Further, infrastructures are often part of extractivist projects and material processes which, often irrevocably, alter environments, affecting certain communities more than others and causing “feral” effects in the more-than-human world (Tsing et al. 2024). These processes have, among other concepts, been captured as planetary urbanization (Brenner 2013) or slow violence (Nixon 2013).

The session aims to address these infrastructurally organized inequalities as injustices. This points towards moments of resistance that allow to oppose dominant forms of infrastructures with different and more just practices of infrastructuring (Volmar 2023) organized by practices of care, commoning and intimacy (Berlant 2016; Zechner 2021). Research within this field often challenges not only the dominant notions of infrastructural organization but also reflects on scientific knowledge production as infrastructure itself addressing ethical questions about research, theory and the use of knowledge (Puig de la Bellacasa 2017, Berlant 2016). These debates come with a range of theoretical and methodological contributions for the challenges of research on infrastructures, highlighting the importance of connecting different strands of thought to further develop theory and practice in the field.

Infrastructuring Care: Unequal Social Infrastructures and the Politics of Care Innovation
Between Possibility and Frustration: Perceptions of Vacancies in a Shrinking City
Struggles/injustices around infrastructures for infill development in the Greater Accra Metropolitan Area, Ghana.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Points of departure: just and sustainable agri-food system transitions across places, scales and temporalities
schedule_event.description:
Contemporary industrial food systems have a homogenising effect: extractive and exploitative modes of food production, processing, distribution, retail and consumption dominate globally, driving profound environmental harms alongside socio-cultural flattening, political-economic concentration, and deepening inequalities (Clapp 2020; Patel 2007). In response, alternative food movements have proliferated: from slow food, organic, regenerative and agroecological farming to vegan and locavore diets. Yet these initiatives remain marginal to dominant food regimes, often constrained by political economic conditions or absorbed into market-oriented and technocratic logics that blunt their transformative potential (McGregor et al. 2024; Bless 2025; Hurst and Pant 2025; Pant et al. 2025). Meanwhile, state-led responses to crises produced by industrial food systems are fragmented, depoliticised and reactive: outsourcing governance to markets, while lacking appropriate long-term visions. "Just transition" has emerged as a prominent concept to bridge this fragmentation, invoked across policy, civil society and grassroots movements alike as a framework for navigating the economic and social upheavals of food systems transformation. 

Yet the rhetorical power of just transition has not been matched by clarity about how they are governed, by whom, through what processes, and in whose interests. This session interrogates the plurality of just transitions across five empirical contributions spanning distinct scales, temporalities, sectors and geographies: from gleaning networks in the US and grain networks in New York and Scotland, to civic food initiatives in Australia, dairy sector policy gaps, and epistemic authority in UK agri-food transformation networks. 

The papers expose a common tension between the promise of participatory, place-specific alternatives and the structural constraints — bureacratic, economic, epistemic — that constrain their capacity for transformation. The panel foregrounds place-based and peripheral imaginaries as essential to navigating the impasse between macro-level policy and micro-level practice, interrgoating how just food alternatives operate beyond, and in friction with, dominant institutional norms (Tribaldos & Kortetmäki 2022; Coenen et al. 2012). A key concern throughout is how transitions can occur without undermining rural livelihoods or weakening the social and economic cohesion of communities that depend on existing food insutries, indicating that justice may require more robust relational infrastructures and flexible processes, rather than purely top-down, measurable outcomes. 

 This panel contends that just agri-food transitions cannot be forged through technical or managerial consensus but must be negotiated across the irreducibly plural and inherently messy visions that arise from differently situated places, scales and temporalities. Justice requires not solely more ambitious policy, but honest reckoning with whose perspectives are privileged and what futures are built into, or foreclosed by, prevailing transition imaginaries.

paper.title: Policy gaps for a just transition in the Australian Dairy Sector
ReGraining Ground: Exploring Regional Alternative Grain Networks in Scotland and New York
Who is the “our” in “Our recommendations”? A network analysis of knowledge synthesis in a UK agri-food systems governance initiative
Cultivating Ecologies of Mattering: A Food Systems Transition Design Framework for Cultivating  Care Through Food

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Just Transitions in and beyond China (1)
schedule_event.description:
The global climate and biodiversity crises cannot be addressed without ‘just transitions’ – profound and far-reaching social reconfigurations that place well-being and social justice at their core. While just transition narratives resonate with activists, scholars, and policy makers across geographies, the concept has gained significantly less traction in China. This represents a critical oversight. First, China is the largest emitter of greenhouse gases (IEA, 2023) and leading nation in renewable electricity capacity additions at the global level (IEA, 2025). China’s increasing influence – economically, politically and ecologically – places the nation in a position to significantly shape global transition trajectories. Second, ongoing and planned environmental reform within China is already producing social impacts (e.g., through the phase-out of fossil fuel industries (Gong and Lewis, 2024) and local, uneven effects of environmental conservation (Wang and Lo, 2022), only likely to deepen as transitions unfold. Third, Chinese flows of investment shape patterns of economic development and socio-spatial configurations in locations around the world (Apostolopoulou et al., 2024), with highly uneven and uncertain outcomes. Finally, theoretical frameworks on ‘justice’ (social, environmental, political, and beyond) may require re-conceptualisation in relation to China’s socio-cultural and political context. Mainstream understandings remain shaped by liberal-democratic traditions, which may constrain examinations of transitions in China and China-connected spaces (Wang and Lo, 2021).

Mapping and exploring how just transitions unfold within and beyond China is therefore an urgent research agenda. This panel engages with multiple dimensions of these dynamics, including but not limited to:

  • Social justice and socio-economic impacts of transition-related policies and reforms in China, including uneven outcomes of industrial restructuring, labour displacement, infrastructure investment, and urban-rural inequality. This includes studies on environmental justice dimensions of transitions, such as uneven impacts of environmental policies and programmes of ecological conservation.
  • Just transition outcomes of Chinese overseas investment, such as renewable energy projects, forestry/afforestation, and mining of critical minerals. We especially invite contributions that unpack the complex dynamics at play in global supply chains and the decoupling of impacts across locations of resource extraction and product deployment.
  • Chinese discourse of justice and just transition, including studies that explore contemporary policy narratives and public debates and their approaches to justice, as well as critiques of Anglophone conceptions of these debates. We also welcome work that engages wider and diverse forms of justice and just transition issues across China and China-connected spaces.

paper.title: Whose Justice and Justice for Whom? Reinterpreting Environmental Justice in Contemporary China
Greening the Gentrification Trajectory: State-Led Ecological Transformation and Socio-Spatial Justice in Shanghai
Just Transition in China: Silences and Discourses
Rural Revitalisation as Spatial Transition? Land Reform and Justice in Guangzhou, China

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Sustainable Future Making and its (Uneven) Geographies (1)
schedule_event.description:
As climate change gathers pace with distinctly uneven geographical impacts and a growing number of world regions find themselves confronted with the socio-economic vulnerabilities arising from intersecting planetary crises, calls gather pace to envisage and enact a distinct future that is climate sensitive, environmentally sustainable and socio-ecologically fairer and just.

In this context we have seen increasing attention being given to future oriented frameworks on matters of the environment, climate change and sustainability. This includes compelling or dystopic narratives of future strategically mobilised as desirable or inevitable (Arnall and Hilson, 2023; Kanarp et al.,2025; Peakock and Devine-Wright, 2025) and social action that builds on the symbolic and discursive power of spatial imaginaries to challenge low-carbon futures (Ryder et al., 2025). We also witness the triumph of anticipatory governance and planning (Anderson, 2010; Granjou et al., 2017) and of future oriented tools, including scenarios, deliberative mapping foresight, anticipatory gaming, visioning, experimentation, utilised in imagining sustainable futures (Bellamy et al., 2022; Candy and Dunagan, 2017; Hebinck et al., 2018; Muiderman et al., 2017; Oomen et al., 2021). Research also directs attention to more radical visions of the future, including prefigurative futuring practices (Burnett and Nunes, 2021; Jeffrey and Dyson, 2020) and counter-imaginaries emerging from activism (Matheney et al., 2024), alongside efforts to build abundant futures (Collard et al., 2015) and liberatory futures (Narayanan, 2023). As such, scholarship increasingly points to the emotions and affects involved in constraining, enabling, accelerating or delaying transitions to sustainable futures (Durnova et al., 2025; Martiskainen and Sovacool, 2021).

This sensibility to futures, however, also encounter critical challenges in the form, for instance, of instances of resistance (Caris and Cao, 2024; Hilson and Arnall, 2024) or greenlash/backlash (Alter and Zürn, 2020; Wild et al., 2018); embedded forms of thinking and acting that can captivate otherwise possible futures (Haajer and Oomen, 2025); ongoing and unresolved tensions around exclusion and marginalisation that can spur feelings of futurelessness and stymie sustainable futures (Della Bosca and Gillespie, 2018; Lewis, 2024; Tutton, 2023); (ideological) power structures that perpetuate high carbon futures (Bedford et al., 2023) and geo-political risks, uncertainties, changes that can disrupt desirable futures (++).

It is within this urgent context that we propose this session as a space to think critically and collectively about this future turn on matters of environment, climate change and sustainability. We are interested in sharing and learning from a range of geographical contexts and sectoral domains and invite contributions that conceptually and empirically explore the notion of ‘sustainable future making’, sharpen the focus on its (uneven) geographies, and deepen our understanding of the generative potential of these processes. Possible topics include:

  • Imagining and Enacting Sustainable Futures
  • Futures as Governing Devices, the Governance of Sustainable Futures
  • Contested Sites and Negotiated Trajectories of Sustainable Futures
  • Temporalities of Sustainable Future Making
  • Co-producing sustainable futures: Methodological considerations and challenges
  • (Un)making Sustainable Futures: Power and Inequalities
  • Emotional geographies of sustainable future-making

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Working towards Equality in Indigenous Tourism
schedule_event.description:
Indigenous peoples have struggled to gain recognition of their ownership of their lands and knowledge systems. Despite this, the past 20+ years are showing the growth of independent Indigenous-owned tourism ventures and also an increased interest in Indigenous tourism. Indigenous peoples steward 25% of the earth’s land and most tourism experiences around the world take place on Indigenous territories, yet their role is little acknowledged. Indigenous tourism can help protect sacred places, strengthen sustainable practices, and preserve land, culture, and languages. It can also support Indigenous communities in their pursuit of recognition and self-determination. So how do we work together toward a more inclusive and equitable future?

We have personal and professional experience with Indigenous communities around the globe, including work focused in the social geographies of injustice and inequality, bridging spaces, time, and cultures. While Luis Felipe (Puruhá) explains the overarching conditions for Indigenous peoples across the globe, focused on economic inequities, Sébastien (Wendat) will highlight how the Indigenous-led tourism mark of excellence and authenticity, the Original Original, will help consumers and destinations better interact with Indigenous experiences as well as how global Indigenous tourism data is guiding Indigenous tourism worldwide. Juan Carlos (Aymara) will share examples of the real challenges faced by many Indigenous communities in regions with fewer financial resources, limited infrastructure, and lower levels of tourism development.

The discussion will be moderated by Amy, who works on inclusive cultural initiatives with Indigenous communities, including capacity-building in museums and tourism with both Luis Felipe’s and Juan Carlos’s communities in the Andes.

Moderator:

Amy Van Allen (USA), PhD Geography (Leicester), Fulbright Scholar, senior project manager Smithsonian's National Museum of the American Indian

Panelists:

Sébastien Desnoyers-Picard (Canada), President and Chief Executive Officer, Destination Original Indigenous Tourism (DO-IT), former Vice-President Indigenous Tourism Canada

Luis Felipe Duchicela (USA/Ecuador), CEO ISP Global, former Global Indigenous Program Advisor to the World Bank and USAID

Juan Carlos Patón (Bolivia), legal representative ASOREMUC-QŃ and Qhapaq Ñan Tours

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Environmental geography, invasion ecology and the politics of nature
schedule_event.description:
As human migration is becoming increasingly contested, so too is the ongoing redistribution of plant and animal species across the globe. In an era of accelerating ecological change, the issue of biological invasions has emerged as a pressing—and politically charged—environmental challenge. Non-native species that naturalize and spread disproportionately in new environments can dramatically reshape ecosystems, threaten biodiversity, disrupt economic activities and unsettle long-held assumptions about what belongs where.

Yet invasion science is far from a purely environmental matter. Species movements have been fundamentally shaped by colonial expansion, global trade networks and contemporary regimes of mobility, as well as by Anthropocene's blurring of the boundaries of what we consider ‘natural’. What counts as invasive is often determined not just by ecological impact but by political priorities, economic interests, cultural values and historical contingencies. While the global circulation of species continues to intensify, the number of endemic species extinctions linked to invasive dynamics is also accelerating, raising urgent questions of responsibility, governance and justice.

This complexity demands interdisciplinary inquiry. How do we navigate the tension between ecological urgency, environmental and social justice and the problematic politics of belonging? How do economic demands, social perceptions and regulatory frameworks shape contemporary conservation priorities? As climate change accelerates, how do we distinguish between adaptation, assisted migration and invasion without reproducing exclusionary or technocratic logics?

This session seeks critical, interdisciplinary engagement with the complexities of the invasive species discourse to explore the conceptual entanglements between human and non-human mobility, the cultural work performed by invasion narratives, and the uneven geographies of environmental management and biosecurity. This panel seeks to present an open space of dialogue between empirical research, critical theory and policy analysis, and to contribute to ongoing debates on invasive species as a social and political issue.

paper.title: Nature, restoration and the invasive species dilemma
Do ‘non-native’ species belong in urban nature? Rethinking biodiversity, resilience and designed landscapes
Cultivating resistance with weeds; Figuring new ‘Countrymen’ in Indigenous worlds

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Centering intersectional feminist approaches in mobility planning (2)
schedule_event.description:
The lack of free, safe, and accessible mobility limits access to opportunities and restricts full participation in urban life. In response, transforming transport systems towards more sustainable and just models has become urgent, guided by principles of equity and inclusion that underpin “just mobility transitions” (Oliveira Soares & Glaser, 2025; Schwanen, 2021; Sheller, 2018). Within this process, the gender equality perspective has been crucial in making visible the structural inequalities faced by women and other historically marginalised groups in urban planning and mobility. However, while some cities worldwide have implemented gender-sensitive urban mobility policies, the transformative potential of intersectional feminist planning as an integral part of just mobility transitions is yet to be realised. Gender-focused/gender-mainstreaming policies targeting women have been a key starting point for addressing existing inequalities (Jaimurzina et al., 2017; Sánchez de Madariaga, 2013). However, without an intersectional dimension and integral approach, such policies may have a narrow focus and limited impact, thus benefitting some groups more than others or be at risk of being watered down or even hijacked by populist agendas. Additionally, even within feminist movements and organisations pursuing mobility justice for various groups, intersectional thinking is sometimes not integrated, leading to exclusions, omissions and missed opportunities for demanding a broader change. A more radical revision of mobility planning regimes that intersectional feminist lens could inspire is yet to happen. This revision would challenge assumptions and values underpinning mobility planning beyond just making selected services and places ‘women friendly’. Instead, applying intersectional feminist lens to rethinking mobilities could inspire centering care, accessibility for all and combatting intersectional exclusions of multiple marginalized groups, linked to income, disability, ethnic and religious background, gender identity, sexual orientation, etc. (Castañeda et al., 2024; Jirón et al., 2022; Nikolaeva & Shakthi, 2025).

This challenge resonates with what epistemic justice scholarship in mobility planning has highlighted: the epistemic frameworks that shape urban and transport planning remain largely gender-insensitive (Nikolaeva & Shakthi, 2025), thus making it difficult to effectively integrate this approach. Conceptual frameworks, tools, and indicators that guide planning policies are still anchored in dominant ways of producing data, designing policy, and evaluating mobility systems. These technocratic and androcentric paradigms prioritise quantitative evidence, productive (work-related) mobility, and a conception of the human being as a rational, utility-maximising agent. In doing so, they marginalise relational, collective, and everyday experiences of mobility, care-related and recreational mobilities, and erase the diverse positionalities from which the city is experienced. Additionally, politics and geographies of academic and professional knowledge production and circulation do not offer an even ground for ideas, practices and activist efforts to receive exposure.

Bringing intersectional feminist and epistemic justice perspectives together, the session interrogates dominant gender-insensitive planning paradigms – quantitative, productivity-centred, androcentric, and oriented around the rational, utility‑maximising individual – that systematically marginalise care, relational, recreational and collective everyday (im)mobilities. It asks how knowledge hierarchies in academia, policy, and professional practice shape what counts as evidence, which actors and geographies are heard, and whose experiences of the city remain invisible.

schedule_event.title: ONLINE - Restoration of Urban Nature as Living Labs: Advancing Mental Health, Heat Resilience, and Equity
schedule_event.description:
As climate change intensifies, urban heatwaves pose a critical threat to public health and ecological stability, disproportionately impacting low-income and marginalised communities. These environmental inequalities intersect with existing social vulnerabilities, creating compound disadvantages that demand urgent and just intervention. This session argues for a paradigm shift in urban climate adaptation: from top-down infrastructure projects to participatory, place-based experimentation through the restoration of vital blue-green spaces. 

We invite contributions that critically examine how the restoration and co-management of urban wetlands, green spaces, rivers, and forests can serve as equitable interventions to mitigate heatwaves and enhance mental health.

This session positions these restored ecosystems not merely as final products, but as dynamic Urban Living Labs. This framework allows researchers, practitioners, and residents to collaboratively experiment with and monitor Nature-based Solutions (NbS), iteratively testing restoration methods and governance models in real-world contexts before scaling them to other sites. By focusing on spaces accessible to the urban poor, we move beyond the concept of green gentrification toward a model of 'just green enough'. 

We seek to explore how these co-produced labs can foster ecological literacy, social cohesion, and psychological well-being, while simultaneously cooling urban heat islands and improving quality of life.

Furthermore, nature‑positive engineering plays a vital role by ensuring that infrastructure not only minimises harm but also actively restores and strengthens local ecosystems. Approaches such as green infrastructure, biodiversity‑sensitive design, water‑sensitive urban planning, and climate‑resilient materials help rebuild ecological health while centring equity. Embedding these principles enables cities to improve air quality, manage stormwater, reduce urban heat, and enhance community wellbeing—without displacing vulnerable residents or triggering green gentrification. 

We welcome work that investigates the cross‑sector partnerships, policy frameworks, and long‑term stewardship models required to achieve regenerative, inclusive urban development that benefits those most affected by climate risks.

Central to our inquiry is equity: 

Who benefits from urban greening?

How can we ensure that restored rivers, wetlands, or forests become resources for thermal comfort and mental restoration for all, rather than amenities that displace vulnerable populations? 

We will explore the tensions, successes, and failures of using these natural spaces as living labs to democratise climate action. By bringing together geographers, urban ecologists, planners, and community partners, this session aims to forge a new research agenda that places justice at the heart of nature-based experimentation, ultimately contributing to the development of climate-resilient and mentally healthy urban environments for everyone.

schedule_event.title: ONLINE - Queer Arab Geographies – Navigating Inequalities Towards Just Spaces
schedule_event.description:
This proposed session, “Queer Arab Geographies: Navigating Inequalities Towards Just Spaces,” seeks to explore the multifaceted intersections of queerness, Arab identities, and spatial dynamics within the broader context of geographical inequalities. Aligning with the 2026 RGS-IBG Annual International Conference theme, “Geographies of Inequalities: Towards Just Places,” the session examines how queer Arabs negotiate, resist, and reimagine spaces marked by exclusion, peril, and privilege. From urban nocturnes in Gulf cities to diasporic migrations and historical temporalities, the session aims to highlight the ways in which queer experiences in Arab contexts challenge heteronormative structures, fostering pathways to more equitable and just geographies.

Queer Arab geographies remain under-researched in mainstream geographical scholarship, often overshadowed by Western-centric queer theories or Orientalist framings that exoticize or pathologize non-normative sexualities in the Middle East and North Africa (MENA). Drawing on works like Joseph Massad’s Desiring Arabs (2007) and its critiques, this session interrogates how colonial legacies, state surveillance, and global Islamophobia shape queer spatial practices. For instance, research on expatriate gay men in Dubai reveals “nocturnal performative geographies” where privilege intersects with vulnerability—spaces of temporary inclusion amid legal precarity, as expatriates leverage Western passports to navigate underground nightlife while local queer Arabs face heightened risks (Centner, 2021). Such dynamics underscore inequalities not just in access to space but in the very legibility of queer existence.

The session will also address migration and diaspora as key sites of queer Arab spatial politics. Queer Middle Eastern refugees often encounter “ambivalent geographies” in host countries, where asylum narratives demand performative authenticity—recounting trauma to fit Western notions of the “true self”—while erasing complex home ties (Jabr, 2021). In diasporic contexts, organizations like Al-Fatiha demonstrate coalitional possibilities, bridging queer Muslim identities across borders to counter Islamophobia and advocate for asylum without reinforcing imperial agendas (Rouhani, 2009). These migrations highlight how queer Arabs “undo belonging” to forge hybrid spaces, resisting binary notions of home/exile and contributing to just places through reimagined communities.

I invite contributions across formats: empirical papers on queer urbanism in cities like Beirut or Amman; theoretical explorations of queer theory’s integration into Arab geographies (e.g., clitocentrism and unfulfilled needs in queer studies); or creative interventions on digital spaces where queer Arabs build solidarity. The session aims for two timeslots (1 hour 40 minutes each), potentially online to include voices from restricted regions, ensuring inclusive engagement through live Q&A, pre-recorded inputs, and post-session sharing of materials. By centering queer Arab perspectives, this session advances towards just places, amplifying marginalized voices in geographical discourse.
